greek_head: Αὐτός ὦ Φαίδων παρεγένου Σωκράτει ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ᾗ τὸ φάρμακον ἔπιεν ἐν τῷ δεσμωτηρίῳ ἢ ἄλλου του
greek_tokens: autos eimi faidwn paragignomai swkratew ekeinos o hmeros h o farmakon efihmi en o desmwthrion h allos tis akouw autos eimi exekraths tis oun dh eimi ostis eipon o anhr pro o qanatos kai pws teleutaw hdews gar an egw akouw kai gar oute o poliths fleiasios oudeis panu tis epixwriazw o nun aqhnaze oute tis cenos afikneomai xronos suxnos
english_head: Were you with Socrates yourself, Phaedo, on the day when he drank the poison in prison, or did you hear about it from someone else? I was there myself, Echecrates. Then what did he say before his death? and how did he die? I should like to hear, for nowadays none of the Phliasians go to Athens at all, and no stranger has come from there for a long time,

greek_head: ἐκεῖθεν ὅστις ἂν ἡμῖν σαφές τι ἀγγεῖλαι οἷός τ' ἦν περὶ τούτων πλήν γε δὴ ὅτι φάρμακον πιὼν ἀποθάνοι τῶν δὲ
greek_tokens: ekeiqen ostis an egw safhs tis aggellw oios su eimi peri outos plhn ge dh ostis farmakon pinw apoqnhskw o de allos oudeis exw frazw
english_head: who could tell us anything definite about this matter, except that he drank poison and died, so we could learn no further details.

greek_head: Οὐδὲ τὰ περὶ τῆς δίκης ἄρα ἐπύθεσθε ὃν τρόπον ἐγένετο
greek_tokens: oude o peri o dikh ara puqw os tropos gignomai nai outos men egw aggellw tis kai qaumazw ge ostis pala gignomai autos polus usteron fainw apoqnhskw tis oun eimi outos eimi faidwn tuxh tis autos eimi exekraths sumbainw tugxanw gar o proteraios o dikh o prumna stefw o ploion o eis dhlos aqhnaios pempw outos de dh tis eimi outos eimi o ploion os fhmi aqhnaios en os qhseus pote eis krhth o dis epta ekeinos oixomai
english_head: Did you not even hear about the trial and how it was conducted? Yes, some one told us about that, and we wondered that although it took place a long time ago, he was put to death much later. Now why was that, Phaedo? It was a matter of chance, Echecrates. It happened that the stern of the ship which the Athenians send to Delos was crowned on the day before the trial. What ship is this? This is the ship, as the Athenians say, in which Theseus once went to Crete with the fourteen

greek_head: ἄγων καὶ ἔσωσέ τε καὶ αὐτὸς ἐσώθη τῷ οὖν Ἀπόλλωνι ηὔξαντο ὡς λέγεται τότε εἰ σωθεῖεν ἑκάστου ἔτους θεωρίαν
greek_tokens: agw kai eiswqew te kai autos eiswqew o oun apollwn euxomai ws legw tote ei swzw ekastos etos qewria apagw eis dhlos os dh aei kai nun eti ek ekeinos kata eniautos o qeaw pempw epeidan oun arxw o qewria nomos eimi autos en o xronos outos kaqareuw o polis kai dhmosios mhdeis apokteinw prin an eis dhlos te afikneomai o ploion kai palin deuro outos de eniote en polus xronos gignomai otan tugxanw anemos apolambanw
english_head: youths and maidens, and saved them and himself. Now the Athenians made a vow to Apollo, as the story goes, that if they were saved they would send a mission every year to Delos. And from that time even to the present day they send it annually in honor of the god. Now it is their law that after the mission begins the city must be pure and no one may be publicly executed until the ship has gone to Delos and back; and sometimes, when contrary winds

greek_head: αὐτούς ἀρχὴ δ' ἐστὶ τῆς θεωρίας ἐπειδὰν ὁ ἱερεὺς τοῦ Ἀπόλλωνος στέψῃ τὴν πρύμναν τοῦ πλοίου
greek_tokens: autos arxh de eimi o qewria epeidan o iereus o apollwn stefw o prumna o ploion outos de tugxanw wsper legos o proteraios o dikh gignomai dia outos kai polus xronos gignomai o swkratew en o desmwthrion o metacu o dikh te kai o qanatos tis de dh o peri autos o qanatos eimi faidwn tis eimi o legw kai prassw kai tinw e paragignomai o epithdeios o andris h ou eaw e arxw pareimi alla erhmos teleutaw filos
english_head: detain it, this takes a long time. The beginning of the mission is when the priest of Apollo crowns the stern of the ship; and this took place, as I say, on the day before the trial. For that reason Socrates passed a long time in prison between his trial and his death. What took place at his death, Phaedo? What was said and done? And which of his friends were with him? Or did the authorities forbid them to be present, so that he died without his friends?

greek_head: Οὐδαμῶς ἀλλὰ παρῆσάν τινες καὶ πολλοί γε Ταῦτα δὴ πάντα προθυμήθητι ὡς σαφέστατα ἡμῖν
greek_tokens: oudamos alla pareimi tis kai polus ge outos dh pas proqumeomai ws safhs egw apaggellw ei mh tis su asxolia tugxanw eimi alla sxolazw ge kai peiraw su dihgeomai kai gar o mimnhskw swkraths kai autos legw kai allos akouw egw aei pas hdus alla meis eimi faidwn kai o akouw ge toioutos eteros exis alla peiraw ws an dunamai akribhs diecerxomai pas
english_head: Not at all. Some were there, in fact, a good many. Be so good as to tell us as exactly as you can about all these things, if you are not too busy. I am not busy and I will try to tell you. It is always my greatest pleasure to be reminded of Socrates whether by speaking of him myself or by listening to someone else. Well, Phaedo, you will have hearers who feel as you do; so try to tell us everything as accurately as you can.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν ἔγωγε θαυμάσια ἔπαθον παραγενόμενος οὔτε γὰρ ὡς θανάτῳ παρόντα με ἀνδρὸς ἐπιτηδείου ἔλεος
greek_tokens: kai meis egw qaumasios pasxw paragignomai oute gar ws qanatos pareimi egw anhr epithdeios eleos eiseimi eudaimwn gar egw anhr fainw eimi exekraths kai o tropos kai o logos ws adehs kai gennaios teleutaw wste egw ekeinos paristhmi mhde eis adhs eimi aneu qeia moira eimi alla kai ekeise afikneomai eu prassw
english_head: For my part, I had strange emotions when I was there. For I was not filled with pity as I might naturally be when present at the death of a friend; since he seemed to me to be happy, both in his bearing and his words, he was meeting death so fearlessly and nobly. And so I thought that even in going to the abode of the dead he was not going without the protection of the gods, and that when he arrived there

greek_head: εἴπερ τις πώποτε καὶ ἄλλος διὰ δὴ ταῦτα οὐδὲν πάνυ μοι ἐλεινὸν εἰσῄει ὡς εἰκὸς ἂν δόξειεν εἶναι παρόντι πένθει
greek_tokens: eiper tis pwpote kai allos dia dh outos oudeis panu egw eleeinos eiseimi ws eoika an dokew eimi pareimi penqos oute au hdona ws en filosofia egw eimi wsper eqw kai gar e logos toioutos tis eimi alla atexnos atopos tis egw paqos pareimi kai tis ahqhs krasis apo te o hdona sugkerannumi omos kai apo o luph enqumeomai ostis autika ekeinos mellw teleutaw kai pas e pareimi sxedon tis outws diakeimai tote men gelaw eniote de dakruw eis de egw kai diaferontws apollodwros
english_head: it would be well with him, if it ever was well with anyone. And for this reason I was not at all filled with pity, as might seem natural when I was present at a scene of mourning; nor on the other hand did I feel pleasure because we were occupied with philosophy, as was our custom—and our talk was of philosophy;—but a very strange feeling came over me, an unaccustomed mixture of pleasure and of pain together, when I thought that Socrates was presently to die. And all of us who were there were in much the same condition, sometimes laughing and sometimes weeping; especially one of us, Apollodorus; you know him

greek_head: οἶσθα γάρ που τὸν ἄνδρα καὶ τὸν τρόπον αὐτοῦ Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: oida gar pou o anhr kai o tropos autos pws gar ou ekeinos te toinun pantapasi outws exw kai autos egw tarassw kai e allos tugxanw de eimi faidwn tinw paragignomai outos te dh o apollodwros o epixwrios pareimi kai kritoboulos kai o pathr autos kai eti ermogenhs kai epigenhs kai aisxinhs kai antisqenhs eimi de kai kthsippos o paianieus kai menecenos kai allos tis o epixwrios platwn de oimh asqenew cenos de tis pareimi
english_head: and his character. To be sure I do. He was quite unrestrained, and I was much agitated myself, as were the others. Who were these, Phaedo? Of native Athenians there was this Apollodorus, and Critobulus and his father, and Hermogenes and Epiganes and Aeschines and Antisthenes; and Ctesippus the Paeanian was there too, and Menexenus and some other Athenians. But Plato, I think, was ill.

greek_head: Ναί Σιμμίας τέ γε ὁ Θηβαῖος καὶ Κέβης καὶ Φαιδώνδης καὶ Μεγαρόθεν Εὐκλείδης τε καὶ Τερψίων
greek_tokens: nai simmias su ge o qhbaios kai kebhs kai faidwndhs kai megaroqen eukleidhs te kai terpw tis de aristippos kai kleombrotos paragignomai ou dhta en aiginh gar legw eimi allos de tis pareimi sxedon tis oimh outos paragignomai tis oun dh tinw fhmi eimi e logos egw su ek arxh pas peiraw dihgeomai
english_head: Were any foreigners there? Yes, Simmias of Thebes and Cebes and Phaedonides, and from Megara Euclides and Terpsion. What? Were Aristippus and Cleombrotus there? No. They were said to be in Aegina. Was anyone else there? I think these were about all. Well then, what was the conversation? I will try to tell you everything from the beginning. On the previous days

greek_head: ἀεὶ γὰρ δὴ καὶ τὰς πρόσθεν ἡμέρας εἰώθεμεν φοιτᾶν καὶ ἐγὼ καὶ οἱ ἄλλοι παρὰ τὸν Σωκράτη συλλεγόμενοι
greek_tokens: aei gar dh kai o prosqen hmeros eqw foiths kai egw kai e allos para o swkratew sullegw ewqen eis o dikasthrion en os kai o dikh gignomai pimplhmi gar eimi o desmwthrion perimenw oun ekastote ews anoignumi o desmwthrion diatribw meta allhlwn anoignumi gar ou prwi epei de anoignumi eiseimi para o swkratew kai o polus dihmereuw meta autos kai dh kai tote prwi sunlegw o gar proteraios hmeros
english_head: I and the others had always been in the habit of visiting Socrates. We used to meet at daybreak in the court where the trial took place, for it was near the prison; and every day we used to wait about, talking with each other, until the prison was opened, for it was not opened early; and when it was opened, we went in to Socrates and passed most of the day with him. On that day we came together earlier; for the day before,

greek_head: ἐπειδὴ ἐξήλθομεν ἐκ τοῦ δεσμωτηρίου ἑσπέρας ἐπυθόμεθα ὅτι τὸ πλοῖον ἐκ Δήλου ἀφιγμένον εἴη παρηγγείλαμεν οὖν
greek_tokens: epei ecerxomai ek o desmwthrion espera puqw ostis o ploion ek boulomai afizw eaw paraggellw oun allhlwn hkw ws prwi eis o eqw kai hkw kai egw ecerxomai o qurwros os eqw upakouw eipon perimenw kai mh proteros pareimi ews an autos keleusis luw gar fhmi e endeka swkratew kai paraggellw opws an ode o hmeros teleutaw ou polus de oun xronos epwxato hkw kai keleuw egw eiseimi eiseimi oun
english_head: when we left the prison in the evening we heard that the ship had arrived from Delos. So we agreed to come to the usual place as early in the morning as possible. And we came, and the jailer who usually answered the door came out and told us to wait and not go in until he told us. For, he said, the eleven are releasing Socrates from his fetters and giving directions how he is to die today. So after a little delay he came and

greek_head: κατελαμβάνομεν τὸν μὲν Σωκράτη ἄρτι λελυμένον τὴν δὲ Ξανθίππην γιγνώσκεις γάρ ἔχουσάν τε τὸ παιδίον αὐτοῦ
greek_tokens: katalambanw o men swkratew arti luw o de canqipph gignwskw gar exw te o paidion autos kai parakaqhmai ws oun eidon egw o canqipph aneufhmew te kai toioutos atta eipon oios dh eqw o gunh ostis eimi swkraths ustatos dh su proserew nun e epithdeios kai su outos kai o swkratew blepw eis o kritwna eimi kritwn fhmi apagw tis autos oikade kai ekeinos men apagw tis o o kritwnos boaw
english_head: told us to go in. We went in then and found Socrates just released from his fetters and Xanthippe—you know her—with his little son in her arms, sitting beside him. Now when Xanthippe saw us, she cried out and said the kind of thing that women always do say: Oh Socrates, this is the last time now that your friends will speak to you or you to them. And Socrates glanced at Crito and said, Crito, let somebody take her home. And some of Crito’s people took her away wailing

greek_head: τε καὶ κοπτομένην ὁ δὲ Σωκράτης ἀνακαθιζόμενος εἰς τὴν κλίνην συνέκαμψέ τε τὸ σκέλος καὶ ἐξέτριψε τῇ χειρί καὶ
greek_tokens: te kai koptw o de swkratew anakaqizw eis o klinh sugkamptw te o skelos kai ektribw o xeir kai tribos ama ws atopos fhmi eimi anhr eoika tis eimi outos o kalew e anqrwpos hdus ws qaumasios fuw pros o dokew enantion eimi o luphros o ama men autos mh eqelw paragignomai o anqrwpos ean de tis diwkw o eteros kai lambanw sxedon tis anagkazw aei lambanw kai o eteros wsper ek eis korufh aptw
english_head: and beating her breast. But Socrates sat up on his couch and bent his leg and rubbed it with his hand, and while he was rubbing it, he said, What a strange thing, my friends, that seems to be which men call pleasure! How wonderfully it is related to that which seems to be its opposite, pain, in that they will not both come to a man at the same time, and yet if he pursues the one and captures it he is generally obliged to take the other also, as if the two were joined together in one head. And I think,

greek_head: δύ' ὄντε καί μοι δοκεῖ ἔφη εἰ ἐνενόησεν αὐτὰ Αἴσωπος μῦθον ἂν συνθεῖναι ὡς ὁ θεὸς βουλόμενος αὐτὰ διαλλάξαι
greek_tokens: duh eimi kai egw dokew fhmi ei ennoew autos aiswpos muqos an suntiqhmi ws o qeos boulomai autos diallassw polemew epei ou dunamai sunaptw eis tautos autos o korufas kai dia outos os an o eteros paragignomai epakolouqew usteron kai o eteros wsper oun kai autos egw eoika epei upo o desmew eimi en o skelos o algeinos hkw dh fainw epakolouqew o hdus o oun kebhs upolambanw nh o dios eimi swkraths fhmi eu ge poiew anamimnhskw egw peri gar su o
english_head: he said, if Aesop had thought of them, he would have made a fable telling how they were at war and god wished to reconcile them, and when he could not do that, he fastened their heads together, and for that reason, when one of them comes to anyone, the other follows after. Just so it seems that in my case, after pain was in my leg on account of the fetter, pleasure appears to have come following after. Here Cebes interrupted and said, By Zeus, Socrates, I am glad you reminded me.

greek_head: ποιημάτων ὧν πεποίηκας ἐντείνας τοὺς τοῦ Αἰσώπου λόγους καὶ τὸ εἰς τὸν Ἀπόλλω προοίμιον καὶ ἄλλοι τινές με ἤδη
greek_tokens: poihma os poiew enteinw o o aiswpos logos kai o eis o apolouw prooimion kai allos tis egw hdh arow atar kai euhnos prwios ostis pote dianoeomai epei deuro erxomai poiew autos proteros oudeis pwpote poiew ei oun tis su melos o exw egw euhnos apokrinw otan egw auqis erwtaw eu oida gar ostis eromai eipon tis xrh legw legos toinun fhmi autos eimi kebhs alhqhs ostis ou ekeinos boulomai oude o poihma autos antitexnos
english_head: Several others have asked about the poems you have composed, the metrical versions of Aesop’s fables and the hymn to Apollo, and Evenus asked me the day before yesterday why you never wrote any poetry before, composed these verses after you came to prison. Now, if you care that I should be able to answer Evenus when he asks me again—and I know he will ask me—tell me what to say. Then tell him, Cebes, said he, the truth, that I composed these verses not because I wished to rival him or his poems,

greek_head: εἶναι ἐποίησα ταῦτα ᾔδη γὰρ ὡς οὐ ῥᾴδιον εἴη ἀλλ' ἐνυπνίων τινῶν ἀποπειρώμενος τί λέγοι καὶ ἀφοσιούμενος
greek_tokens: eimi poiew outos oida gar ws ou radios eaw alla enupnion tis apopeiraomai tis legos kai afosiow ei ara pollakis outos o mousikh egw epitassw poiew eimi gar dh atta toiosde pollakis egw foiths o autos enupnion en o parerxomai bios allote en allh oraw fainw o autos de legos eimi swkraths fhmi mousikh poiew kai ergazomai kai egw en ge o prosqen xronos osper prassw outos upolambanw autos egw parakeleuomai te
english_head: for I knew that would not be easy, but because I wished to test the meaning of certain dreams, and to make sure that I was neglecting no duty in case their repeated commands meant that I must cultivate the Muses in this way. They were something like this. The same dream came to me often in my past life, sometimes in one form and sometimes in another, but always saying the same thing: Socrates, it said, make music and work at it. And I formerly thought it was urging and encouraging me

greek_head: καὶ ἐπικελεύειν ὥσπερ οἱ τοῖς θέουσι διακελευόμενοι καὶ ἐμοὶ οὕτω τὸ ἐνύπνιον ὅπερ ἔπραττον τοῦτο ἐπικελεύειν
greek_tokens: kai epikeleuw wsper e o qew diakeleuomai kai egw outws o enupnion osper prassw outos epikeleuw mousikh poiew ws filosofia men eimi megas mousikh egw de outos prassw nun de epei o te dikh gignomai kai o o qeaomai eorth diakwluw egw apoqnhskw dokew xrh ei ara pollakis egw prostassw o enupnion outos o dhmwdhs mousikh poiew mh apeiqew autos alla poiew asfalhs gar eimi mh apeimi prin afosiow
english_head: to do what I was doing already and that just as people encourage runners by cheering, so the dream was encouraging me to do what I was doing, that is, to make music, because philosophy was the greatest kind of music and I was working at that. But now, after the trial and while the festival of the god delayed my execution, I thought, in case the repeated dream really meant to tell me to make this which is ordinarily called music, I ought to do so and not to disobey. For I thought it was safer not to go hence

greek_head: ποιήσαντα ποιήματα καὶ πιθόμενον τῷ ἐνυπνίῳ οὕτω δὴ πρῶτον μὲν εἰς τὸν θεὸν ἐποίησα οὗ ἦν ἡ παροῦσα
greek_tokens: poiew poihma kai peiqw o enupnion outws dh proteros men eis o qeos poiew e eimi o pareimi qusia meta de o qeos ennoew ostis o poihths dew eiper mellw poihths eimi poiew muqos alla ou logos kai autos ou eimi muqologikos dia outos dh os proxeiros exw muqos kai epistamai o aiswpos outos poiew os proteros entugxanw outos oun eimi kebhs euhnos frazw kai rwnnumi kai an swfronew egw diwkw ws taxistos
english_head: before making sure that I had done what I ought, by obeying the dream and composing verses. So first I composed a hymn to the god whose festival it was; and after the god, considering that a poet, if he is really to be a poet, must compose myths and not speeches, since I was not a maker of myths, I took the myths of Aesop, which I had at hand and knew, and turned into verse the first I came upon. So tell Evenus that, Cebes, and bid him farewell, and tell him, if he is wise, to come after me as quickly as he can.

greek_head: ἄπειμι δέ ὡς ἔοικε τήμερον κελεύουσι γὰρ Ἀθηναῖοι Καὶ ὁ Σιμμίας Οἷον παρακελεύῃ ἔφη τοῦτο ὦ Σώκρατες
greek_tokens: apeimi de ws eoika shmeron keleuw gar aqhnaios kai o simmias oios parakeleuomai fhmi outos eimi swkraths euhnos polus gar hdh entugxanw o andris sxedon oun ek os egw aisqanomai oude opwsoun su ekwn eimi pasxw tis de eimi de os ou filosofos euhnos egw dokew fhmi o simmias eqelw toinun kai euhnos kai pas ostis acios outos o pragma meteimi ou mentoi isos biaw eautou ou gar fhmi qemitos eimi kai ama legos outos kaqhkw
english_head: I, it seems, am going today; for that is the order of the Athenians. And Simmias said, What a message that is, Socrates, for Evenus! I have met him often, and from what I have seen of him, I should say that he will not take your advice in the least if he can help it. Why so? said he. Is not Evenus a philosopher? I think so, said Simmias. Then Evenus will take my advice, and so will every man who has any worthy interest in philosophy. Perhaps, however, he will not take his own life, for they say that is not permitted.

greek_head: τὰ σκέλη ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν καὶ καθεζόμενος οὕτως ἤδη τὰ λοιπὰ διελέγετο
greek_tokens: o skelos epi o gaia kai kataezomai outws hdh o loipas dialegw airw oun autos o kebhs pws outos legw eimi swkraths o mh qemitos eimi eautou biazw eqelw de an o apoqnhskw o filosofos epomai tis de eimi kebhs ou akouw su te kai simmias peri o toioutos filolaos suggignomai oudeis ge safhs eimi swkraths alla meis kai egw ek akoh peri autos legos o men oun tugxanw akouw fqonos oudeis legw kai gar isos
english_head: And as he spoke he put his feet down on the ground and remained sitting in this way through the rest of the conversation. Then Cebes asked him: What do you mean by this, Socrates, that it is not permitted to take one’s life, but that the philosopher would desire to follow after the dying? How is this, Cebes? Have you and Simmias, who are pupils of Philolaus, not heard about such things? Nothing definite, Socrates. I myself speak of them only from hearsay; but I have no objection to telling what I have heard. And indeed it is perhaps especially fitting,

greek_head: καὶ μάλιστα πρέπει μέλλοντα ἐκεῖσε ἀποδημεῖν διασκοπεῖν τε καὶ μυθολογεῖν περὶ τῆς ἀποδημίας τῆς ἐκεῖ ποίαν τινὰ
greek_tokens: kai malista prepw mellw ekeise apodhmew diaskopew te kai muqologew peri o apodhmia o ekei poa tis autos oiomai eimi tis gar an tis kai poiew allos en o mexri hlios dusmh xronos kata tis dh oun pote ou fhmi qemitos eimi autos eautou apokteinw eimi swkraths hdh gar egw osper nundi su arow kai filolaos akouw oste para egw diadiaitaw hdh de kai allos tis ws ou dew outos poiew safhs de peri autos oudeis pwpote oudeis akouw
english_head: as I am going to the other world, to tell stories about the life there and consider what we think about it; for what else could one do in the time between now and sunset? Why in the world do they say that it is not permitted to kill oneself, Socrates? I heard Philolaus, when he was living in our city, say the same thing you just said, and I have heard it from others, too, that one must not do this; but I never heard anyone say anything definite about it.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ προθυμεῖσθαι χρή ἔφη τάχα γὰρ ἂν καὶ ἀκούσαις ἴσως μέντοι θαυμαστόν σοι φανεῖται εἰ τοῦτο μόνον
greek_tokens: alla proqumeomai xrh fhmi taxa gar an kai aekwn isos mentoi qaumastos su fainw ei outos monos o allos apas aploos eimi kai oudepote tugxanw o anqrwpos wsper kai allos eimi oste kai os beltiwn eimi qnhskw h zaw os de beltiwn qnhskw qaumastos isos su fainw ei outos o anqrwpos mh osios autos eautou eu poiew alla allos deomai perimenw euergeths kai o kebhs hrema epigelaw oida zeus fhmi o eautou fwnew eipon
english_head: You must have courage, said he, and perhaps you might hear something. But perhaps it will seem strange to you that this alone of all laws is without exception, and it never happens to mankind, as in other matters, that only at some times and for some persons it is better to die than to live; and it will perhaps seem strange to you that these human beings for whom it is better to die cannot without impiety do good to themselves, but must wait for some other benefactor. And Cebes, smiling gently, said, God knows it does, speaking in his own dialect. It would seem unreasonable, if put in this way, said Socrates,

greek_head: Καὶ γὰρ ἂν δόξειεν ἔφη ὁ Σωκράτης οὕτω γ' εἶναι ἄλογον οὐ μέντοι ἀλλ' ἴσως γ' ἔχει τινὰ λόγον ὁ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: kai gar an dokew fhmi o swkratew outws ge eimi alogos ou mentoi alla isos ge exis tis logos o men oun en aporrhtos legw peri autos logos ws en tis froura eimi e anqrwpos kai ou deomai dh eautou ek outos luw oude apodidraskw megas su tis egw fainw kai ou radios dieidon ou mentoi alla ode ge egw dokew eimi kebhs eu legw o qeos eimi egw o epimeleomai kai egw o anqrwpos eis o kthma o qeaw eimi h sos ou dokew outws egw fhmi o kebhs
english_head: but perhaps there is some reason in it. Now the doctrine that is taught in secret about this matter, that we men are in a kind of prison and must not set ourselves free or run away, seems to me to be weighty and not easy to understand. But this at least, Cebes, I do believe is sound, that the gods are our guardians and that we men are one of the chattels of the gods. Do you not believe this? Yes, said Cebes,

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἦ δ' ὅς καὶ σὺ ἂν τῶν σαυτοῦ κτημάτων εἴ τι αὐτὸ ἑαυτὸ ἀποκτεινύοι μὴ σημήναντός σου ὅτι βούλει
greek_tokens: oukoun eimi de os kai su an o sautou kthma ei tis autos eautou apokteinw mh shmainw su ostis boulomai autos qnhskw xalepainw an autos kai ei tis exw timwria timwrew an panu ge fhmi isos toinun outos ou alogos mh proteros eautou apokteinw dew prin anagkh tis qeos epipemyis wsper kai o nun egw pareimi alla eoika fhmi o kebhs outos ge fainw o mentoi nundi legw o o filosofos radios an eqelw
english_head: I do. Well then, said he, if one of your chattels should kill itself when you had not indicated that you wished it to die, would you be angry with it and punish it if you could? Certainly, he replied. Then perhaps from this point of view it is not unreasonable to say that a man must not kill himself until god sends some necessity upon him, such as has now come upon me. That, said Cebes, seems sensible. But what you said just now, Socrates, that philosophers ought to be ready and willing to die, that seems

greek_head: ἀποθνῄσκειν ἔοικεν τοῦτο ὦ Σώκρατες ἀτόπῳ εἴπερ ὃ νυνδὴ ἐλέγομεν εὐλόγως ἔχει τὸ θεόν τε εἶναι τὸν ἐπιμελούμενον
greek_tokens: apoqnhskw eoika outos eimi swkraths atopos eiper o nundi legw eulogos exis o qeos te eimi o epimeleomai egw kai egw ekeinos kthma eimi o gar mh aganaktew o fronimos ek outos o qerapeia apeimi en h epistatew autos e aristos eimi o eimi epistath qeos ou exis logos ou gar pou autos ge eautou oiomai ameinwn epimeleomai eleuqeros gignomai alla anohtos men anqrwpos taxa an oiomai outos feukteos
english_head: strange if we were right just now in saying that god is our guardian and we are his possessions. For it is not reasonable that the wisest men should not be troubled when they leave that service in which the gods, who are the best overseers in the world, are watching over them. A wise man certainly does not think that when he is free he can take better care of himself than they do. A foolish man might perhaps think so, that he ought to run away from his master,

greek_head: εἶναι ἀπὸ τοῦ δεσπότου καὶ οὐκ ἂν λογίζοιτο ὅτι οὐ δεῖ ἀπό γε τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ φεύγειν ἀλλ' ὅτι μάλιστα παραμένειν διὸ
greek_tokens: eimi apo o despoths kai ou an logizomai ostis ou deomai apo ge o agaqos feugw alla ostis malista paramenw dio alogistos an feugw o de new exw epiqumew pou an aei eimi para o eautou beltiwn kaitoi outws eimi swkraths enantion eimi eoika h o nundi legw o men gar fronimos aganaktew apoqnhskw prepw o de afrwn xairw aekwn oun o swkratew hdomai su egw dokew o o
english_head: and he would not consider that he must not run away from a good master, but ought to stay with him as long as possible; and so he might thoughtlessly run away; but a man of sense would wish to be always with one who is better than himself. And yet, Socrates, if we look at it in this way, the contrary of what we just said seems natural; for the wise ought to be troubled at dying and the foolish to rejoice. When Socrates heard this

greek_head: Κέβητος πραγματείᾳ καὶ ἐπιβλέψας εἰς ἡμᾶς Ἀεί τοι ἔφη ὁ Κέβης λόγους τινὰς ἀνερευνᾷ καὶ οὐ πάνυ εὐθέως
greek_tokens: kebhtos pragmateia kai epiblepw eis egw aei su fhmi o kebhs logos tis anereunaw kai ou panu euqews eqelw peiqw ostis an tis eipon kai o simmias alla meis fhmi eimi swkraths nun ge egw dokew tis kai autos legw kebhs tis gar an boulomai anhr sofos ws alhqhs despoths ameinwn eautou feugw kai radios apallassw autos kai egw dokew kebhs eis sos teinw o logos ostis outws radios ferw kai egw apoleipw kai arxw agaqos ws autos omologew qeos
english_head: I thought he was pleased by Cebes’ earnestness, and glancing at us, he said, Cebes is always on the track of arguments and will not be easily convinced by whatever anyone says. And Simmias said, Well, Socrates, this time I think myself that Cebes is right. For why should really wise men run away from masters who are better than they and lightly separate themselves from them? And it strikes me that Cebes is aiming his argument at you, because you are so ready to leave us and the gods, who are, as

greek_head: Δίκαια ἔφη λέγετε οἶμαι γὰρ ὑμᾶς λέγειν ὅτι χρή με πρὸς ταῦτα ἀπολογήσασθαι ὥσπερ ἐν δικαστηρίῳ
greek_tokens: dikaios fhmi legw oimh gar su legw ostis xrh egw pros outos apologeomai wsper en dikasthrion panu men oun fhmi o simmias ferw dh eimi de os peiraw piqanos pros su apologeomai h pros o dikasths egw gar fhmi eimi simmias te kai kebhs ei men mh oiomai hkw proteros men para qeos allos sofos te kai agaqos epeita kai para anqrwpos teleutaw ameinwn o enqade adikew an ou aganaktew o qanatos nun de eu oida ostis para
english_head: you yourself agree, good rulers. You have a right to say that, he replied; for I think you mean that I must defend myself against this accusation, as if we were in a law court. Precisely, said Simmias. Well, then, said he, I will try to make a more convincing defence than I did before the judges. For if I did not believe, said he, that I was going to other wise and good gods, and, moreover, to men who have died, better men than those here, I should be wrong in not grieving at death. But as it is, you may rest assured

greek_head: ἄνδρας τε ἐλπίζω ἀφίξεσθαι ἀγαθούς καὶ τοῦτο μὲν οὐκ ἂν πάνυ διισχυρισαίμην ὅτι μέντοι παρὰ θεοὺς δεσπότας πάνυ
greek_tokens: anhr te elpizw afizw agaqos kai outos men ou an panu diisxurizomai ostis mentoi para qeos despoths panu agaqos hkw eu oida ostis eiper tis allos o toioutos diisxurizomai an kai outos wste dia outos ou omoios aganaktew alla euelpis eimi eimi tis o teleutaw kai wsper ge kai pala legw polus ameinwn o agaqos h o kakos tis oun fhmi o simmias eimi swkraths autos exw o dianoia outos en egw exis apeimi h kan egw metadidwmi
english_head: that I expect to go to good men, though I should not care to assert this positively; but I would assert as positively as anything about such matters that I am going to gods who are good masters. And therefore, so far as that is concerned, I not only do not grieve, but I have great hopes that there is something in store for the dead, and, as has been said of old, something better for the good than for the wicked. Well, said Simmias, do you intend to go away, Socrates,

greek_head: κοινὸν γὰρ δὴ ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ καὶ ἡμῖν εἶναι ἀγαθὸν τοῦτο καὶ ἅμα σοι ἡ ἀπολογία ἔσται ἐὰν ἅπερ λέγεις ἡμᾶς πείσῃς
greek_tokens: koinos gar dh egw dokew kai egw eimi agaqos outos kai ama su o apologia eimi ean o legw egw peiqw alla peiraw fhmi proteros de kritwna ode skeptomai tis eimi o boulomai egw dokew pala eipon tis de eimi swkraths fhmi o kritwn allos ge h pala egw legw o mellw su didwmi o farmakon ostis xrh su frazw ws elaxistos dialegw fhmi gar qermainw mallon dialegw dew de oudeis toioutos prosferw
english_head: and keep your opinion to yourself, or would you let us share it? It seems to me that this is a good which belongs in common to us also, and at the same time, if you convince us by what you say, that will serve as your defence. I will try, he replied. But first let us ask Crito there what he wants. He has apparently been trying to say something for a long time. Only, Socrates, said Crito, that the man who is to administer the poison to you has been telling me for some time to warn you to talk as little as possible. He says people get warm when they talk and heat has a bad effect on the action of the poison;

greek_head: τῷ φαρμάκῳ εἰ δὲ μή ἐνίοτε ἀναγκάζεσθαι καὶ δὶς καὶ τρὶς πίνειν τούς τι τοιοῦτον ποιοῦντας
greek_tokens: o farmakon ei de mh eniote anagkazw kai dis kai tris pinw o tis toioutos poiew kai o swkratew ea fhmi xairw autos alla monos o eautou paraskeuazw ws kai dis didwmi ean de deomai kai tris alla sxedon men tis oida fhmi o kritwn alla egw pala pragma paraxew ea autos fhmi alla su dh o dikasths boulomai hdh o logos apodidwmi os egw fainw eoikotws anhr o eimi en filosofia diatribw o bios qarsew mellw
english_head: so sometimes he has to make those who talk too much drink twice or even three times. And Socrates said: Never mind him. Just let him do his part and prepare to give it twice or even, if necessary, three times. I was pretty sure that was what you would say, said Crito, but he has been bothering me for a long time. Never mind him, said Socrates. I wish now to explain to you, my judges, the reason why I think a man who has really spent his life in philosophy is naturally of good courage

greek_head: ἀποθανεῖσθαι καὶ εὔελπις εἶναι ἐκεῖ μέγιστα οἴσεσθαι ἀγαθὰ ἐπειδὰν τελευτήσῃ πῶς ἂν οὖν δὴ τοῦθ' οὕτως ἔχοι ὦ
greek_tokens: apoqnhskw kai euelpis eimi ekei megas ferw agaqos epeidan teleutaw pws an oun dh outos outws exw eimi simmias te kai kebhs egw peiraw frazw kinduneuw gar osos tugxanw orqos aptw filosofia lanqanw o allos ostis oudeis allos autos epithdeuw h apoqnhskw te kai qnhskw ei oun outos alhqhs atopos dhpou an eaw proqumeomai men en pas o bios mhdeis allos h outos hkw de dh autos aganaktew o pala proqumeomai te kai epithdeuw kai o simmias gelaw nh o dios fhmi eimi swkraths
english_head: when he is to die, and has strong hopes that when he is dead he will attain the greatest blessings in that other land. So I will try to tell you, Simmias, and Cebes, how this would be. Other people are likely not to be aware that those who pursue philosophy aright study nothing but dying and being dead. Now if this is true, it would be absurd to be eager for nothing but this all their lives, and then to be troubled when that came for which they had all along been eagerly practicing. And Simmias laughed and said, By Zeus,

greek_head: οὐ πάνυ γέ με νυνδὴ γελασείοντα ἐποίησας γελάσαι οἶμαι γὰρ ἂν τοὺς πολλοὺς αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἀκούσαντας δοκεῖν εὖ πάνυ
greek_tokens: ou panu ge egw nundi gelaseiw poiew gelaw oimh gar an o polus autos outos akouw dokew eu panu erw eis o filosofew kai sumfhmi an o men para egw anqrwpos kai panu ostis o eimi e filosofew qanataw kai sfazw ge ou lanqanw ostis acios eimi outos pasxw kai alhqhs ge an legw eimi simmias plhn ge o sfazw mh lanqanw lanqanw gar autos h te qanataw kai h acios eimi qanatos kai oios qanatos e ws alhqhs filosofos
english_head: Socrates, I don’t feel much like laughing just now, but you made me laugh. For I think the multitude, if they heard what you just said about the philosophers, would say you were quite right, and our people at home would agree entirely with you that philosophers desire death, and they would add that they know very well that the philosophers deserve it. And they would be speaking the truth, Simmias, except in the matter of knowing very well. For they do not know in what way the real philosophers desire death, nor in what way they deserve death, nor what kind of a death it is.

greek_head: εἴπωμεν γάρ ἔφη πρὸς ἡμᾶς αὐτούς χαίρειν εἰπόντες ἐκείνοις ἡγούμεθά τι τὸν θάνατον εἶναι
greek_tokens: eipon gar fhmi pros egw autos xairw eipon ekeinos ageomai tis o qanatos eimi panu ge fhmi upolambanw o simmias ara mh allos tis h o o yuxazw apo o swma apallagh kai eimi outos o qnhskw xwris men apo o yuxazw apallassw autos kaqa eautou o swma gignomai xwris de o yuxh apo o swma apallassw autos kaqa eautou eimi ara mh allos tis eimi o qanatos h outos ou alla outos fhmi skeptomai dh agaqos ean ara kai sos sundokew o egw
english_head: Let us then, said he, speak with one another, paying no further attention to them. Do we think there is such a thing as death? Certainly, replied Simmias. We believe, do we not, that death is the separation of the soul from the body, and that the state of being dead is the state in which the body is separated from the soul and exists alone by itself and the soul is separated from the body and exists alone by itself? Is death anything other than this? No, it is this, said he. Now, my friend, see if you agree with me;

greek_head: ἐκ γὰρ τούτων μᾶλλον οἶμαι ἡμᾶς εἴσεσθαι περὶ ὧν σκοποῦμεν φαίνεταί σοι φιλοσόφου ἀνδρὸς εἶναι ἐσπουδακέναι
greek_tokens: ek gar outos mallon oimh egw eimi peri os skopaw fainw su filosofos anhr eimi spoudazw peri o hdona kalew o toiosde oios siteomai te kai poths hkistos eimi swkraths fhmi o simmias tis de o o afrodisios oudamos tis de o allos o peri o swma qerapeia dokew su entimos hgeomai o toioutos oios imation diaferw kthsis kai upodhma kai o allos kallwpismos o peri o swma poteros timaw dokew su h atimazw
english_head: for, if you do, I think we shall get more light on our subject. Do you think a philosopher would be likely to care much about the so-called pleasures, such as eating and drinking? By no means, Socrates, said Simmias. How about the pleasures of love? Certainly not. Well, do you think such a man would think much of the other cares of the body—I mean such as the possession of fine clothes and shoes and the other personal adornments? Do you think he would care about them

greek_head: καθ' ὅσον μὴ πολλὴ ἀνάγκη μετέχειν αὐτῶν Ἀτιμάζειν ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ ἔφη ὅ γε ὡς ἀληθῶς φιλόσοφος
greek_tokens: kaqa osos mh polus anagkh metexw autos atimazw egw dokew fhmi o ge ws alhqhs filosofos oukoun oloc dokew su fhmi o o toioutos pragmateia ou peri o swma eimi alla kaqa osos dunamai afisthmi autos pros de o yuxh trepw egw ara oun proteros men en o toioutos dhlos eimi o
english_head: or despise them, except so far as it is necessary to have them? I think the true philosopher would despise them, he replied. Altogether, then, you think that such a man would not devote himself to the body, but would, so far as he was able, turn away from the body and concern himself with the soul? Yes. To begin with, then, it is clear that in such matters the philosopher, more than other men, separates

greek_head: φιλόσοφος ἀπολύων ὅτι μάλιστα τὴν ψυχὴν ἀπὸ τῆς τοῦ σώματος κοινωνίας διαφερόντως τῶν ἄλλων ἀνθρώπων
greek_tokens: filosofos apoluw ostis malista o yuxh apo o o swma koinwnia diaferontws o allos anqrwpos fainw kai dokew ge pou eimi simmias o polus anqrwpos os mhdeis hdus o toioutos mhde metexw autos ou acios eimi zaw alla eggus tis teinw o qnhskw o mhdeis frontizw o hdona o dia o swma eimi panu men oun alhqhs legw tis de dh peri autos o o fronhsis ktaomai poteros empodios o swma h ou ean tis autos en o zhthsis
english_head: the soul from communion with the body? It is. Now certainly most people think that a man who takes no pleasure and has no part in such things doesn’t deserve to live, and that one who cares nothing for the pleasures of the body is about as good as dead. That is very true. Now, how about the acquirement of pure knowledge? Is the body a hindrance or not, if it is made to share in the search for wisdom?

greek_head: κοινωνὸν συμπαραλαμβάνῃ οἷον τὸ τοιόνδε λέγω ἆρα ἔχει ἀλήθειάν τινα ὄψις τε καὶ ἀκοὴ τοῖς ἀνθρώποις ἢ τά γε
greek_tokens: koinwnos sumparalambanw oios o toiosde legos ara exis alhqeia tis oyis te kai akoh o anqrwpos h o ge toioutos kai e poihths egw aei qrulew ostis oute akouw akribhs oudeis oute oraw kaitoi ei outos o peri o swma aisqhsis mh akribhs eimi mhde safhs sxolazw o ge allos paomai gar pou outos faulos eimi h sos ou dokew panu men oun fhmi pote oun eimi de os o yuxh o alhqeia aptw otan men gar meta o swma epixeirew tis skopaw dhlos ostis tote ecapataw upo autos
english_head: What I mean is this: Have the sight and hearing of men any truth in them, or is it true, as the poets are always telling us, that we neither hear nor see any thing accurately? And yet if these two physical senses are not accurate or exact, the rest are not likely to be, for they are inferior to these. Do you not think so? Certainly I do, he replied. Then, said he, when does the soul attain to truth? For when it tries to consider anything in company with the body, it is evidently deceived by it.

greek_head: Ἀληθῆ λέγεις Ἆρ' οὖν οὐκ ἐν τῷ λογίζεσθαι εἴπερ που ἄλλοθι κατάδηλον
greek_tokens: alhqhs legw ara oun ou en o logizomai eiper pou alloqi katadhlos autos gignomai tis o eimi nai logizomai de ge pou tote kallista otan autos outos mhdeis paralupew mhte akoh mhte oyis mhte alghdwn mhde tis hdona alla ostis malista autos kaqa eautou gignomai eaw xairw o swma kai kaqa osos dunamai mh koinwnew autos mhde aptw oregw o eimi eimi outos oukoun kai entauqa o o filosofos yuxh malista
english_head: True. In thought, then, if at all, something of the realities becomes clear to it? Yes. But it thinks best when none of these things troubles it, neither hearing nor sight, nor pain nor any pleasure, but it is, so far as possible, alone by itself, and takes leave of the body, and avoiding, so far as it can, all association or contact with the body, reaches out toward the reality. That is true. In this matter also, then,

greek_head: ἀτιμάζει τὸ σῶμα καὶ φεύγει ἀπ' αὐτοῦ ζητεῖ δὲ αὐτὴ καθ' αὑτὴν γίγνεσθαι
greek_tokens: atimazw o swma kai feugw apo autos zhtew de autos kaqa eautou gignomai fainw tis de dh o toiosde eimi simmias fhmi tis eimi dikaios autos h oudeis fhmi mentoi nh dios kai au kalos ge tis kai agaqos pws de ou hdh oun pwpote tis o toioutos o ofqalmos eidon oudamos eimi de os alla allh tis aisqhsis o dia o swma efaptw autos legos de peri pas oios megeqos peri ugieia isxus kai o allos eis logos apas o ousia o
english_head: the soul of the philosopher greatly despises the body and avoids it and strives to be alone by itself? Evidently. Now how about such things as this, Simmias? Do we think there is such a thing as absolute justice, or not? We certainly think there is. And absolute beauty and goodness. Of course. Well, did you ever see anything of that kind with your eyes? Certainly not, said he. Or did you ever reach them with any of the bodily senses? I am speaking of all such things, as size, health, strength, and in short the essence

greek_head: τυγχάνει ἕκαστον ὄν ἆρα διὰ τοῦ σώματος αὐτῶν τὸ ἀληθέστατον θεωρεῖται ἢ ὧδε ἔχει ὃς ἂν μάλιστα ἡμῶν
greek_tokens: tugxanw ekastos eimi ara dia o swma autos o alhqhs qewrew h wde exis os an malista egw kai akribhs paraskeuazw autos ekastos dianoeomai peri e skopaw outos an eggus eimi o gignwskw ekastos panu men oun ara oun ekeinos an outos poiew kaqaros ostis ostis malista autos o dianoia eimi epi ekastos mhte tis oyis paratiqhmi en o dianoeomai mhte tis allos
english_head: or underlying quality of everything. Is their true nature contemplated by means of the body? Is it not rather the case that he who prepares himself most carefully to understand the true essence of each thing that he examines would come nearest to the knowledge of it? Certainly. Would not that man do this most perfectly who approaches each thing, so far as possible, with the reason alone, not introducing sight into his reasoning nor dragging in

greek_head: αἴσθησιν ἐφέλκων μηδεμίαν μετὰ τοῦ λογισμοῦ ἀλλ' αὐτῇ καθ' αὑτὴν εἰλικρινεῖ τῇ διανοίᾳ χρώμενος αὐτὸ καθ' αὑτὸ εἰλικρινὲς
greek_tokens: aisqhsis efelkw mhdeis meta o logismos alla autos kaqa eautou eilikrinew o dianoia xraomai autos kaqa eautou eilikrinhs ekastos epixeirew qhreuw o eimi apallassw ostis malista ofqalmos te kai aatos kai ws epos eipon sumpas o swma ws tarassw kai ou eaw o yuxh ktaomai alhqeia te kai fronhsis otan koinwnew ara ou outos eimi eimi simmias eiper tis kai allos o teuxw o eimi uperfuhs fhmi o simmias ws alhqhs legw eimi swkraths
english_head: any of the other senses along with his thinking, but who employs pure, absolute reason in his attempt to search out the pure, absolute essence of things, and who removes himself, so far as possible, from eyes and ears, and, in a word, from his whole body, because he feels that its companionship disturbs the soul and hinders it from attaining truth and wisdom? Is not this the man, Simmias, if anyone, to attain to the knowledge of reality? That is true as true can be, Socrates, said Simmias.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἀνάγκη ἔφη ἐκ πάντων τούτων παρίστασθαι δόξαν τοιάνδε τινὰ τοῖς γνησίως φιλοσόφοις ὥστε καὶ πρὸς
greek_tokens: oukoun anagkh fhmi ek pas outos paristhmi doca toiosde tis o gnhsios filosofos wste kai pros allhlwn toioutos atta legw ostis kinduneuw su wsper atrapos tis ekferw egw meta o logos en o skeptomai ostis ews an o swma exw kai sumfurw eimi egw o yuxh meta toioutos kakos ou mh pote ktaomai ikanos e epiqumew fhmi de outos eimi o alhqhs murios men gar egw asxolia paraxew o swma dia o anagkh
english_head: Then, said he, all this must cause good lovers of wisdom to think and say one to the other something like this: There seems to be a short cut which leads us and our argument to the conclusion in our search that so long as we have the body, and the soul is contaminated by such an evil, we shall never attain completely what we desire, that is, the truth. For the body keeps us constantly busy by reason of its need of sustenance;

greek_head: τροφήν ἔτι δέ ἄν τινες νόσοι προσπέσωσιν ἐμποδίζουσιν ἡμῶν τὴν τοῦ ὄντος θήραν ἐρώτων δὲ καὶ ἐπιθυμιῶν καὶ
greek_tokens: trofh eti de an tis nosos prospitnw empodizw egw o o eimi qhra erws de kai epiqumia kai fobos kai eidwlon pantodaphs kai fluaria empiplhmi egw polus wste o legw ws alhqhs o eimi upo autos oude fronew egw eggignomai oudepote oudeis kai gar polemos kai isthmi kai maxh oudeis allos paraxew h o swma kai o outos epiqumia dia gar o o xrhma ktaomai pas e polemos gignomai o de xrhma
english_head: and moreover, if diseases come upon it they hinder our pursuit of the truth. And the body fills us with passions and desires and fears, and all sorts of fancies and foolishness, so that, as they say, it really and truly makes it impossible for us to think at all. The body and its desires are the only cause of wars and factions and battles; for all wars arise for the sake of gaining money, and we are compelled to gain money

greek_head: ἀναγκαζόμεθα κτᾶσθαι διὰ τὸ σῶμα δουλεύοντες τῇ τούτου θεραπείᾳ καὶ ἐκ τούτου ἀσχολίαν ἄγομεν φιλοσοφίας πέρι
greek_tokens: anagkazw ktaomai dia o swma douleuw o outos qerapeia kai ek outos asxolia agw filosofia peri dia pas outos o de esxatos pas ostis ean tis egw kai sxolh gignomai apo autos kai trapew pros o skopaw tis en o zhthsis au pantaxou parapiptw qorubos paraxew kai taraxh kai ekplhssw wste mh dunamai upo autos kaqoraw alhqhs alla o eimi egw deiknumi ostis ei mellw pote kaqaros tis eimi
english_head: for the sake of the body. We are slaves to its service. And so, because of all these things, we have no leisure for philosophy. But the worst of all is that if we do get a bit of leisure and turn to philosophy, the body is constantly breaking in upon our studies and disturbing us with noise and confusion, so that it prevents our beholding the truth, and in fact we perceive that, if we are ever to know anything absolutely, we must be free from the body and must behold

greek_head: ἀπαλλακτέον αὐτοῦ καὶ αὐτῇ τῇ ψυχῇ θεατέον αὐτὰ τὰ πράγματα καὶ τότε ὡς ἔοικεν ἡμῖν ἔσται οὗ ἐπιθυμοῦμέν
greek_tokens: apallakteon autos kai autos o yuxw qeateon autos o pragma kai tote ws eoika egw eimi e epiqumew te kai fhmi erasths eimi fronhsis epeidan teleutaw ws o logos shmainw zaw de ou ei gar mh oios te meta o swma mhdeis kaqaros gignwskw duo eteros h oudamos eimi ktaomai o oida h teleutaw tote
english_head: the actual realities with the eye of the soul alone. And then, as our argument shows, when we are dead we are likely to possess the wisdom which we desire and claim to be enamored of, but not while we live. For, if pure knowledge is impossible while the body is with us, one of two thing must follow, either it cannot be acquired at all or only when we are dead; for then the soul

greek_head: γὰρ αὐτὴ καθ' αὑτὴν ἡ ψυχὴ ἔσται χωρὶς τοῦ σώματος πρότερον δ' οὔ καὶ ἐν ᾧ ἂν ζῶμεν οὕτως ὡς ἔοικεν
greek_tokens: gar autos kaqa eautou o yuxh eimi xwris o swma proteros de ou kai en os an zaw outws ws eoika eggus eimi o oida ean ostis malista mhdeis omilew o swma mhde koinwnew ostis mh pas anagkh mhde anapimplhmi o outos fusis alla kaqareuw apo autos ews an o qeos autos apolusis egw kai outws men kaqaros apallassw o o swma afrosunh ws o eoika meta toioutos te eimi kai gignwskw dia egw
english_head: will be by itself apart from the body, but not before. And while we live, we shall, I think, be nearest to knowledge when we avoid, so far as possible, intercourse and communion with the body, except what is absolutely necessary, and are not filled with its nature, but keep ourselves pure from it until God himself sets us free. And in this way, freeing ourselves from the foolishness of the body and being pure, we shall, I think, be with the pure and shall know of ourselves all that is pure,—

greek_head: αὐτῶν πᾶν τὸ εἰλικρινές τοῦτο δ' ἐστὶν ἴσως τὸ ἀληθές μὴ καθαρῷ γὰρ καθαροῦ ἐφάπτεσθαι μὴ οὐ θεμιτὸν ᾖ
greek_tokens: autos pas o eilikrinhs outos de eimi isos o alhqhs mh kaqaros gar kaqaros efaptw mh ou qemitos eimi toioutos oimh eimi simmias anagkaion eimi pros allhlwn legw te kai docazw pas o orqos filomaqew h ou dokew su outws pas ge mallon eimi swkraths oukoun fhmi o swkratew ei outos alhqhs eimi etairos polus elpis afikneomai e egw poreuw ekei ikanos eiper pou alloqi ktaomai outos e eneka o polus pragmateia egw en o parerxomai bios gignomai wste o ge
english_head: and that is, perhaps, the truth. For it cannot be that the impure attain the pure. Such words as these, I think, Simmias, all who are rightly lovers of knowledge must say to each other and such must be their thoughts. Do you not agree? Most assuredly, Socrates. Then, said Socrates, if this is true, my friend, I have great hopes that when I reach the place to which I am going, I shall there, if anywhere, attain fully to that which has been my chief object in my past life, so that the journey which is now

greek_head: ἀποδημία ἡ νῦν μοι προστεταγμένη μετὰ ἀγαθῆς ἐλπίδος γίγνεται καὶ ἄλλῳ ἀνδρὶ ὃς ἡγεῖταί οἱ παρεσκευάσθαι τὴν
greek_tokens: apodhmia o nun egw prostassw meta agaqos elpis gignomai kai allos andris os hgeomai e paraskeuazw o dianoia wsper kekaqarmenhn panu men oun fhmi o simmias kaqarsis de eimi ara ou outos sumbainw osper pala en o logos legw o xwrizw ostis malista apo o swma o yuxh kai eqizw autos kaqa eautou pantaxoqen ek o swma sunageirw te kai aqroizw kai oikew kata o dunatos kai en o nun pareimi kai en o
english_head: imposed upon me is begun with good hope; and the like hope exists for every man who thinks that his mind has been purified and made ready. Certainly, said Simmias. And does not the purification consist in this which has been mentioned long ago in our discourse, in separating, so far as possible, the soul from the body and teaching the soul the habit of collecting and bringing itself together from all parts of the body, and living, so far as it can, both now

greek_head: ἔπειτα μόνην καθ' αὑτήν ἐκλυομένην ὥσπερ ἐκ δεσμῶν ἐκ τοῦ σώματος
greek_tokens: epeita monos kaqa eautou ekluw wsper ek desmh ek o swma panu men oun fhmi oukoun outos ge qanatos onomazw lusis kai xwrismos yuxazw apo swma pantapasi ge eimi de os luw de ge autos os fhmi proqumeomai aei malista kai monos e filosofew orqos kai o melethma autos outos eimi o filosofos lusis kai xwrismos yuxazw apo swma h ou fainw oukoun osper en arxh elegos geloios an eaw anhr
english_head: and hereafter, alone by itself, freed from the body as from fetters? Certainly, said he. Well, then, this is what we call death, is it not, a release and separation from the body? Exactly so, said he. But, as we hold, the true philosophers and they alone are always most eager to release the soul, and just this—the release and separation of the soul from the body—is their study, is it not? Obviously. Then, as I said in the beginning, it would be absurd if a man who had been all his life fitting himself to live as nearly

greek_head: παρασκευάζονθ' ἑαυτὸν ἐν τῷ βίῳ ὅτι ἐγγυτάτω ὄντα τοῦ τεθνάναι οὕτω ζῆν κἄπειθ' ἥκοντος αὐτῷ τούτου ἀγανακτεῖν

english_head: in a state of death as he could, should then be disturbed when death came to him. Would it not be absurd? Of course. In fact, then, Simmias, said he, the true philosophers practice dying, and death is less terrible to them than to any other men. Consider it in this way. They are in every way hostile to the body and they desire to have the soul apart by itself alone. Would it not be very foolish if they should be frightened and troubled when this very thing happens, and if they should not be glad to go to the place where there is hope of attaining

greek_head: ἅσμενοι ἐκεῖσε ἴοιεν οἷ ἀφικομένοις ἐλπίς ἐστιν οὗ διὰ βίου ἤρων τυχεῖν ἤρων δὲ φρονήσεως ᾧ τε διεβέβληντο τούτου
greek_tokens: asmenos ekeise eimi e afikneomai elpis eimi e dia bios arow tugxanw arow de fronhsis os te diaballw outos apallassw suneimi autos h anqrwpinos men paidikon kai gunaikow kai uios apoqnhskw polus dh ekwn eqelw eis adhs meterxomai upo outos agw o elpis o o oraw te ekei os epiqumew kai suneimi fronhsis de ara tis o eimi era kai lambanw sfodra o autos outos elpis mhdamos alloqi entugxanw autos
english_head: what they longed for all through life—and they longed for wisdom—and of escaping from the companionship of that which they hated? When human loves or wives or sons have died, many men have willingly gone to the other world led by the hope of seeing there those whom they longed for, and of being with them; and shall he who is really in love with wisdom and has a firm belief that he can find it nowhere else

greek_head: ἀξίως λόγου ἢ ἐν Ἅιδου ἀγανακτήσει τε ἀποθνῄσκων καὶ οὐχ ἅσμενος εἶσιν αὐτόσε οἴεσθαί γε χρή ἐὰν τῷ ὄντι γε
greek_tokens: acios logos h en adhs aganakthsis te apoqnhskw kai ou asmenos eiseimi autose oiomai ge xrh ean o eimi ge eimi eimi etairos filosofos sfodra gar autos outos docis mhdamos alloqi kaqaros entugxanw fronhsis alla h ekei ei de outos outws exis osper arti elegos ou polus an alogia eaw ei fobew o qanatos o toioutos polus mentoi nh dios eimi de os oukoun ikanos su tekmhrion fhmi outos anhr os an eidon aganaktew mellw apoqnhskw ostis ou ara
english_head: than in the other world grieve when he dies and not be glad to go there? We cannot think that, my friend, if he is really a philosopher; for he will confidently believe that he will find pure wisdom nowhere else than in the other world. And if this is so, would it not be very foolish for such a man to fear death? Very foolish, certainly, said he. Then is it not, said Socrates, a sufficient indication, when you see a man troubled because he is going to die, that he was not a lover of wisdom but a lover of the body?

greek_head: ἦν φιλόσοφος ἀλλά τις φιλοσώματος ὁ αὐτὸς δέ που οὗτος τυγχάνει ὢν καὶ φιλοχρήματος καὶ φιλότιμος ἤτοι τὰ
greek_tokens: eimi filosofos alla tis filoswmatos o autos de pou outos tugxanw eimi kai filoxrhmatos kai filotimos htoi o eteros outos h amfoteros panu fhmi exis outws ws legw ara oun fhmi eimi simmias ou kai o onomazw andreia o outws diakeimai malista proshkw pantws dhpou fhmi oukoun kai o swfrosunos os kai e polus onomazw swfrosunos o peri o epiqumia mh ptoew alla oligwros exw kai kosmios ara ou outos monos proshkw o malista o swma oligwrew te kai en filosofia zaw
english_head: And this same man is also a lover of money and of honor, one or both. Certainly, said he, it is as you say. Then, Simmias, he continued, is not that which is called courage especially characteristic of philosophers? By all means, said he. And self-restraint—that which is commonly called self-restraint, which consists in not being excited by the passions and in being superior to them and acting in a seemly way—is not that characteristic of those alone who despise the body

greek_head: Ἀνάγκη ἔφη Εἰ γὰρ ἐθέλεις ἦ δ' ὅς ἐννοῆσαι τήν γε τῶν ἄλλων
greek_tokens: anagkh fhmi ei gar eqelw eimi de os ennoew o ge o allos andreia te kai swfrosunos docis su eimi atopos pws dh eimi swkraths oida eimi de os ostis o qanatos hgeomai pas e allos o megas kakh kai mala fhmi oukoun fobos megas kakh upomenw autos e andreios o qanatos otan upomenw eimi outos o deidw ara kai deomai andreios eimi pas plhn e filosofos kaitoi alogos ge deomai tis kai deilia andreios eimi
english_head: and pass their lives in philosophy? Necessarily, said he. For, said Socrates, if you care to consider the courage and the self-restraint of other men, you will see that they are absurd. How so, Socrates? You know, do you not, that all other men count death among the great evils? They certainly do. And do not brave men face death—when they do face it—through fear of greater evils? That is true. Then all except philosophers are brave through fear. And yet it is absurd to be brave through fear and cowardice.

greek_head: Πάνυ μὲν οὖν Τί δὲ οἱ κόσμιοι αὐτῶν οὐ ταὐτὸν τοῦτο πεπόνθασιν
greek_tokens: panu men oun tis de e kosmios autos ou tautos outos pasxw akolasia tis swfrwn eimi kaitoi fhmi ge adunatos eimi alla omws autos sumbainw outos omoios o paqos o peri outos o euhqhs swfrosunos fobew gar eteros hdona sterew kai epiqumew ekeinos allos apexw upo allos kratew kaitoi kalew ge akolasia
english_head: Very true. And how about those of seemly conduct? Is their case not the same? They are self-restrained because of a kind of self-indulgence. We say, to be sure, that this is impossible, nevertheless their foolish self-restraint amounts to little more than this; for they fear that they may be deprived of certain pleasures which they desire, and so they refrain from some because they are under the sway of others. And yet being ruled by pleasures

greek_head: τὸ ὑπὸ τῶν ἡδονῶν ἄρχεσθαι ἀλλ' ὅμως συμβαίνει αὐτοῖς κρατουμένοις ὑφ' ἡδονῶν κρατεῖν ἄλλων ἡδονῶν
greek_tokens: o upo o hdona arxw alla omws sumbainw autos kratew ufh hdona kratew allos hdona outos de omoios eimi os nundi legw o tropos tis dia akolasia autos swfronizw eoika gar eimi makarios simmias mh gar ou outos eimi o orqos pros areth allagh hdona pros hdona kai luph pros luph kai fobos pros fobos katallassw kai megas pros elasswn wsper nomisma alla eimi ekeinos monos o nomisma orqos anti e deomai pas outos katallassw fronhsis
english_head: is called self-indulgence. Nevertheless they conquer pleasures because they are conquered by other pleasures. Now this is about what I said just now, that they are self-restrained by a kind of self-indulgence. So it seems. My dear Simmias, I suspect that this is not the right way to purchase virtue, by exchanging pleasures for pleasures, and pains for pains, and fear for fear, and greater for less, as if they were coins, but the only right coinage, for which all those things

greek_head: καὶ τούτου μὲν πάντα καὶ μετὰ τούτου ὠνούμενά τε καὶ πιπρασκόμενα τῷ ὄντι ᾖ καὶ ἀνδρεία καὶ σωφροσύνη καὶ
greek_tokens: kai outos men pas kai meta outos wneomai te kai pipraskw o eimi eimi kai andreia kai swfrosunos kai dikaiosunh kai sullhbdhn alhqhs areth meta fronhsis kai prosgignomai kai apogignomai kai hdona kai fobos kai o allos pas o toioutos xwrizw de fronhsis kai allassw anti allhlwn mh skiagrafia tis eimi o toioutos areth kai o eimi andrapodwdhs te kai oudeis ugihs oude alhqhs exis o de alhqhs o eimi eimi
english_head: must be exchanged and by means of and with which all these things are to be bought and sold, is in fact wisdom; and courage and self-restraint and justice and, in short, true virtue exist only with wisdom, whether pleasures and fears and other things of that sort are added or taken away. And virtue which consists in the exchange of such things for each other without wisdom, is but a painted imitation of virtue and is really slavish and has nothing healthy or true in it; but truth is in fact a purification

greek_head: κάθαρσίς τις τῶν τοιούτων πάντων καὶ ἡ σωφροσύνη καὶ ἡ δικαιοσύνη καὶ ἀνδρεία καὶ αὐτὴ ἡ φρόνησις μὴ καθαρμός
greek_tokens: kaqarsis tis o toioutos pas kai o swfrosunos kai o dikaiosunh kai andreia kai autos o fronhsis mh kaqarmos tis eimi kai kinduneuw kai e o teleth egw outos kaqisthmi ou faulos tis eimi alla o eimi pala ainissomai ostis os an amuhtos kai atelestos eis adhs afikneomai en borboros keimai o de kekaqarmenos te kai telew ekeise afikneomai meta qea oikhsis eimi gar dh os fhmi e peri o teleth narqhkoforos
english_head: from all these things, and self-restraint and justice and courage and wisdom itself are a kind of purification. And I fancy that those men who established the mysteries were not unenlightened, but in reality had a hidden meaning when they said long ago that whoever goes uninitiated and unsanctified to the other world will lie in the mire, but he who arrives there initiated and purified will dwell with the gods. For as they say in the mysteries, the thyrsus-bearers are many, but the mystics few;

greek_head: μὲν πολλοί βάκχοι δέ τε παῦροι οὗτοι δ' εἰσὶν κατὰ τὴν ἐμὴν δόξαν οὐκ ἄλλοι ἢ οἱ πεφιλοσοφηκότες ὀρθῶς ὧν δὴ
greek_tokens: men polus bakxos de te pauros outos de eimi kata o emos doca ou allos h e filosofew orqos os dh kai egw kata ge o dunatos oudeis apoleipw en o bios alla pas tropos proqumeomai gignomai ei de orqos proqumeomai kai tis anuw ekeise erxomai o safhs eimi an qeos eqelw oligos usteron ws egw dokew outos oun egw fhmi eimi simmias te kai kebhs apologeomai ws eoikotws su te apoleipw kai o enqade despoths ou
english_head: and these mystics are, I believe, those who have been true philosophers. And I in my life have, so far as I could, left nothing undone, and have striven in every way to make myself one of them. But whether I have striven aright and have met with success, I believe I shall know clearly, when I have arrived there, very soon, if it is God’s will. This then, Simmias and Cebes, is the defence I offer to show that it is reasonable for me not to be grieved or troubled at leaving you and the rulers I have here,

greek_head: χαλεπῶς φέρω οὐδ' ἀγανακτῶ ἡγούμενος κἀκεῖ οὐδὲν ἧττον ἢ ἐνθάδε δεσπόταις τε ἀγαθοῖς ἐντεύξεσθαι καὶ ἑταίροις
greek_tokens: xalepos ferw oude aganaktew hgeomai akeomai oudeis hsswn h enqade despoths te agaqos entugxanw kai etairos o de polus apistia paraxew ei tis oun su piqanos eimi en o apologia h o aqhnaia dikasths eu an exw eipon dh o swkraths outos upolambanw o kebhs fhmi eimi swkraths o men allos egw dokew kalos legw
english_head: because I believe that there, no less than here, I shall find good rulers and friends. If now I am more successful in convincing you by my defence than I was in convincing my Athenian judges, it is well. When Socrates had finished, Cebes answered and said: Socrates, I agree to

greek_head: τὰ δὲ περὶ τῆς ψυχῆς πολλὴν ἀπιστίαν παρέχει τοῖς ἀνθρώποις μή ἐπειδὰν ἀπαλλαγῇ τοῦ σώματος οὐδαμοῦ ἔτι ᾖ ἀλλ' ἐκείνῃ
greek_tokens: o de peri o yuxazw polus apistia paraxew o anqrwpos mh epeidan apallassw o swma oudamos eti eimi alla ekeinos o hmeros diafqeirw te kai apollumi h an o anqrwpos apoqnhskw euqus apallassw o swma kai ekbainw wsper pneuma h kapnos diaskedannumi oixomai diapetamai kai oudeis eti oudamos eimi epei eiper eaw pou autos kaqa eautou sunaqroizw kai apallassw outos o kakh os su nundi dierxomai polus an eaw elpis kai kalos eimi
english_head: the other things you say, but in regard to the soul men are very prone to disbelief. They fear that when the soul leaves the body it no longer exists anywhere, and that on the day when the man dies it is destroyed and perishes, and when it leaves the body and departs from it, straightway it flies away and is no longer anywhere, scattering like a breath or smoke. If it exists anywhere by itself as a unit, freed from these evils which you have enumerated just now,

greek_head: Σώκρατες ὡς ἀληθῆ ἐστιν ἃ σὺ λέγεις ἀλλὰ τοῦτο δὴ ἴσως οὐκ ὀλίγης παραμυθίας δεῖται καὶ πίστεως ὡς ἔστι τε
greek_tokens: swkraths ws alhqhs eimi o su legw alla outos dh isos ou oligos paramuqia deomai kai pistis ws eimi te yuxh apoqnhskw o anqrwpos kai tis dunamis exis kai fronhsis alhqhs fhmi legw o swkratew eimi kebhs alla tis dh poiew h peri autos outos boulomai diamuqologew eite eoika outws exw eite mh egw goun fhmi o kebhs hdews an akouw ostis doca exis peri autos oukoun ge an oimh eimi de os o swkratew eipon tis nun
english_head: there would be good reason for the blessed hope, Socrates, that what you say is true. But perhaps no little argument and proof is required to show that when a man is dead the soul still exists and has any power and intelligence. What you say, Cebes, is true, said Socrates. Now what shall we do? Do you wish to keep on conversing about this to see whether it is probable or not? I do, said Cebes. I should like to hear what you think about it. Well, said Socrates, I do not believe anyone who heard us now,

greek_head: ἀκούσαντα οὐδ' εἰ κωμῳδοποιὸς εἴη ὡς ἀδολεσχῶ καὶ οὐ περὶ προσηκόντων τοὺς λόγους ποιοῦμαι εἰ οὖν δοκεῖ χρὴ
greek_tokens: akouw oude ei kwmwdopoios eaw ws adolesxew kai ou peri proshkw o logos poiew ei oun dokew xrh diaskopew skeptomai de autos ode ph eite ara en adhs eimi o yuxh teleutaw o anqrwpos eite kai ou palaios men oun eimi tis logos e mimnhskw ws eimi enqende afikneomai ekei kai palin ge deuro afikneomai kai gignomai ek o qnhskw kai ei outos outws exis palin gignomai ek o apoqnhskw o zaw allos tis h eien
english_head: even if he were a comic poet, would say that I am chattering and talking about things which do not concern me. So if you like, let us examine the matter to the end. Let us consider it by asking whether the souls of men who have died are in the nether world or not. There is an ancient tradition, which we remember, that they go there from here and come back here again and are born from the dead. Now if this is true, if the living are born again from the dead, our souls would exist there,

greek_head: ἂν αἱ ψυχαὶ ἡμῶν ἐκεῖ οὐ γὰρ ἄν που πάλιν ἐγίγνοντο μὴ οὖσαι καὶ τοῦτο ἱκανὸν τεκμήριον τοῦ ταῦτ' εἶναι εἰ τῷ
greek_tokens: an o yuxh egw ekei ou gar an pou palin gignomai mh eimi kai outos ikanos tekmhrion o outos eimi ei o eimi faneros gignomai ostis oudamoqen alloqen gignomai e zaw h ek o qnhskw ei de mh eimi outos allos an tis dew logos panu men oun fhmi o kebhs mh toinun kata anqrwpos eimi de os skopaw monos outos ei boulomai raios manqanw alla kai kata zwion pas kai futon kai sullhbdhn osos exis genesis peri pas
english_head: would they not? For they could not be born again if they did not exist, and this would be a sufficient proof that they exist, if it should really be made evident that the living are born only from the dead. But if this is not so then some other argument would be needed. Certainly, said Cebes. Now, said he, if you wish to find this out easily, do not consider the question with regard to men only, but with regard to all animals and plants, and, in short, to all things which may be said to have birth. Let us see with regard to all these, whether it is true that they are all born or generated

Even if we do not in every case have the words to express it, yet in fact is it not always inevitable that there is a process of generation from each to the other? Certainly, said he.

I consider that all the senses are alike. Yes, Socrates, they are all alike, for the purposes of our argument. Then it is through the senses that we must learn

greek_tokens: pas o en o aisqhsis ekeinos te oregw o o eimi isos kai autos endehs eimi h pws legw outws pro o ara arxw egw oraw kai akouw kai allos aisqanomai tugxanw dew pou lambanw episthmh autos o isos ostis eimi ei mellw o ek o aisqhsis isos ekeise anaferw ostis proqumeomai men pas toioutos eimi oios ekeinos eimi de autos faulos anagkh ek o proerew eimi swkraths oukoun gignomai euqus oraw te kai akouw kai o allos aisqhsis exw panu ge
english_head: that all sensible objects strive after absolute equality and fall short of it. Is that our view? Yes. Then before we began to see or hear or use the other senses we must somewhere have gained a knowledge of abstract or absolute equality, if we were to compare with it the equals which we perceive by the senses, and see that all such things yearn to be like abstract equality but fall short of it. That follows necessarily from what we have said before, Socrates. And we saw and heard and had the other senses as soon as we were born?

greek_head: Ἔδει δέ γε φαμέν πρὸ τούτων τὴν τοῦ ἴσου ἐπιστήμην εἰληφέναι
greek_tokens: dew de ge fhmi pro outos o o isos episthmh lambanw nai prin gignomai ara ws eoika anagkh egw autos lambanw eoika oukoun ei men lambanw autos pro o gignomai exw gignomai epistamai kai prin gignomai kai euqus gignomai ou monos o isos kai o megas kai o elasswn alla kai sumpas o toioutos ou gar peri o isos nun o logos egw mallon tis h kai peri autos o kalew kai autos o
english_head: Certainly. But, we say, we must have acquired a knowledge of equality before we had these senses? Yes. Then it appears that we must have acquired it before we were born. It does. Now if we had acquired that knowledge before we were born, and were born with it, we knew before we were born and at the moment of birth not only the equal and the greater and the less, but all such abstractions? For our present argument is no more concerned with the equal than with absolute beauty and the absolute good and the just and the holy, and, in short,

greek_head: ἀγαθοῦ καὶ δικαίου καὶ ὁσίου καί ὅπερ λέγω περὶ ἁπάντων οἷς ἐπισφραγιζόμεθα τὸ αὐτὸ ὃ ἔστι καὶ ἐν ταῖς ἐρωτήσεσιν
greek_tokens: agaqos kai dikaios kai osios kai osper legos peri apas os episfragizw o autos o eimi kai en o erwthsis erwtaw kai en o apokrisls apokrinw wste anagkaion egw outos pas o episthmh pro o gignomai lambanw eimi outos kai ei men ge lambanw ekastote mh epilanqanomai oida aei gignomai kai aei dia bios oida o gar oida outos eimi lambanw tis episthmh exw kai mh apollumi h ou outos lanqanw legw eimi simmias episthmh apobolh
english_head: with all those things which we stamp with the seal of absolute in our dialectic process of questions and answers; so that we must necessarily have acquired knowledge of all these before our birth. That is true. And if after acquiring it we have not, in each case, forgotten it, we must always be born knowing these things, and must know them throughout our life; for to know is to have acquired knowledge and to have retained it without losing it, and the loss of knowledge is just what we mean when we speak of forgetting, is it not, Simmias? Certainly,

greek_head: Πάντως δήπου ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες Εἰ δέ γε οἶμαι λαβόντες πρὶν γενέσθαι γιγνόμενοι ἀπωλέσαμεν
greek_tokens: pantws dhpou fhmi eimi swkraths ei de ge oimh lambanw prin gignomai gignomai apollumi usteron de o aisqhsis xraomai peri autos ekeinos analambanw o episthmh os pote kai prin exw ara ou o kalew manqanw oikeios an episthmh analambanw eaw outos de pou anamimnhskw legw orqos an legw panu ge
english_head: Socrates, said he. But, I suppose, if we acquired knowledge before we were born and lost it at birth, but afterwards by the use of our senses regained the knowledge which we had previously possessed, would not the process which we call learning really be recovering knowledge which is our own? And should we be right in calling this recollection? Assuredly. For we found that it is possible,

greek_head: Δυνατὸν γὰρ δὴ τοῦτό γε ἐφάνη αἰσθόμενόν τι ἢ ἰδόντα ἢ ἀκούσαντα ἤ τινα ἄλλην αἴσθησιν λαβόντα ἕτερόν τι ἀπὸ
greek_tokens: dunatos gar dh outos ge fainw aisqanomai tis h eidon h akouw h tis allos aisqhsis lambanw eteros tis apo outos ennoew o epilanqanomai os outos plhsiazw anomoios eimi h os omoios wste osper legos duo eteros htoi efisthmi ge autos gignomai kai efisthmi dia bios pas h usteron os fhmi manqanw oudeis alla h anamimnhskw outos kai o maqhsis anamnhsis an eaw kai mala dh outws exis eimi swkraths poteros oun airew eimi simmias efisthmi egw gignomai
english_head: on perceiving a thing by the sight or the hearing or any other sense, to call to mind from that perception another thing which had been forgotten, which was associated with the thing perceived, whether like it or unlike it; so that, as I said, one of two things is true, either we are all born knowing these things and know them all our lives, or afterwards, those who are said to learn merely remember, and learning would then be recollection. That is certainly true, Socrates. Which then do you choose, Simmias? Were we born

greek_head: ἢ ἀναμιμνῄσκεσθαι ὕστερον ὧν πρότερον ἐπιστήμην εἰληφότες ἦμεν
greek_tokens: h anamimnhskw usteron os proteros episthmh lambanw eimi ou exw eimi swkraths en o pareimi airew tis de ode exis airew kai ph su dokew peri autos anhr efisthmi peri os efisthmi exw an didwmi logos h ou polus anagkh fhmi eimi swkraths eimi kai dokew su pas exw didwmi logos peri outos os nundi legw boulomai mentoi fhmi o simmias alla polus mallon fobew mh aurion thnikade ouketi eimi anqrwpos oudeis acios oios te outos poiew
english_head: with the knowledge, or do we recollect afterwards things of which we had acquired knowledge before our birth? I cannot choose at this moment, Socrates. How about this question? You can choose and you have some opinion about it: When a man knows, can he give an account of what he knows or not? Certainly he can, Socrates. And do you think that everybody can give an account of the matters about which we have just been talking? I wish they might, said Simmias; but on the contrary I fear that tomorrow, at this time, there will be no longer any man living who is able to do so properly.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἄρα δοκοῦσί σοι ἐπίστασθαί γε ἔφη ὦ Σιμμία πάντες αὐτά
greek_tokens: ou ara dokew su efisthmi ge fhmi eimi simmias pas autos oudamos anamimnhskw ara o pote manqanw anagkh pote lambanw o yuxh egw o episthmh autos ou gar dh apo e ge anqrwpos gignomai ou dhta proteros ara nai eimi ara eimi simmias o yuxh kai proteros prin eimi en anqrwpos eidomai xwris swma kai fronhsis exw ei mh ara ama gignomai lambanw eimi swkraths outos o episthmh outos gar leipw eti o xronos
english_head: Then, Simmias, you do not think all men know these things? By no means. Then they recollect the things they once learned? Necessarily. When did our souls acquire the knowledge of them? Surely not after we were born as human beings. Certainly not. Then previously. Yes. Then, Simmias, the souls existed previously, before they were in human form, apart from bodies, and they had intelligence. Unless, Socrates, we acquire these ideas at the moment of birth; for that time

greek_head: Εἶεν ὦ ἑταῖρε ἀπόλλυμεν δὲ αὐτὰς ἐν ποίῳ ἄλλῳ χρόνῳ οὐ γὰρ δὴ ἔχοντές γε αὐτὰς γιγνόμεθα ὡς ἄρτι ὡμολογήσαμεν ἢ
greek_tokens: eien eimi etairos apollumi de autos en poios allos xronos ou gar dh exw ge autos gignomai ws arti omologew h en outos apollumi en os kai lambanw h exis allos tis eipon xronos oudamos eimi swkraths alla lanqanw emautou oudeis eipon ara oun outws exis fhmi egw eimi simmias ei men eimi o qrulew aei kalos su tis kai agaqos kai pas o toioutos ousia kai epi outos o ek o aisqhsis pas anaferw
english_head: still remains. Very well, my friend. But at what other time do we lose them? For we are surely not born with them, as we just now agreed. Do we lose them at the moment when we receive them, or have you some other time to suggest? None whatever, Socrates. I did not notice that I was talking nonsense. Then, Simmias, said he, is this the state of the case? If, as we are always saying, the beautiful exists, and the good, and every essence of that kind, and if we refer all our sensations to these,

greek_head: ὑπάρχουσαν πρότερον ἀνευρίσκοντες ἡμετέραν οὖσαν καὶ ταῦτα ἐκείνῃ ἀπεικάζομεν ἀναγκαῖον οὕτως ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: uparxw proteros aneuriskw hmeteros eimi kai outos ekeinos apeikazw anagkaion outws wsper kai outos eimi outws kai o hmeteros yuxh eimi kai prin gignomai egw ei de mh eimi outos allos an o logos outos erw eaw ara outws exis kai isos anagkh outos te eimi kai o hmeteros yuxh prin kai egw gignomai kai ei mh outos oude ode uperfuhs eimi swkraths fhmi o simmias dokew egw o autos anagkh eimi kai eis kalos ge katafeugw o logos eis
english_head: which we find existed previously and are now ours, and compare our sensations with these, is it not a necessary inference that just as these abstractions exist, so our souls existed before we were born; and if these abstractions do not exist, our argument is of no force? Is this the case, and is it equally certain that provided these things exist our souls also existed before we were born, and that if these do not exist, neither did our souls? Socrates, it seems to me that there is absolutely the same certainty, and our argument comes to the excellent conclusion that

greek_head: τὸ ὁμοίως εἶναι τήν τε ψυχὴν ἡμῶν πρὶν γενέσθαι ἡμᾶς καὶ τὴν οὐσίαν ἣν σὺ νῦν λέγεις οὐ γὰρ ἔχω ἔγωγε οὐδὲν
greek_tokens: o omoios eimi o te yuxh egw prin gignomai egw kai o ousia os su nun legw ou gar exw egw oudeis outws egw enarghs eimi ws outos o pas o toioutos eimi ws oios te malista kalos te kai agaqos kai allos pas o su nundi legw kai egw dokew ikanos apodeiknumi tis de dh kebhti fhmi o swkratew deomai gar kai kebhta peiqw ikanos fhmi o simmias ws egw oimh kaitoi karteros anqrwpos eimi pros o apistew o logos alla oimh ou endehs outos peiqw autos ostis prin gignomai
english_head: our soul existed before we were born, and that the essence of which you speak likewise exists. For there is nothing so clear to me as this, that all such things, the beautiful, the good, and all the others of which you were speaking just now, have a most real existence. And I think the proof is sufficient. But how about Cebes? said Socrates. For Cebes must be convinced, too. He is fully convinced, I think, said Simmias; and yet he is the most obstinately incredulous of mortals. Still, I believe he is quite convinced of this, that our soul existed

greek_head: ἡμᾶς ἦν ἡμῶν ἡ ψυχή εἰ μέντοι καὶ ἐπειδὰν ἀποθάνωμεν ἔτι ἔσται οὐδὲ αὐτῷ μοι δοκεῖ ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες ἀποδεδεῖχθαι
greek_tokens: egw eimi egw o yuxh ei mentoi kai epeidan apoqnhskw eti eimi oude autos egw dokew fhmi eimi swkraths apodeiknumi alla eti enisthmi o nundi kebhs elegos o o polus opws mh ama apoqnhskw o anqrwpos diaskedannumi o yuxh kai autos o eimi outos telos eimi tis gar kwluw gignomai men autos kai sunisthmi alloqen poqen kai eimi prin kai eis anqrwpeios swma afikneomai epeidan de afikneomai kai apallassw outos tote kai autos teleutaw kai diafqeirw
english_head: before we were born. However, that it will still exist after we die does not seem even to me to have been proved, Socrates, but the common fear, which Cebes mentioned just now, that when a man dies the soul is dispersed and this is the end of his existence, still remains. For assuming that the soul comes into being and is brought together from some source or other and exists before it enters into a human body, what prevents it, after it has entered into and left that body, from coming to an end and being destroyed itself?

greek_head: Εὖ λέγεις ἔφη ὦ Σιμμία ὁ Κέβης φαίνεται γὰρ ὥσπερ ἥμισυ ἀποδεδεῖχθαι οὗ δεῖ ὅτι πρὶν γενέσθαι ἡμᾶς
greek_tokens: eu legw fhmi eimi simmias o kebhs fainw gar wsper hmisus apodeiknumi e deomai ostis prin gignomai egw eimi egw o yuxh deomai de prosapodeiknumi ostis kai epeidan apoqnhskw oudeis hsswn eimi h prin gignomai ei mellw telos o apodeicis exw apodeiknumi men fhmi eimi simmias te kai kebhs o swkratew kai nun ei eqelw suntiqhmi outos te o logos eis tautos kai os pro outos omologew o gignomai pas o zaw ek o qnhskw ei gar eimi men
english_head: You are right, Simmias, said Cebes. It seems to me that we have proved only half of what is required, namely, that our soul existed before our birth. But we must also show that it exists after we are dead as well as before our birth, if the proof is to be perfect. It has been shown, Simmias and Cebes, already, said Socrates, if you will combine this conclusion with the one we reached before, that every living being is born from the dead. For if the soul exists before birth, and,

greek_head: ἡ ψυχὴ καὶ πρότερον ἀνάγκη δὲ αὐτῇ εἰς τὸ ζῆν ἰούσῃ τε καὶ γιγνομένῃ μηδαμόθεν ἄλλοθεν ἢ ἐκ θανάτου καὶ τοῦ
greek_tokens: o yuxh kai proteros anagkh de autos eis o zaw eimi te kai gignomai mhdamoqen alloqen h ek qanatos kai o qnhskw gignomai pws ou anagkh autos kai epeidan apoqnhskw eimi epei ge deomai auqis autos gignomai apodeiknumi men oun osper legw kai nun omws de egw dokew su te kai simmias hdews an kai outos diapragmateuomai o logos eti mallon kai deidw o o pais mh ws alhqhs o anemos autos ekbainw ek o swma diafusaw
english_head: when it comes into life and is born, cannot be born from anything else than death and a state of death, must it not also exist after dying, since it must be born again? So the proof you call for has already been given. However, I think you and Simmias would like to carry on this discussion still further. You have the childish fear that when the soul goes out from the body the wind will really blow it away and scatter it, especially
