greek_head: καὶ διασκεδάννυσιν ἄλλως τε καὶ ὅταν τύχῃ τις μὴ ἐν νηνεμίᾳ ἀλλ' ἐν μεγάλῳ τινὶ πνεύματι ἀποθνῄσκων
greek_tokens: kai diaskedannumi allos te kai otan tuxh tis mh en nhnemia alla en megas tis pneuma apoqnhskw kai o kebhs epigelaw ws deidw fhmi eimi swkraths peiraw anapeiqw mallon de mh ws egw deidw alla isos en tis kai en egw pas ostis o toioutos fobew outos oun peiraw metapeiqw mh deidw o qanatos wsper o mormolukeion alla xrh fhmi o swkratew epaeidw autos ekastos hmeros ews an ecepadw
english_head: if a man happens to die in a high wind and not in calm weather. And Cebes laughed and said, Assume that we have that fear, Socrates, and try to convince us; or rather, do not assume that we are afraid, but perhaps there is a child within us, who has such fears. Let us try to persuade him not to fear death as if it were a hobgoblin. Ah, said Socrates, you must sing charms to him every day until you charm away his fear.

greek_head: Πόθεν οὖν ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες τῶν τοιούτων ἀγαθὸν ἐπῳδὸν ληψόμεθα ἐπειδὴ σύ ἔφη ἡμᾶς ἀπολείπεις
greek_tokens: pinw oun fhmi eimi swkraths o toioutos agaqos epwdos lambanw epei su fhmi egw apoleipw polus men o ella fhmi eimi kebhs en h eneimi pou agaqos anhr polus de kai o o barbaros genos os pas xrh diereunaw zhtew toioutos epwdos mhte xrhma feidomai mhte ponos ws ou eimi eis ostis an eukairos analiskw xrhma zhtew de xrh kai autos meta allhlwn isos gar an oude radios euriskw mallon su dunamai outos poiew alla outos men dh fhmi uparcis o kebhs oqen de
english_head: Where then, Socrates, said he, shall we find a good singer of such charms, since you are leaving us? Hellas, Cebes, he replied, is a large country, in which there are many good men, and there are many foreign peoples also. You ought to search through all of them in quest of such a charmer, sparing neither money nor toil, for there is no greater need for which you could spend your money. And you must seek among yourselves, too, for perhaps you would hardly find others better able to do this than you. That, said Cebes, shall be done. But let us return to the point where we left off,

greek_head: ἀπελίπομεν ἐπανέλθωμεν εἴ σοι ἡδομένῳ ἐστίν Ἀλλὰ μὴν ἡδομένῳ γε πῶς γὰρ οὐ μέλλει
greek_tokens: apoleipw epanerxomai ei su hdomai eimi alla meis hdomai ge pws gar ou mellw kalos fhmi legw oukoun toiosde tis eimi de os o swkratew deomai egw aneromai eautou o poios tis ara proshkw outos o paqos pasxw o diaskedannumi kai uper o poios tis deidw mh paqh autos kai o poios tis ou kai meta outos au episkeptomai poteros o yuxh eimi kai ek outos qarsew h deidw uper o hmeteros yuxazw alhqhs fhmi legw
english_head: if you are willing. Oh, I am willing, of course. Good, said he. Well then, said Socrates, must we not ask ourselves some such question as this? What kind of thing naturally suffers dispersion, and for what kind of thing might we naturally fear it, and again what kind of thing is not liable to it? And after this must we not inquire to which class the soul belongs and base our hopes or fears for our souls upon the answers to these questions? You are quite right, he replied. Now is not that which is compounded

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν τῷ μὲν συντεθέντι τε καὶ συνθέτῳ ὄντι φύσει προσήκει τοῦτο πάσχειν διαιρεθῆναι ταύτῃ ᾗπερ συνετέθη
greek_tokens: ara oun o men suntiqhmi te kai sunqetos eimi fusis proshkw outos pasxw diairew outos hper suntiqhmi ei de tis tugxanw eimi asunqetos outos monos proshkw mh pasxw outos eiper tis allos dokew egw fhmi outws exw o kebhs oukoun o aei kata tautos kai wsautws exis outos malista eoika eimi o asunqetos o de allote allos kai mhdepote kata tautos outos de sunqetos egw dokew outws eimi dh fhmi epi tautos epi o en o emprosqen
english_head: and composite naturally liable to be decomposed, in the same way in which it was compounded? And if anything is uncompounded is not that, if anything, naturally unlikely to be decomposed? I think, said Cebes, that is true. Then it is most probable that things which are always the same and unchanging are the uncompounded things and the things that are changing and never the same are the composite things? Yes, I think so. Let us then, said he, turn to what we were discussing before.

greek_head: λόγῳ αὐτὴ ἡ οὐσία ἧς λόγον δίδομεν τοῦ εἶναι καὶ ἐρωτῶντες καὶ ἀποκρινόμενοι πότερον ὡσαύτως ἀεὶ ἔχει κατὰ
greek_tokens: logos autos o ousia eis logos didwmi o eimi kai erwtaw kai apokrinw poteros wsautws aei exis kata tautos h allote allos autos o isos autos o kalos autos ekastos o eimi o eimi mh pote metabolh kai ostis endexomai h aei autos ekastos o eimi monoeidhs eimi autos kaqa eautou wsautws kata tautos exis kai oudepote oudamh oudamos alloiwsis oudeis endexomai wsautws fhmi anagkh o kebhs kata tautos exw eimi swkraths tis de o polus kalh oios anqrwpos h ippos h
english_head: Is the absolute essence, which we in our dialectic process of question and answer call true being, always the same or is it liable to change? Absolute equality, absolute beauty, any absolute existence, true being—do they ever admit of any change whatsoever? Or does each absolute essence, since it is uniform and exists by itself, remain the same and never in any way admit of any change? It must, said Cebes, necessarily remain the same,

greek_head: ἱματίων ἢ ἄλλων ὡντινωνοῦν τοιούτων ἢ ἴσων ἢ καλῶν ἢ πάντων τῶν ἐκείνοις ὁμωνύμων ἆρα κατὰ ταὐτὰ ἔχει ἢ πᾶν
greek_tokens: imation h allos ostis toioutos h isos h kalh h pas o ekeinos omwnumos ara kata tautos exis h pas enantion ekeinos oute autos eautou oute allhlwn oudepote ws epos eipon oudamos kata tautos outws au fhmi o kebhs outos oudepote wsautws exis
english_head: Socrates. But how about the many things, for example, men, or horses, or cloaks, or any other such things, which bear the same names as the absolute essences and are called beautiful or equal or the like? Are they always the same? Or are they, in direct opposition to the essences, constantly changing in themselves, unlike each other, and, so to speak, never the same? The latter, said Cebes; they are never the same.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν τούτων μὲν κἂν ἅψαιο κἂν ἴδοις κἂν ταῖς ἄλλαις αἰσθήσεσιν αἴσθοιο τῶν δὲ κατὰ ταὐτὰ ἐχόντων οὐκ ἔστιν
greek_tokens: oukoun outos men kan aptw kan eidon kan o allos aisqhsis aisqanomai o de kata tautos exw ou eimi ostis pote an allos epilambanw h o o dianoia logismos alla eimi aidhs o toioutos kai ou oraths pantapasi fhmi alhqhs legw qazw oun boulomai fhmi duo eidos o eimi o men oratos o de aidhs qazw fhmi kai o men aidhs aei kata tautos exw o de oratos mhdepote kata tautos kai outos fhmi qazw
english_head: And you can see these and touch them and perceive them by the other senses, whereas the things which are always the same can be grasped only by the reason, and are invisible and not to be seen? Certainly, said he, that is true. Now, said he, shall we assume two kinds of existences, one visible, the other invisible? Let us assume them, said Cebes. And that the invisible is always the same and the visible constantly changing? Let us assume that also, said he.

greek_head: Φέρε δή ἦ δ' ὅς ἄλλο τι ἡμῶν αὐτῶν τὸ μὲν σῶμά ἐστι τὸ δὲ ψυχή
greek_tokens: ferw dh eimi de os allos tis egw autos o men swma eimi o de yuxh oudeis allos fhmi poteros oun omoios o eidomai fhmi an eimi kai suggenhs o swma pas fhmi outos ge dhlos ostis o oratos tis de o yuxh oratos h aidhs ou upo anqrwpos ge eimi swkraths fhmi alla meis egw ge o oraths kai o mh o o anqrwpos fusis legw h allh tis oiomai o o anqrwpos tis oun peri yuxazw legw oratos h aoratos eimi ou oratos aidhs ara nai omoios ara yuxh swma eimi o aidhs o de o oratos
english_head: Well then, said Socrates, are we not made up of two parts, body and soul? Yes, he replied. Now to which class should we say the body is more similar and more closely akin? To the visible, said he; that is clear to everyone. And the soul? Is it visible or invisible? Invisible, to man, at least, Socrates. But we call things visible and invisible with reference to human vision, do we not? Yes, we do. Then what do we say about the soul? Can it be seen or not? It cannot be seen. Then it is invisible? Yes. Then the soul is more like the invisible than the body is,

greek_head: Πᾶσα ἀνάγκη ὦ Σώκρατες Οὐκοῦν καὶ τόδε πάλαι ἐλέγομεν ὅτι ἡ ψυχή ὅταν μὲν
greek_tokens: pas anagkh eimi swkraths oukoun kai ode pala legw ostis o yuxh otan men o swma prosxraomai eis o skopaw tis h dia o oraw h dia o akouw h dia allos tis aisqhsis outos gar eimi o dia o swma o dia aisqhsis skopaw tis tote men elkw upo o swma eis o oudepote kata tautos exw kai autos planaw kai tarassw kai eiliggiaw wsper mequw ate toioutos efaptw panu ge
english_head: and the body more like the visible. Necessarily, Socrates. Now we have also been saying for a long time, have we not, that, when the soul makes use of the body for any inquiry, either through seeing or hearing or any of the other senses—for inquiry through the body means inquiry through the senses,—then it is dragged by the body to things which never remain the same, and it wanders about and is confused and dizzy like a drunken man because it lays hold upon such things? Certainly. But when the soul

greek_head: Ὅταν δέ γε αὐτὴ καθ' αὑτὴν σκοπῇ ἐκεῖσε οἴχεται εἰς τὸ καθαρόν τε καὶ ἀεὶ ὂν καὶ ἀθάνατον καὶ ὡσαύτως ἔχον
greek_tokens: otan de ge autos kaqa eautou skopaw ekeise oixomai eis o kaqaros te kai aei eimi kai aqanatos kai wsautws exw kai ws suggenhs eimi autos aei meta ekeinos te gignomai otan autos kaqa eautou gignomai kai ecesti autos kai pauw te o planos kai peri ekeinos aei kata tautos wsautws exis ate toioutos efaptw kai outos autos o paqhma fronhsis kalew pantapasi fhmi kalos kai alhqhs legw eimi swkraths poteros oun au su dokew o eidomai kai ek o prosqen kai ek
english_head: inquires alone by itself, it departs into the realm of the pure, the everlasting, the immortal and the changeless, and being akin to these it dwells always with them whenever it is by itself and is not hindered, and it has rest from its wanderings and remains always the same and unchanging with the changeless, since it is in communion therewith. And this state of the soul is called wisdom. Is it not so? Socrates, said he, what you say is perfectly right and true. And now again, in view of what we said before and of what has just been said, to which class do you think

greek_head: τῶν νῦν λεγομένων ψυχὴ ὁμοιότερον εἶναι καὶ συγγενέστερον Πᾶς ἄν μοι δοκεῖ ἦ δ' ὅς συγχωρῆσαι ὦ Σώκρατες ἐκ
greek_tokens: o nun legw yuxh omoios eimi kai suggenhs pas an egw dokew eimi de os sugxwrew eimi swkraths ek outos o meqodos kai o dusmaqhs ostis oloc kai pas omoios eimi yuxh o aei wsautws exw mallon h o mh tis de o swma o eteros oraw dh kai ode ostis epeidan en o autos eimi yuxh kai
english_head: the soul has greater likeness and kinship? I think, Socrates, said he, that anyone, even the dullest, would agree, after this argument that the soul is infinitely more like that which is always the same than that which is not. And the body? Is more like the other. Consider, then, the matter in another way. When the soul

greek_head: σῶμα τῷ μὲν δουλεύειν καὶ ἄρχεσθαι ἡ φύσις προστάττει τῇ δὲ ἄρχειν καὶ δεσπόζειν καὶ κατὰ ταῦτα αὖ πότερόν σοι
greek_tokens: swma o men douleuw kai arxw o fusis prostassw o de arxw kai despozw kai kata outos au poteros su dokew omoios o qeios eimi kai poteros o qnhtos h ou dokew su o men qew oios arxw te kai hgemoneuw fuw o de qnhtos arxw te kai douleuw egw poteros oun o yuxh eoika dhlos dh eimi swkraths ostis o men yuxh o qeios o de swma o qnhtos skopaw dh fhmi eimi kebhs ei ek pas o erw
english_head: and the body are joined together, nature directs the one to serve and be ruled, and the other to rule and be master. Now this being the case, which seems to you like the divine, and which like the mortal? Or do you not think that the divine is by nature fitted to rule and lead, and the mortal to obey and serve? Yes, I think so. Which, then, does the soul resemble? Clearly, Socrates, the soul is like the divine and the body like the mortal. Then see, Cebes, if this is not the conclusion from all that we have said,

greek_head: τάδε ἡμῖν συμβαίνει τῷ μὲν θείῳ καὶ ἀθανάτῳ καὶ νοητῷ καὶ μονοειδεῖ καὶ ἀδιαλύτῳ καὶ ἀεὶ ὡσαύτως κατὰ ταὐτὰ
greek_tokens: ode egw sumbainw o men qeios kai aqanatos kai nohtos kai monoeidhs kai adialutos kai aei wsautws kata tautos exw eautou omoios eimi yuxh o de anqrwpinos kai qnhtos kai polueidhs kai anohtos kai dialutos kai mhdepote kata tautos exw eautou omoios au eimi swma exw tis para outos allos legw eimi filos kebhs h ou outws exis ou exw tis oun outos outws exw ara ou swma men taxus dialuw proshkw yuxw de au o parapan adialutos eimi h eggus tis outos
english_head: that the soul is most like the divine and immortal and intellectual and uniform and indissoluble and ever unchanging, and the body, on the contrary, most like the human and mortal and multiform and unintellectual and dissoluble and ever changing. Can we say anything, my dear Cebes, to show that this is not so? No, we cannot. Well then, since this is the case, is it not natural for the body to meet with speedy dissolution and for the soul, on the contrary, to be entirely indissoluble, or nearly so?

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Ἐννοεῖς οὖν ἔφη ἐπειδὰν ἀποθάνῃ ὁ ἄνθρωπος τὸ μὲν
greek_tokens: pws gar ou ennoew oun fhmi epeidan apoqnhskw o anqrwpos o men oratos autos o swma kai en oratos keimai o dh nekros kalew os proshkw dialuw kai diapiptw kai diapnew ou euqus outos oudeis pasxw alla epieikhs suxnos epimenw xronos ean men tis kai xarieis exw o swma teleutaw kai en toioutos wra kai panu mala sumpitnw gar o swma kai tarixeuw wsper e en aiguptos tarixeuw oligos oloc menos amhxanos osos xronos
english_head: Of course. Observe, he went on, that when a man dies, the visible part of him, the body, which lies in the visible world and which we call the corpse, which is naturally subject to dissolution and decomposition, does not undergo these processes at once, but remains for a considerable time, and even for a very long time, if death takes place when the body is in good condition, and at a favorable time of the year. For when the body is shrunk and embalmed, as is done in Egypt, it remains almost entire for an incalculable time. And even if the body decay,

greek_head: ἔνια δὲ μέρη τοῦ σώματος καὶ ἂν σαπῇ ὀστᾶ τε καὶ νεῦρα καὶ τὰ τοιαῦτα πάντα ὅμως ὡς ἔπος εἰπεῖν ἀθάνατά ἐστιν
greek_tokens: enioi de mera o swma kai an shpw osteon te kai neuron kai o toioutos pas omws ws epos eipon aqanatos eimi h ou nai o de yuxh ara o aidhs o eis toioutos topos eteros oixomai gennaios kai kaqaros kai aidhs eis adhs ws alhqhs para o agaqos kai fronimos qeos e an qeos eqelw autika kai o emew yuxw iteon outos de dh egw o toioutos kai outws fuw apallassw o swma euqus diafusaw kai apollumi os fhmi e polus
english_head: some parts of it, such as the bones and sinews and all that, are, so to speak, indestructible. Is not that true? Yes. But the soul, the invisible, which departs into another place which is, like itself, noble and pure and invisible, to the realm of the god of the other world in truth, to the good and wise god, whither, if God will, my soul is soon to go,—is this soul, which has such qualities and such a nature, straightway scattered and destroyed when it departs from the body, as most men say?

greek_head: ἄνθρωποι πολλοῦ γε δεῖ ὦ φίλε Κέβης τε καὶ Σιμμία ἀλλὰ πολλῷ μᾶλλον ὧδ' ἔχει ἐὰν μὲν καθαρὰ ἀπαλλάττηται
greek_tokens: anqrwpos polus ge deomai eimi filos kebhs te kai simmias alla polus mallon wde exis ean men kaqaros apallassw mhdeis o swma sunefelkw ate oudeis koinwnew autos en o bios ekwn eimi alla feugw autos kai sunaqroizw autos eis eautou ate meletaw aei outos o de oudeis allos eimi h orqos filosofew kai o eimi
english_head: Far from it, dear Cebes and Simmias, but the truth is much rather this—if it departs pure, dragging with it nothing of the body, because it never willingly associated with the body in life, but avoided it and gathered itself into itself alone, since this has always been its constant study—but this means nothing else than that it pursued philosophy rightly and

greek_head: τεθνάναι μελετῶσα ῥᾳδίως ἢ οὐ τοῦτ' ἂν εἴη μελέτη θανάτου
greek_tokens: qnhskw meletaw radios h ou outos an eaw meleth qanatos pantapasi ge oukoun outws men exw eis o omoios autos o aidhs aperxomai o qew te kai aqanatos kai fronimos e afikneomai uparxw autos eudaimwn eimi planh kai anoia kai fobos kai agrios erws kai o allos kakh o anqrwpeios apallassw wsper de legw kata o muaw ws alhqhs o loipos xronos meta qea diagw outws fhmi eimi kebhs h allos outws nh dios fhmi o kebhs
english_head: really practiced being in a state of death: or is not this the practice of death? By all means. Then if it is in such a condition, it goes away into that which is like itself, into the invisible, divine, immortal, and wise, and when it arrives there it is happy, freed from error and folly and fear and fierce loves and all the other human ills, and as the initiated say, lives in truth through all after time with the gods. Is this our belief, Cebes, or not? Assuredly, said Cebes. But, I think,

greek_head: Ἐὰν δέ γε οἶμαι μεμιασμένη καὶ ἀκάθαρτος τοῦ σώματος ἀπαλλάττηται ἅτε τῷ σώματι ἀεὶ συνοῦσα καὶ τοῦτο θεραπεύουσα
greek_tokens: ean de ge oimh miainw kai akaqartos o swma apallassw ate o swma aei suneimi kai outos qerapeuw kai eraw kai gohteuw upo autos upo te o epiqumia kai hdona wste mhdeis allos dokew eimi alhqhs alla h o swmatoeidhs e tis an aptw kai eidon kai pinw kai esqiw kai pros o afrodisios xraw o de o omma skotwdhs kai aidhs nohtos de kai filosofia airetos outos de eqizw misew te kai tremw kai feugw outws
english_head: if when it departs from the body it is defiled and impure, because it was always with the body and cared for it and loved it and was fascinated by it and its desires and pleasures, so that it thought nothing was true except the corporeal, which one can touch and see and drink and eat and employ in the pleasures of love, and if it is accustomed to hate and fear and avoid that which is shadowy and invisible to the eyes but is intelligible and tangible to philosophy—do you think a soul in this condition

greek_head: δὴ ἔχουσαν οἴει ψυχὴν αὐτὴν καθ' αὑτὴν εἰλικρινῆ ἀπαλλάξεσθαι
greek_tokens: dh exw oiomai yuxh autos kaqa eautou eilikrinhs apallassw oude opwsoun fhmi alla kai dialambanw ge oimh upo o swmatoeidhs o autos o omilia te kai sunousia o swma dia o aei suneimi kai dia o polus melw empoiew sumfuton panu ge embriqhs de ge eimi filos outos oiomai xrh eimi kai barus kai gewdhs kai oratos o dh kai exw o toioutos yuxh barunw te kai elkw palin eis o oratos topos fobos o aidhs te kai adhs wsper legw peri o
english_head: will depart pure and uncontaminated? By no means, said he. But it will be interpenetrated, I suppose, with the corporeal which intercourse and communion with the body have made a part of its nature because the body has been its constant companion and the object of its care? Certainly. And, my friend, we must believe that the corporeal is burdensome and heavy and earthly and visible. And such a soul is weighed down by this and is dragged back into the visible world, through fear of the invisible and of the other world, and so,

greek_head: μνήματά τε καὶ τοὺς τάφους κυλινδουμένη περὶ ἃ δὴ καὶ ὤφθη ἄττα ψυχῶν σκιοειδῆ φαντάσματα οἷα παρέχονται αἱ
greek_tokens: mnhma te kai o tafos kulindew peri o dh kai oraw atta yuxos skioeidhs fantasma oios parexw o toioutos yuxh eidwlon o mh kaqaros apoluw alla o oraths metexw dio kai oraw eoika ge eimi swkraths eoika mentoi eimi kebhs kai ou tis ge o o agaqos autos eimi alla o o faulos o peri o toioutos anagkazw planaw dikh tinw o proteros trefw kakazw eimi kai mexri ge outos planaw ews an o
english_head: as they say, it flits about the monuments and the tombs, where shadowy shapes of souls have been seen, figures of those souls which were not set free in purity but retain something of the visible; and this is why they are seen. That is likely, Socrates. It is likely, Cebes. And it is likely that those are not the souls of the good, but those of the base, which are compelled to flit about such places as a punishment for their former evil mode of life. And they flit about

greek_head: τοῦ συνεπακολουθοῦντος τοῦ σωματοειδοῦς ἐπιθυμίᾳ πάλιν ἐνδεθῶσιν εἰς σῶμα ἐνδοῦνται δέ ὥσπερ εἰκός εἰς τοιαῦτα
greek_tokens: o sunepakolouqew o swmatoeidhs epiqumia palin endew eis swma endew de wsper eoika eis toioutos hqos opoios atta an kai meletaw tugxanw en o bios o poios dh outos legw eimi swkraths oios o men gastrimargia te kai ubris kai filoposia meletaw kai mh dieulabeomai eis o o onos genos
english_head: until through the desire of the corporeal which clings to them they are again imprisoned in a body. And they are likely to be imprisoned in natures which correspond to the practices of their former life. What natures do you mean, Socrates? I mean, for example, that those who have indulged in gluttony and violence and drunkenness, and have taken no pains to avoid them, are likely to pass into the bodies of asses and other beasts of that sort.

greek_head: καὶ τῶν τοιούτων θηρίων εἰκὸς ἐνδύεσθαι ἢ οὐκ οἴει Πάνυ μὲν οὖν εἰκὸς λέγεις
greek_tokens: kai o toioutos qhraw eoika enduw h ou oiomai panu men oun eoika legw o de ge adikia te kai turannis kai arpagh protimaw eis o o lukos te kai ierac kai iktinos genos h poi an allote fhmi o toioutos eimi amelei fhmi o kebhs eis o toioutos oukoun eimi de os dhlos dh kai allos h an ekastos eimi kata o autos omoioths o meleth dhlos dh fhmi pws de ou oukoun eudaimwn fhmi kai outos eimi kai eis beltistos topos eimi e o dhmotikos kai politikos
english_head: Do you not think so? Certainly that is very likely. And those who have chosen injustice and tyranny and robbery pass into the bodies of wolves and hawks and kites. Where else can we imagine that they go? Beyond a doubt, said Cebes, they pass into such creatures. Then, said he, it is clear where all the others go, each in accordance with its own habits? Yes, said Cebes, of course. Then, said he, the happiest of those, and those who go to the best place, are those who have practiced,

greek_head: ἀρετὴν ἐπιτετηδευκότες ἣν δὴ καλοῦσι σωφροσύνην τε καὶ δικαιοσύνην ἐξ ἔθους τε καὶ μελέτης γεγονυῖαν ἄνευ φιλοσοφίας
greek_tokens: areth epithdeuw os dh kalew swfrosunos te kai dikaiosunh ek eqos te kai meleth gignomai aneu filosofia te kai neomai ph dh outos eudaimwn ostis outos eoika eimi eis toioutos palin afikneomai politikos kai hmeros genos h pou melissa h sfhc h murmhc kai eis tautos ge palin o anqrwpinos genos kai gignomai ek autos anhr metrios eoika eis de ge qea genos mh filosofew kai pantelhs
english_head: by nature and habit, without philosophy or reason, the social and civil virtues which are called moderation and justice? How are these happiest? Don’t you see? Is it not likely that they pass again into some such social and gentle species as that of bees or of wasps or ants, or into the human race again, and that worthy men spring from them? Yes. And no one who has not been a philosopher and who is not wholly pure when he departs, is allowed to enter into the communion of the gods,

greek_head: καθαρῷ ἀπιόντι οὐ θέμις ἀφικνεῖσθαι ἀλλ' ἢ τῷ φιλομαθεῖ ἀλλὰ τούτων ἕνεκα ὦ ἑταῖρε Σιμμία τε καὶ Κέβης οἱ
greek_tokens: kaqaros apeimi ou qemis afikneomai alla h o filomaqew alla outos eneka eimi etairos simmias te kai kebhs e orqos filosofos apexw o kata o swma epiqumia apas kai karterew kai ou paradidwmi autos eautou ou tis oikofqoria te kai penia fobew wsper e polus kai filoxrhmatos oude au atimia te kai adocia moxqhria deidw wsper e filarxos te kai filotimos epeita apexw autos ou gar an prepw fhmi eimi swkraths o kebhs
english_head: but only the lover of knowledge. It is for this reason, dear Simmias and Cebes, that those who truly love wisdom refrain from all bodily desires and resist them firmly and do not give themselves up to them, not because they fear poverty or loss of property, as most men, in their love of money, do; nor is it because they fear the dishonor or disgrace of wickedness, like the lovers of honor and power, that they refrain from them. No, that would not be seemly for them, Socrates, said Cebes. Most assuredly not,

greek_head: Οὐ μέντοι μὰ Δία ἦ δ' ὅς τοιγάρτοι τούτοις μὲν ἅπασιν ὦ Κέβης ἐκεῖνοι οἷς τι μέλει τῆς ἑαυτῶν ψυχῆς
greek_tokens: ou mentoi ma dios eimi de os toigar outos men apas eimi kebhs ekeinos os tis melos o eautou yuxazw alla mh swma plassw zaw xairw eipon ou kata tautos poreuw autos ws ou oida oph erxomai autos de hgeomai ou dew enantios o filosofia prassw kai o ekeinos lusis te kai kaqarmos outos dh trepw ekeinos epomai h ekeinos ufhgeomai pws eimi swkraths egw eraomai fhmi gignwskw gar eimi de os e filomaqew
english_head: said he. And therefore those who care for their own souls, and do not live in service to the body, turn their backs upon all these men and do not walk in their ways, for they feel that they know not whither they are going. They themselves believe that philosophy, with its deliverance and purification, must not be resisted, and so they turn and follow it whithersoever it leads. How do they do this, Socrates? I will tell you, he replied. The lovers of knowledge, said he, perceive that when philosophy first takes possession of their soul it is entirely

greek_head: ὅτι παραλαβοῦσα αὐτῶν τὴν ψυχὴν ἡ φιλοσοφία ἀτεχνῶς διαδεδεμένην ἐν τῷ σώματι καὶ προσκεκολλημένην ἀναγκαζομένην
greek_tokens: ostis paralambanw autos o yuxh o filosofia atexnos diadew en o swma kai proskollaw anagkazw de wsper dia eirgmos dia outos skopaw o eimi alla mh autos dia eautou kai en paomai amaqia kulindew kai o eirgmos o deinoths kateidon ostis dia epiqumia eimi ws an malista autos o dew sullhptwr
english_head: fastened and welded to the body and is compelled to regard realities through the body as through prison bars, not with its own unhindered vision, and is wallowing in utter ignorance. And philosophy sees that the most dreadful thing about the imprisonment is the fact that it is caused by the lusts of the flesh, so that the prisoner is the

greek_head: εἴη τοῦ δεδέσθαι ὅπερ οὖν λέγω γιγνώσκουσιν οἱ φιλομαθεῖς ὅτι οὕτω παραλαβοῦσα ἡ φιλοσοφία ἔχουσαν αὐτῶν
greek_tokens: eaw o dew osper oun legos gignwskw e filomaqew ostis outws paralambanw o filosofia exw autos o yuxh hrema paramuqeomai kai luw epixeirew endeiknumi ostis apath men mestos o dia o omma skeyis apath de o dia o aatos kai o allos aisqhsis peiqw de ek outos men anaxwrew osos mh anagkh autos xraomai autos de eis eautou sullegw kai aqroizw parakeleuomai pisteuw de mhdeis allos alla
english_head: chief assistant in his own imprisonment. The lovers of knowledge, then, I say, perceive that philosophy, taking possession of the soul when it is in this state, encourages it gently and tries to set it free, pointing out that the eyes and the ears and the other senses are full of deceit, and urging it to withdraw from these, except in so far as their use is unavoidable, and exhorting it to collect and concentrate itself within itself, and to trust nothing except

greek_head: ἢ αὐτὴν αὑτῇ ὅτι ἂν νοήσῃ αὐτὴ καθ' αὑτὴν αὐτὸ καθ' αὑτὸ τῶν ὄντων ὅτι δ' ἂν δι' ἄλλων σκοπῇ ἐν ἄλλοις ὂν
greek_tokens: h autos eautou ostis an nohsis autos kaqa eautou autos kaqa eautou o eimi ostis de an dia allos skopaw en allos eimi allos mhdeis hgeomai alhqhs eimi de o men toioutos aisqhtos te kai oratos o de autos oraw nohtos te kai aidhs outos oun o lusis ou oiomai dew enantioomai o o ws alhqhs filosofos yuxh outws apexw o hdona te kai epiqumia kai luph kai fobos kaqa osos dunamai logizomai ostis epeidan tis sfodra hdomai h fobew h lupew h epiqumhsis oudeis tosoutos kakos pasxw apo
english_head: itself and its own abstract thought of abstract existence; and to believe that there is no truth in that which it sees by other means and which varies with the various objects in which it appears, since everything of that kind is visible and apprehended by the senses, whereas the soul itself sees that which is invisible and apprehended by the mind. Now the soul of the true philosopher believes that it must not resist this deliverance, and therefore it stands aloof from pleasures and lusts and griefs and fears, so far as it can, considering that when anyone has violent pleasures or fears or griefs or lusts he suffers from them not merely what one might think—for example, illness or loss of money spent

greek_head: αὐτῶν ὧν ἄν τις οἰηθείη οἷον ἢ νοσήσας ἤ τι ἀναλώσας διὰ τὰς ἐπιθυμίας ἀλλ' ὃ πάντων μέγιστόν τε κακῶν καὶ
greek_tokens: autos os an tis oiomai oios h nosew h tis analiskw dia o epiqumia alla o pas megas te kakh kai esxatos eimi outos pasxw kai ou logizomai autos tis outos eimi swkraths fhmi o kebhs ostis yuxh pas anqrwpos anagkazw ama te hdomai sfodra h lupew epi tis kai hgeomai peri o an malista outos pasxw outos enarghs te eimi kai alhqhs ou outws exw outos de malista o oraths h ou panu ge
english_head: for his lusts—but he suffers the greatest and most extreme evil and does not take it into account. What is this evil, Socrates? said Cebes. The evil is that the soul of every man, when it is greatly pleased or pained by anything, is compelled to believe that the object which caused the emotion is very distinct and very true; but it is not. These objects are mostly the visible ones, are they not?

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἐν τούτῳ τῷ πάθει μάλιστα καταδεῖται ψυχὴ ὑπὸ σώματος
greek_tokens: oukoun en outos o paqos malista katadeomai yuxh upo swma pws dh ostis ekastos hdona kai luph wsper aliskomai exw proshlow autos pros o swma kai prosperonaw kai poiew swmatoeidhs docazw outos alhqhs eimi o an kai o swma fhmi ek gar o omodocew o swma kai o autos xairw anagkazw oimh omotropos te kai omotrofos gignomai kai oios mhdepote eis adhs kaqaros afikneomai alla aei o swma anapleos eceimi wste taxus palin piptw eis
english_head: Certainly. And when this occurs, is not the soul most completely put in bondage by the body? How so? Because each pleasure or pain nails it as with a nail to the body and rivets it on and makes it corporeal, so that it fancies the things are true which the body says are true. For because it has the same beliefs and pleasures as the body it is compelled to adopt also the same habits and mode of life, and can never depart in purity to the other world, but must always go away contaminated with the body; and so it sinks quickly into another body again and grows into it,

greek_head: ἄλλο σῶμα καὶ ὥσπερ σπειρομένη ἐμφύεσθαι καὶ ἐκ τούτων ἄμοιρος εἶναι τῆς τοῦ θείου τε καὶ καθαροῦ καὶ μονοειδοῦς
greek_tokens: allos swma kai wsper speirw emfuw kai ek outos amoiros eimi o o qeios te kai kaqaros kai monoeidhs sunousia alhqhs fhmi legw o kebhs eimi swkraths outos toinun eneka eimi kebhs e dikaios filomaqew kosmios eimi kai andreios ou os e polus eneka fhmi h su oiomai
english_head: like seed that is sown. Therefore it has no part in the communion with the divine and pure and absolute. What you say, Socrates, is very true, said Cebes. This, Cebes, is the reason why the true lovers of knowledge are temperate and brave; not the world’s reason. Or do you disagree?

greek_head: Οὐ δῆτα ἔγωγε Οὐ γάρ ἀλλ' οὕτω λογίσαιτ' ἂν ψυχὴ ἀνδρὸς φιλοσόφου
greek_tokens: ou dhta egw ou gar alla outws logizomai an yuxh anhr filosofos kai ou an oiomai o men filosofia xrh autos luw luw de ekeinos autos paradidwmi o hdona kai luph eautou palin au egkatadew kai anhnutos ergnumi prassw phnelopeia tis enantios istos metaxeirizomai alla galhnh outos paraskeuazw epomai o logismos kai aei en outos eimi o alhqhs kai o qew kai o adocastos
english_head: Certainly not. No, for the soul of the philosopher would not reason as others do, and would not think it right that philosophy should set it free, and that then when set free it should give itself again into bondage to pleasure and pain and engage in futile toil, like Penelope unweaving the web she wove. No, his soul believes that it must gain peace from these emotions, must follow reason and abide always in it, beholding that which is true and divine and not a matter of opinion, and making that its only food;

greek_head: θεωμένη καὶ ὑπ' ἐκείνου τρεφομένη ζῆν τε οἴεται οὕτω δεῖν ἕως ἂν ζῇ καὶ ἐπειδὰν τελευτήσῃ εἰς τὸ συγγενὲς
greek_tokens: qeaomai kai upo ekeinos trefw zaw te oiomai outws dew ews an zaw kai epeidan teleutaw eis o suggenhs kai eis o toioutos afikneomai apallassw o anqrwpinos kakh ek dh o toioutos trefw oudeis deinos mh fobew outos de epithdeuw eimi simmias te kai kebhs opws mh diaspaw en o apallassw o swma upo o anemos diafusaw kai diapetamai oixomai kai oudeis eti oudamos eimi
english_head: and in this way it believes it must live, while life endures, and then at death pass on to that which is akin to itself and of like nature, and be free from human ills. A soul which has been nurtured in this way, Simmias and Cebes, is not likely to fear that it will be torn asunder at its departure from the body and will vanish into nothingness, blown apart by the winds, and be no longer anywhere. When Socrates had said this there was silence

greek_head: Σιγὴ οὖν ἐγένετο ταῦτα εἰπόντος τοῦ Σωκράτους ἐπὶ πολὺν χρόνον καὶ αὐτός τε πρὸς τῷ εἰρημένῳ λόγῳ ἦν ὁ
greek_tokens: sigh oun gignomai outos eipon o swkraths epi polus xronos kai autos te pros o erw logos eimi o swkratew ws eidon fainw kai egw e pleistos kebhs de kai simmias mikros pros allhlwn dialegw kai o swkratew eidon autos airw tis fhmi su o legw mhs mh dokew endehs legw polus gar dh eti exis upoyia kai antilabh ei ge dh tis autos mellw ikanos dieceimi ei men oun tis allos skopaw oudeis legos ei de tis peri outos aforaw mhdeis apoknew kai autos eipon kai
english_head: for a long time, and Socrates himself was apparently absorbed in what had been said, as were also most of us. But Simmias and Cebes conversed a little with each other; and Socrates saw them and said: Do you think there is any incompleteness in what has been said? There are still many subjects for doubt and many points open to attack, if anyone cares to discuss the matter thoroughly. If you are considering anything else, I have nothing to say; but if you are in any difficulty about these matters, do not hesitate

greek_head: διελθεῖν εἴ πῃ ὑμῖν φαίνεται βέλτιον ἂν λεχθῆναι καὶ αὖ καὶ ἐμὲ συμπαραλαβεῖν εἴ τι μᾶλλον οἴεσθε μετ' ἐμοῦ
greek_tokens: dierxomai ei ph su fainw beltiwn an legw kai au kai egw sumparalambanw ei tis mallon oiomai meta egw euporew kai o simmias fhmi kai meis eimi swkraths alhqhs su eraomai pala gar egw ekateros aforaw o eteros prowqew kai keleuw eromai dia o epiqumew men akew oknew de oxlos parexw mh su ahdhs eimi dia o pareimi sumfora kai os aekwn gelaw te hrema kai fhmi babai eimi simmias eimi pou xalepos an o allos anqrwpos peiqw
english_head: to speak and discuss them yourselves, if you think anything better could be said on the subject, and to take me along with you in the discussion, if you think you can get on better in my company. And Simmias said: Socrates, I will tell you the truth. For some time each of us has been in doubt and has been egging the other on and urging him to ask a question, because we wish to hear your answer, but hesitate to trouble you, for fear that it may be disagreeable to you in your present misfortune. And when he heard is, he laughed gently and said: Ah,

greek_head: ὡς οὐ συμφορὰν ἡγοῦμαι τὴν παροῦσαν τύχην ὅτε γε μηδ' ὑμᾶς δύναμαι πείθειν ἀλλὰ φοβεῖσθε μὴ δυσκολώτερόν
greek_tokens: ws ou sumfora hgeomai o pareimi tuxh oste ge mhde su dunamai peiqw alla fobew mh duskolos tis nun diakeimai h en o prosqen bios kai ws eoika o kuknos dokazw faulos su eimi o mantikos e epeidan aisqanomai ostis deomai autos apoqnhskw aeidw kai en
english_head: Simmias! I should have hard work to persuade other people that I do not regard my present situation as a misfortune, when I cannot even make you believe it, but you are afraid I am more churlish now than I used to be. And you seem to think I am inferior in prophetic power to the swans who sing at other times also, but when they feel that they are to die,

greek_head: τῷ πρόσθεν χρόνῳ τότε δὴ πλεῖστα καὶ κάλλιστα ᾄδουσι γεγηθότες ὅτι μέλλουσι παρὰ τὸν θεὸν ἀπιέναι οὗπέρ εἰσι
greek_tokens: o prosqen xronos tote dh pleistos kai kallista aeidw ghqew ostis mellw para o qeos apeimi ouper eimi qerapwn e de anqrwpos dia o eautou deos o qanatos kai o kuknos katayeudomai kai fhmi autos qrhnew o qanatos upo luph ecadw kai ou logizomai ostis oudeis orneon aeidw otan peinaw h rigow h tis allos luph lupew oude autos o te ahdwn kai xelidwn kai o epoy o dh fhmi dia luph qrhnew aeidw alla oute outos egw fainw
english_head: sing most and best in their joy that they are to go to the god whose servants they are. But men, because of their own fear of death, misrepresent the swans and say that they sing for sorrow, in mourning for their own death. They do not consider that no bird sings when it is hungry or cold or has any other trouble; no, not even the nightingale or the swallow or the hoopoe which are said to sing in lamentation. I do not believe they sing for grief, nor do the swans;

greek_head: λυπούμενα ᾄδειν οὔτε οἱ κύκνοι ἀλλ' ἅτε οἶμαι τοῦ Ἀπόλλωνος ὄντες μαντικοί τέ εἰσι καὶ προειδότες τὰ ἐν Ἅιδου
greek_tokens: lupew aeidw oute e kuknos alla ate oimh o apollwn eimi mantikos su eimi kai prooida o en adhs agaqos aeidw kai terpw ekeinos o hmeros diaferontws h en o emprosqen xronos egw de kai autos hgeomai omodoulos te eimi o kuknos kai ieros o autos qeaomai kai ou xeirwn ekeinos o mantikos exw para o despoths oude dusqumos autos o bios apallassw alla outos ge eneka legw te xrh kai erwtaw ostis an boulomai ews an aqhnaia eaw anhr endeka kalos fhmi legw o simmias kai egw su su eraomai o
english_head: but since they are Apollo’s birds, I believe they have prophetic vision, and because they have foreknowledge of the blessings in the other world they sing and rejoice on that day more than ever before. And I think that I am myself a fellow-servant of the swans; and am consecrated to the same God and have received from our master a gift of prophecy no whit inferior to theirs, and that I go out from life with as little sorrow as they. So far as this is concerned, then, speak and ask what ever questions you please, so long as the eleven of the Athenians permit. Good, said Simmias.

greek_head: ἀπορῶ καὶ αὖ ὅδε ᾗ οὐκ ἀποδέχεται τὰ εἰρημένα ἐμοὶ γὰρ δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες περὶ τῶν τοιούτων ἴσως ὥσπερ καὶ
greek_tokens: aforaw kai au ode h ou apodexomai o erw egw gar dokew eimi swkraths peri o toioutos isos wsper kai sos o men safhs oida en o nun bios h adunatos eimi h pagxalepos tis o mentoi au o legw peri autos mh ou pas tropos elegxw kai mh proafistamai prin an pantaxh skopaw apeipon tis panu malqakos eimi anhr dew gar peri autos eis ge tis outos diaprassw h manqanw oph exis h euriskw h ei outos adunatos o goun beltistos o anqrwpinos logos lambanw kai dusecelegktos
english_head: I will tell you my difficulty, and then Cebes in turn will say why he does not agree to all you have said. I think, Socrates, as perhaps you do yourself, that it is either impossible or very difficult to acquire clear knowledge about these matters in this life. And yet he is a weakling who does not test in every way what is said about them and persevere until he is worn out by studying them on every side. For he must do one of two things; either he must learn or discover the truth about these matters, or if that is impossible, he must take whatever human doctrine is best

greek_head: ἐπὶ τούτου ὀχούμενον ὥσπερ ἐπὶ σχεδίας κινδυνεύοντα διαπλεῦσαι τὸν βίον εἰ μή τις δύναιτο ἀσφαλέστερον καὶ
greek_tokens: epi outos oxaomai wsper epi sxedios kinduneuw diaplew o bios ei mh tis dunamai asfalhs kai akindunos epi bebaios oxhma h logos qeios tis diaporeuw kai dh kai nun egw ou epaisxunomai eromai epei kai su outos legw oude emautou aitiaomai en usteron xronos ostis nun ou eipon o egw dokew egw gar eimi swkraths epei kai pros emautou kai pros ode skopaw o erw ou panu fainw ikanos erw
english_head: and hardest to disprove and, embarking upon it as upon a raft, sail upon it through life in the midst of dangers, unless he can sail upon some stronger vessel, some divine revelation, and make his voyage more safely and securely. And so now I am not ashamed to ask questions, since you encourage me to do so, and I shall not have to blame myself hereafter for not saying now what I think. For, Socrates, when I examine what has been said, either alone or with Cebes, it does not seem quite satisfactory.

greek_head: Καὶ ὁ Σωκράτης Ἴσως γάρ ἔφη ὦ ἑταῖρε ἀληθῆ σοι φαίνεται ἀλλὰ λέγε ὅπῃ δὴ οὐχ ἱκανῶς
greek_tokens: kai o swkratew isos gar fhmi eimi etairos alhqhs su fainw alla legos oph dh ou ikanos outos egw eimi de os h dh kai peri armonia an tis kai lura te kai xordh o autos outos logos eipon ws o men armonia aoratos kai aswmatos kai pagkalos tis kai
english_head: And Socrates replied: Perhaps, my friend, you are right. But tell me in what respect it is not satisfactory. In this, said he, that one might use the same argument about harmony and a lyre with its strings.One might say that the harmony is invisible and incorporeal, and very beautiful and

greek_head: θεῖόν ἐστιν ἐν τῇ ἡρμοσμένῃ λύρᾳ αὐτὴ δ' ἡ λύρα καὶ αἱ χορδαὶ σώματά τε καὶ σωματοειδῆ καὶ σύνθετα καὶ
greek_tokens: qew eimi en o armozw lura autos de o lura kai o xordh swma te kai swmatoeidhs kai sunqetos kai gewdhs eimi kai o qnhtos suggeneus epeidan oun h katacis tis o lura h diatemnw kai diarrhcis o xordh ei tis diisxurizomai o autos logos wsper su ws anagkh eti eimi o armonia ekeinos kai mh apollumi oudeis gar mhxanh an eaw o men lura eti eimi diarrhgnumi o xordh kai o xordh qnhtoeidhs eimi o de armonia
english_head: divine in the well attuned lyre, but the lyre itself and its strings are bodies, and corporeal and composite and earthy and akin to that which is mortal. Now if someone shatters the lyre or cuts and breaks the strings, what if he should maintain by the same argument you employed, that the harmony could not have perished and must still exist? For there would be no possibility that the lyre and its strings, which are of mortal nature, still exist after the strings are broken, and the harmony,

greek_head: ἀπολωλέναι τὴν τοῦ θείου τε καὶ ἀθανάτου ὁμοφυῆ τε καὶ συγγενῆ προτέραν τοῦ θνητοῦ ἀπολομένην ἀλλὰ φαίη
greek_tokens: apollumi o o qeios te kai aqanatos omofuhs te kai suggeneus proteros o qnhtos apollumi alla fhmi anagkh eti pou eimi autos o armonia kai proteros o culon kai o xordh katashpw prin tis ekeinos pasxw kai gar oun eimi swkraths oimh egw kai autos su outos enqumeomai ostis toioutos tis malista upolambanw o yuxh eimi wsper enteinw o swma egw kai sunoxwka upo qermos kai yuxroomai kai chros kai ugros kai toioutos tis krasis eimi kai armonia
english_head: which is related and akin to the divine and the immortal, perish before that which is mortal. He would say that the harmony must still exist somewhere, and that the wood and the strings must rot away before anything could happen to it. And I fancy, Socrates, that it must have occurred to your own mind that we believe the soul to be something after this fashion; that our body is strung and held together by heat, cold, moisture, dryness, and the like,

greek_head: αὐτῶν τούτων τὴν ψυχὴν ἡμῶν ἐπειδὰν ταῦτα καλῶς καὶ μετρίως κραθῇ πρὸς ἄλληλα εἰ οὖν τυγχάνει ἡ ψυχὴ οὖσα
greek_tokens: autos outos o yuxh egw epeidan outos kalos kai metrios kerannumi pros allhlwn ei oun tugxanw o yuxh eimi armonia tis dhlos ostis otan xalaw o swma egw ametros h epiteinw upo nosos kai allos kakh o men yuxh anagkh euqus uparxw apollumi kaiper eimi qeios wsper kai o allos armonia o su en o fqoggos kai en o o dhmiourgew ergon pas o de leiyanon o swma ekastos polus xronos paramenw
english_head: and the soul is a mixture and a harmony of these same elements, when they are well and properly mixed. Now if the soul is a harmony, it is clear that when the body is too much relaxed or is too tightly strung by diseases or other ills, the soul must of necessity perish, no matter how divine it is, like other harmonies in sounds and in all the works of artists, and the remains of each body will endure

greek_head: ἕως ἂν ἢ κατακαυθῇ ἢ κατασαπῇ ὅρα οὖν πρὸς τοῦτον τὸν λόγον τί φήσομεν ἐάν τις ἀξιοῖ κρᾶσιν οὖσαν τὴν ψυχὴν
greek_tokens: ews an h katakaiw h katashpw oraw oun pros outos o logos tis fhmi ean tis aciow krasis eimi o yuxh o en o swma en o kalew qanatos proteros apollumi diablepw oun o swkratew wsper o polus eqw kai meidiaw dikaios mentoi fhmi legw o simmias ei oun tis su euporos egw tis ou apokrinw kai gar ou faulos eoika aptw o logos dokew mentoi egw xrh pro o apokrisls eti proteros kebhtos akew
english_head: a long time until they are burnt or decayed. Now what shall we say to this argument, if anyone claims that the soul, being a mixture of the elements of the body, is the first to perish in what is called death? Then Socrates, looking keenly at us, as he often used to do, smiled and said: Simmias raises a fair objection. Now if any of you is readier than I, why does he not reply to him? For he seems to score a good point. However, I think

greek_head: τί αὖ ὅδε ἐγκαλεῖ τῷ λόγῳ ἵνα χρόνου ἐγγενομένου βουλευσώμεθα τί ἐροῦμεν ἔπειτα δὲ ἀκούσαντας ἢ συγχωρεῖν
greek_tokens: tis au ode egkalew o logos ina xronos eggignomai bouleuw tis erew epeita de akouw h sugxwrew autos ean tis dokazw prosadw ean de mh outws hdh uperdikew o logos alla age eimi de os eimi kebhs legos tis eimi o sos au qratton apistia paraxew legos dh eimi de os o kebhs egw gar fainw eti en o autos o logos eimi kai osper en o prosqen legw
english_head: before replying to him we ought to hear what fault our friend Cebes finds with our argument, that we may take time to consider what to say, and then when we have heard them, we can either agree with them, if they seem to strike the proper note, or, if they do not, we can proceed to argue in defence of our reasoning. Come, Cebes, said he, tell us what it was that troubled you. Well, I will tell you, said Cebes.

greek_head: ταὐτὸν ἔγκλημα ἔχειν ὅτι μὲν γὰρ ἦν ἡμῶν ἡ ψυχὴ καὶ πρὶν εἰς τόδε τὸ εἶδος ἐλθεῖν οὐκ ἀνατίθεμαι μὴ οὐχὶ πάνυ
greek_tokens: tautos egklhma exw ostis men gar eimi egw o yuxh kai prin eis ode o eidos erxomai ou anatiqhmi mh ou panu xarieis kai ei mh epaxqhs eimi eipon panu ikanos apodeiknumi ws de kai apoqnhskw egw eti pou eimi ou egw dokew ode ws men ou isxuros kai poluxronios yuxh swma ou sugxwrew o simmias antilhyis dokew gar egw pas outos panu polus diaferw tis oun an fhmi o logos eti apistew epei oraw apoqnhskw o anqrwpos o ge asqenhs eti eimi o de poluxronios
english_head: The argument seems to me to be just where it was, and to be still open to the objection I made before. For I do not deny that it has been very cleverly, and, if I may say so, conclusively shown that the soul existed before it entered into this bodily form, but it does not seem to me proved that it will still exist when we are dead. I do not agree with Simmias’ objection, that the soul is not stronger and more lasting than the body, for I think it is far superior in all such respects. Why then, the argument might say, do you still disbelieve, when you see that after a man dies

greek_head: οὐ δοκεῖ σοι ἀναγκαῖον εἶναι ἔτι σῴζεσθαι ἐν τούτῳ τῷ χρόνῳ πρὸς δὴ τοῦτο τόδε ἐπίσκεψαι εἴ τι λέγω
greek_tokens: ou dokew su anagkaion eimi eti swzw en outos o xronos pros dh outos ode episkeptomai ei tis legos eikwn gar tis ws eoika agos wsper simmias deomai egw gar dokew omoios legw outos wsper an tis peri anqrwpos ufanths presbuths apoqnhskw legos outos o logos ostis ou apollumi o anqrwpos alla eimi pou sos tekmhrion de parexw qoimation o ampexw autos ufainw ostis eimi sew kai ou apollumi kai ei tis
english_head: the weaker part still exists? Do you not think the stronger part must necessarily be preserved during the same length of time? Now see if my reply to this has any sense. I think I may, like Simmias, best express myself in a figure. It seems to me that it is much as if one should say about an old weaver who had died, that the man had not perished but was safe and sound somewhere, and should offer as a proof of this the fact that the cloak which the man had woven and used to wear was still whole and had not perished. Then if anyone did not believe him, he would ask

greek_head: ἀπιστοίη αὐτῷ ἀνερωτῴη πότερον πολυχρονιώτερόν ἐστι τὸ γένος ἀνθρώπου ἢ ἱματίου ἐν χρείᾳ τε ὄντος καὶ φορουμένου
greek_tokens: apistew autos anerwtaw poteros poluxronios eimi o genos anqrwpos h imation en xreia te eimi kai forew apokrinw dh tis ostis polus o o anqrwpos oiomai apodeiknumi ostis pas ara mallon o ge anqrwpos sos eimi epei o ge oligoxronios ou apollumi o de oimh eimi simmias ou outws exis skopaw gar kai su o legos pas gar an upolambanw ostis euhqhs legw o outos legos o gar ufanths outos polus katatribw toioutos imation kai ufainw ekeinos men usteros apollumi polus
english_head: which lasts longer, a man or a cloak that is in use and wear, and when the answer was given that a man lasts much longer, he would think it had been proved beyond a doubt that the man was safe, because that which was less lasting had not perished. But I do not think he is right, Simmias, and I ask you especially to notice what I say. Anyone can understand that a man who says this is talking nonsense. For the weaver in question wove and wore out many such cloaks and

greek_head: ὄντων τοῦ δὲ τελευταίου οἶμαι πρότερος καὶ οὐδέν τι μᾶλλον τούτου ἕνεκα ἄνθρωπός ἐστιν ἱματίου φαυλότερον
greek_tokens: eimi o de teleutaios oimh proteros kai oudeis tis mallon outos eneka anqrwpos eimi imation faulos oude asqenhs o autos de outos oimh eikwn dexomai an yuxh pros swma kai tis legos autos outos peri autos metrios an egw fainw legw ws o men yuxh poluxronios eimi o de swma asqenhs kai oligoxronios alla gar an fhmi ekastos o yuxos polus swma katatribw allos te kan polus eths biaw ei gar rew o swma kai apollumi eti zaw o anqrwpos
english_head: lasted longer than they, though they were many, but perished, I suppose, before the last one. Yet a man is not feebler or weaker than a cloak on that account at all. And I think the same figure would apply to the soul and the body and it would be quite appropriate to say in like manner about them, that the soul lasts a long time, but the body lasts a shorter time and is weaker. And, one might go on to say that each soul wears out many bodies, especially if the man lives many years. For if the body is constantly changing and being destroyed while the man still lives,

greek_head: ἀλλ' ἡ ψυχὴ ἀεὶ τὸ κατατριβόμενον ἀνυφαίνοι ἀναγκαῖον μεντἂν εἴη ὁπότε ἀπολλύοιτο ἡ ψυχή τὸ τελευταῖον ὕφασμα
greek_tokens: alla o yuxh aei o katatribw anufainw anagkaion mentoi eaw opote apollumi o yuxh o teleutaios ufasma tugxanw autos exw kai outos monos proteros apollumi apollumi de o yuxazw tote hdh o fus o asqeneia epideiknumi o swma kai taxus shpw dioixomai wste outos o logos oupw acios pisteuw qarsew ws
english_head: and the soul is always weaving anew that which wears out, then when the soul perishes it must necessarily have on its last garment, and this only will survive it, and when the soul has perished, then the body will at once show its natural weakness and will quickly disappear in decay. And so we are not yet justified in feeling sure, on the strength of this argument,

greek_head: ἐπειδὰν ἀποθάνωμεν ἔτι που ἡμῶν ἡ ψυχὴ ἔστιν εἰ γάρ τις καὶ πλέον ἔτι τῷ λέγοντι ἢ ἃ σὺ λέγεις συγχωρήσειεν
greek_tokens: epeidan apoqnhskw eti pou egw o yuxh eimi ei gar tis kai pleos eti o legw h o su legw sugxwrew didwmi autos mh monos en o prin kai gignomai egw xronos eimi egw o yuxh alla mhdeis kwluw kai epeidan apoqnhskw enioi eti eimi kai eimi kai pollakis gignomai kai apoqnhskw auqis outws gar autos fusis isxuros eimi wste pollakis gignomai yuxh antexw didwmi de outos ekeinos mhketi sugxwrew mh ou ponew autos en o polus genesis kai teleutaw ge en tis o qanatos pantapasi apollumi outos de o
english_head: that our souls will still exist somewhere after we are dead. For if one were to grant even more to a man who uses your argument, Socrates, and allow not only that our souls existed before we were born, but also that there is nothing to prevent some of them from continuing to exist and from being born and dying again many times after we are dead, because the soul is naturally so strong that it can endure repeated births,—even allowing this, one might not grant that it does not suffer by its many births and does not finally perish altogether in one of its deaths.

greek_head: θάνατον καὶ ταύτην τὴν διάλυσιν τοῦ σώματος ἣ τῇ ψυχῇ φέρει ὄλεθρον μηδένα φαίη εἰδέναι ἀδύνατον γὰρ εἶναι
greek_tokens: qanatos kai outos o dialusis o swma o o yuxw ferw oleqros mhdeis fhmi oida adunatos gar eimi ostis aisqanomai egw ei de outos outws exis oudeis proshkw qanatos qarsew mh ou anohtos qarsew os an mh exis apodeiknumi ostis eimi yuxh pantapasi aqanatos te kai anwleqros ei de mh anagkh eimi aei o mellw apoqnhskw deidw uper o eautou yuxazw mh en o nun o swma diazeucis pantapasi apollumi
english_head: But he might say that no one knows beforehand the particular death and the particular dissolution of the body which brings destruction to the soul, for none of us can perceive that. Now if this is the case, anyone who feels confident about death has a foolish confidence, unless he can show that the soul is altogether immortal and imperishable. Otherwise a man who is about to die must always fear that his soul will perish utterly in the impending dissolution of the body. Now all of us, as we remarked to one another afterwards,

greek_head: Πάντες οὖν ἀκούσαντες εἰπόντων αὐτῶν ἀηδῶς διετέθημεν ὡς ὕστερον ἐλέγομεν πρὸς ἀλλήλους ὅτι ὑπὸ τοῦ
greek_tokens: pas oun akouw eipon autos ahdhs diatiqhmi ws usteron legw pros allhlwn ostis upo o emprosqen logos sfodra peiqw egw palin dokew anatarassw kai eis apistia kataballw ou monos o proerew logos alla kai eis o usteron mellw erw mh oudeis acios eimi kriths h kai o pragma autos apistos eimi nh o qeos eimi faidwn suggnwmh ge exw su kai gar autos egw nun akouw su toioutos tis legw
english_head: were very uncomfortable when we heard what they said; for we had been thoroughly convinced by the previous argument, and now they seemed to be throwing us again into confusion and distrust, not only in respect to the past discussion but also with regard to any future one. They made us fear that our judgment was worthless or that no certainty could be attained in these matters. By the gods, Phaedo, I sympathize with you; for I myself after listening to you am inclined to ask myself:

greek_head: πρὸς ἐμαυτὸν ἐπέρχεται Τίνι οὖν ἔτι πιστεύσομεν λόγῳ ὡς γὰρ σφόδρα πιθανὸς ὤν ὃν ὁ Σωκράτης ἔλεγε λόγον
greek_tokens: pros emautou eperxomai tis oun eti pisteuw logos ws gar sfodra piqanos eimi os o swkratew elegos logos nun eis apistia katapiptw qaumastos gar egw o logos outos antilambanw kai nun kai aei o armonia tis egw eimi o yuxh kai wsper upomimnhskw egw erw ostis kai autos egw outos prodokew kai panu deomai palin wsper ek arxh allos tis logos os egw pasxw ws o apoqnhskw ou sunapoqnhskw o yuxh legos oun pros zeus ph o swkratew meterxomai o logos kai poteros
english_head: What argument shall we believe henceforth? For the argument of Socrates was perfectly convincing, and now it has fallen into discredit. For the doctrine that the soul is a kind of harmony has always had (and has now) a wonderful hold upon me, and your mention of it reminded me that I had myself believed in it before. Now I must begin over again and find another argument to convince me that when a man dies his soul does not perish with him. So, for heaven’s sake, tell how Socrates

greek_head: κἀκεῖνος ὥσπερ ὑμᾶς φῄς ἔνδηλός τι ἐγένετο ἀχθόμενος ἢ οὔ ἀλλὰ πρᾴως ἐβοήθει τῷ λόγῳ ἢ καὶ ἱκανῶς ἐβοήθησεν
greek_tokens: ekeinos wsper su fhmi endhlos tis gignomai axqomai h ou alla praos bohqew o logos h kai ikanos bohqew h endehs pas egw dierxomai ws dunamai akribhs kai meis eimi exekraths pollakis qaumazw swkratew ou pwpote mallon agamai h tote paragignomai
english_head: continued the discourse, and whether he also, as you say the rest of you did, showed any uneasiness, or calmly defended his argument. And did he defend it successfully? Tell us everything as accurately as you can. Echecrates, I have often wondered at Socrates, but never did I admire him more

greek_head: τὸ μὲν οὖν ἔχειν ὅτι λέγοι ἐκεῖνος ἴσως οὐδὲν ἄτοπον ἀλλὰ ἔγωγε μάλιστα ἐθαύμασα αὐτοῦ πρῶτον μὲν τοῦτο ὡς ἡδέως
greek_tokens: o men oun exw ostis legos ekeinos isos oudeis atopos alla egw malista qaumazw autos proteros men outos ws hdews kai eumenhs kai agamai o neaniskos o logos apodexomai epeita egw ws ocews aisqanomai o pasxw upo o logos epeita ws eu egw iaomai kai wsper feugw kai hssaomai anakalew kai protrepw pros o parepomai te kai suskopew o logos pws dh egw eraomai tugxanw gar en decia autos kaqhmai
english_head: than then. That he had an answer ready was perhaps to be expected; but what astonished me more about him was, first, the pleasant, gentle, and respectful manner in which he listened to the young men’s criticisms, secondly, his quick sense of the effect their words had upon us, and lastly, the skill with which he cured us and, as it were, recalled us from our flight and defeat and made us face about and follow him and join in his examination of the argument. How did he do it? I will tell you. I was sitting at his right hand on a low stool

greek_head: παρὰ τὴν κλίνην ἐπὶ χαμαιζήλου τινός ὁ δὲ ἐπὶ πολὺ ὑψηλοτέρου ἢ ἐγώ καταψήσας οὖν μου τὴν κεφαλὴν καὶ
greek_tokens: para o klinh epi xamaizhlos tis o de epi polus uyhlos h egw katayaw oun egw o kefalh kai sumpiezw o epi o auxhn qric eqw gar opote tuxos paizw egw eis o qric aurion dh fhmi isos eimi faidwn o kalos outos komh apokeirw eoika eimi de egw eimi swkraths ou an ge egw peiqw alla tis eimi de egw shmeron fhmi agos o emos kai su outos ean ge egw o logos teleutaw kai mh dunamai autos anabiow
english_head: beside his couch, and his seat was a good deal higher than mine. He stroked my head and gathered the hair on the back of my neck into his hand—he had a habit of playing with my hair on occasion—and said, Tomorrow, perhaps, Phaedo, you will cut off this beautiful hair. I suppose so, Socrates, said I. Not if you take my advice. What shall I do then? I asked. You will cut it off today, and I will cut mine, if our argument dies and we cannot bring it to life again.

greek_head: καὶ ἔγωγ' ἄν εἰ σὺ εἴην καί με διαφεύγοι ὁ λόγος ἔνορκον ἂν ποιησαίμην ὥσπερ Ἀργεῖοι μὴ πρότερον
greek_tokens: kai egw an ei su eimi kai egw diafeugw o logos enorkos an poiew wsper argeios mh proteros komaw prin an nikaw anamaxomai o simmias te kai kebhtos logos alla eimi de egw pros duo legw oude o hraklehs oios te eimi alla kai egw fhmi o ioleus parakalew ews eti faos eimi parakalew toinun fhmi ou ws hraklehs alla ws ioleus o hraklh oudeis diaferw fhmi alla proteros eulabeomai tis paqos mh pasxw o poios eimi de egw
english_head: If I were you and the argument escaped me, I would take an oath, like the Argives, not to let my hair grow until I had renewed the fight and won a victory over the argument of Simmias and Cebes. But, I replied, they say that even Heracles is not a match for two. Well, said he, call me to help you, as your Iolaus, while there is still light. I call you to help, then, said I, not as Heracles calling Iolaus, but as Iolaus calling Heracles. That is all one, said he. But first let us guard against a danger. Of what sort? I asked.

greek_head: Μὴ γενώμεθα ἦ δ' ὅς μισόλογοι ὥσπερ οἱ μισάνθρωποι γιγνόμενοι ὡς οὐκ ἔστιν ἔφη ὅτι ἄν τις μεῖζον τούτου
greek_tokens: mh gignomai eimi de os misologos wsper e misanqrwpos gignomai ws ou eimi fhmi ostis an tis megas outos kakos pasxw h logos misew gignomai de ek o autos tropos misologia te kai misanqrwpia o te gar misanqrwpia enduw ek o sfodra tis pisteuw aneu texnh kai hgeomai pantapasi ge alhqhs eimi kai ugihs kai piston o anqrwpos epeita oligos usteron euriskw outos ponhros te kai apistos kai auqis eteros kai otan outos pollakis paqh tis kai upo outos malista os an hgeomai
english_head: The danger of becoming misologists or haters of argument, said he, as people become misanthropists or haters of man; for no worse evil can happen to a man than to hate argument. Misology and misanthropy arise from similar causes. For misanthropy arises from trusting someone implicitly without sufficient knowledge. You think the man is perfectly true and sound and trustworthy, and afterwards you find him base and false. Then you have the same experience with another person. By the time this has happened to a man a good many times, especially if it happens among those whom he might regard as his nearest

greek_head: οἰκειοτάτους τε καὶ ἑταιροτάτους τελευτῶν δὴ θαμὰ προςκρούων μισεῖ τε πάντας καὶ ἡγεῖται οὐδενὸς οὐδὲν ὑγιὲς
greek_tokens: oikeios te kai etairos teleutaw dh qama proskrouw misew te pas kai hgeomai oudeis oudeis ugihs eimi o parapan h ou aisqanomai su pw outos gignomai panu ge eimi de egw oukoun eimi de os aisxros kai dhlos ostis aneu texnh o peri anqrwpeios o toioutos xraomai epixeirew o anqrwpos ei gar pou meta texnh xraomai wsper exis outws
english_head: and dearest friends, he ends by being in continual quarrels and by hating everybody and thinking there is nothing sound in anyone at all. Have you not noticed this? Certainly, said I. Well, he went on, is it not disgraceful, and is it not plain that such a man undertakes to consort with men when he has no knowledge of human nature? For if he had knowledge when he dealt with them, he would think that the good

greek_head: ἂν ἡγήσατο τοὺς μὲν χρηστοὺς καὶ πονηροὺς σφόδρα ὀλίγους εἶναι ἑκατέρους τοὺς δὲ μεταξὺ πλείστους
greek_tokens: an ageomai o men xrhstos kai ponhros sfodra oligos eimi ekateros o de metacu pleistos pws legw fhmi egw wsper eimi de os peri o sfodra mikros kai megas oiomai tis spanios eimi h sfodra megas h sfodra mikros eceuriskw anqrwpos h kuwn h allos otioun h au taxus h bradus h aisxros h kalos h leukon h melas h ou aisqanomai ostis pas o toioutos o men akra o esxatos spanios kai oligos o de metacu afqonos kai polus panu ge eimi de egw
english_head: and the bad are both very few and those between the two are very many, for that is the case. What do you mean? I mean just what I might say about the large and small. Do you think there is anything more unusual than to find a very large or a very small man, or dog, or other creature, or again, one that is very quick or slow, very ugly or beautiful, very black or white? Have you not noticed that the extremes in all these instances are rare and few, and the examples between the extremes are very many? To be sure, said I. And don’t you think,

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν οἴει ἔφη εἰ πονηρίας ἀγὼν προτεθείη πάνυ ἂν ὀλίγους καὶ ἐνταῦθα τοὺς πρώτους φανῆναι
greek_tokens: oukoun oiomai fhmi ei ponhria agwn protiqhmi panu an oligos kai entauqa o proteros fainw eoika ge eimi de egw eoika gar fhmi alla outos men ou omoios e logos o anqrwpos alla sew nundi proagw egw efepw alla ekeinos h epeidan tis pisteusis logos tis alhqhs eimi aneu o peri o logos texnh epeita oligos usteron autos doca yeudhs eimi eniote men eimi eniote de ou eimi kai auqis eteros kai eteros kai malista dh e
english_head: said he, that if there were to be a competition in rascality, those who excelled would be very few in that also? Very likely, I replied. Yes, very likely, he said, But it is not in that respect that arguments are like men; I was merely following your lead in discussing that. The similarity lies in this: when a man without proper knowledge concerning arguments has confidence in the truth of an argument and afterwards thinks that it is false, whether it really is so or not, and this happens again and again; then you know, those men especially who

greek_head: περὶ τοὺς ἀντιλογικοὺς λόγους διατρίψαντες οἶσθ' ὅτι τελευτῶντες οἴονται σοφώτατοι γεγονέναι καὶ κατανενοηκέναι
greek_tokens: peri o antilogikos logos diatribw oistos ostis teleutaw oiomai sofos gignomai kai katanoew monos ostis oute o pragma oudeis oudeis ugihs oude bebaios oute o logos alla pas o eimi atexnos wsper en euripos anoos katos strefw kai xronos oudeis en oudeis menos panu men oun fhmi egw alhqhs legw oukoun eimi faidwn fhmi oiktros an eaw o paqos ei eimi dh tis alhqhs kai bebaios logos kai dunatew
english_head: have spent their time in disputation come to believe that they are the wisest of men and that they alone have discovered that there is nothing sound or sure in anything, whether argument or anything else, but all things go up and down, like the tide in the Euripus, and nothing is stable for any length of time. Certainly, I said, that is very true. Then, Phaedo, he said, if there is any system of argument which is true and sure and can be learned, it would be a sad thing if a man,

greek_head: κατανοῆσαι ἔπειτα διὰ τὸ παραγίγνεσθαι τοιούτοις τισὶ λόγοις τοῖς αὐτοῖς τοτὲ μὲν δοκοῦσιν ἀληθέσιν εἶναι τοτὲ
greek_tokens: katanoew epeita dia o paragignomai toioutos tis logos o autos tote men dokew alhqhs eimi tote de mh mh eautou tis aitiaomai mhde o eautou atexnia alla teleutaw dia o algew asmenos epi o logos apo eautou o aitios apwqew kai hdh o loipos bios misos te kai loidorew o logos diatelew o de eimi o alhqeia te kai episthmh sterew nh o dios eimi de egw oiktros dhta proteros men toinun fhmi outos eulabeomai kai mh
english_head: because he has met with some of those arguments which seem to be sometimes true and sometimes false, should then not blame himself or his own lack of skill, but should end, in his vexation, by throwing the blame gladly upon the arguments and should hate and revile them all the rest of his life, and be deprived of the truth and knowledge of reality. Yes, by Zeus, I said, it would be sad. First, then, said he, let us be on our guard against this,

greek_head: παρίωμεν εἰς τὴν ψυχὴν ὡς τῶν λόγων κινδυνεύει οὐδὲν ὑγιὲς εἶναι ἀλλὰ πολὺ μᾶλλον ὅτι ἡμεῖς οὔπω ὑγιῶς ἔχομεν
greek_tokens: pareimi eis o yuxh ws o logos kinduneuw oudeis ugihs eimi alla polus mallon ostis egw oupw ugihs exw alla andristeon kai proqumhteon ugihs exw sos men oun kai o allos kai o epeita bios pas eneka egw de
english_head: and let us not admit into our souls the notion that there is no soundness in arguments at all. Let us far rather assume that we ourselves are not yet in sound condition and that we must strive manfully and eagerly to become so, you and the others for the sake of all your future life,

greek_head: αὐτοῦ ἕνεκα τοῦ θανάτου ὡς κινδυνεύω ἔγωγε ἐν τῷ παρόντι περὶ αὐτοῦ τούτου οὐ φιλοσόφως ἔχειν ἀλλ' ὥσπερ οἱ πάνυ
greek_tokens: autos eneka o qanatos ws kinduneuw egw en o pareimi peri autos outos ou filosofos exw alla wsper e panu apaideutos filoneikos kai gar ekeinos otan peri tis amfisbhtew oph men exis peri os an o logos eimi ou frontizw opws de o autos tiqhmi outos docis o pareimi outos proqumeomai kai egw egw dokazw en o pareimi tosoutos monos ekeinos diaferw ou gar opws o pareimi o egw legos docis alhqhs eimi proqumeomai ei mh eaw parergon alla opws autos egw ostis malista docis outws
english_head: and I because of my impending death; for I fear that I am not just now in a philosophical frame of mind as regards this particular question, but am contentious, like quite uncultured persons. For when they argue about anything, they do not care what the truth is in the matters they are discussing, but are eager only to make their own views seem true to their hearers. And I fancy I differ from them just now only to this extent: I shall not be eager to make what I say seem true to my hearers, except as a secondary matter, but shall be very eager

greek_head: ἔχειν λογίζομαι γάρ ὦ φίλε ἑταῖρε θέασαι ὡς πλεονεκτικῶς εἰ μὲν τυγχάνει ἀληθῆ ὄντα ἃ λέγω καλῶς δὴ
greek_tokens: exw logizomai gar eimi filos etairos qeaomai ws pleonektikos ei men tugxanw alhqhs eimi o legos kalos dh exis o peiqw ei de mhdeis eimi teleutaw alla oun outos ge o xronos autos o pro o qanatos hsswn o pareimi ahdhs eimi oduromai o de anoia egw outos ou sundiatelew kakos gar an eimi alla oligos usteron apollumi paraskeuazw dh fhmi eimi simmias te kai kebhs outwsi erxomai epi o logos su mentoi an egw peiqw
english_head: to make myself believe it. For see, my friend, how selfish my attitude is. If what I say is true, I am the gainer by believing it; and if there be nothing for me after death, at any rate I shall not be burdensome to my friends by my lamentations in these last moments. And this ignorance of mine will not last, for that would be an evil, but will soon end. So, he said, Simmias and Cebes, I approach the argument with my mind thus prepared. But you,

greek_head: σμικρὸν φροντίσαντες Σωκράτους τῆς δὲ ἀληθείας πολὺ μᾶλλον ἐὰν μέν τι ὑμῖν δοκῶ ἀληθὲς λέγειν συνομολογήσατε
greek_tokens: mikros frontizw swkraths o de alhqeia polus mallon ean men tis su dokazw alhqhs legw sunomologew ei de mh pas logos antiteinw eulabeomai opws mh egw upo proqumia ama emautou te kai su ecapataw wsper melissa o kentron egkataleipw oixomai alla iteon fhmi proteros egw upomimnhskw o legw ean mh fainw mimnhskw simmias men gar ws oimh apistew te kai fobew mh o yuxh omws kai qeios kai
english_head: if you do as I ask, will give little thought to Socrates and much more to the truth; and if you think what I say is true, agree to it, and if not, oppose me with every argument you can muster, that I may not in my eagerness deceive myself and you alike and go away, like a bee, leaving my sting sticking in you. But we must get to work, he said. First refresh my memory, if I seem to have forgotten anything. Simmias, I think, has doubts and fears that the soul, though more divine and

greek_head: κάλλιον ὂν τοῦ σώματος προαπολλύηται ἐν ἁρμονίας εἴδει οὖσα Κέβης δέ μοι ἔδοξε τοῦτο μὲν ἐμοὶ συγχωρεῖν
greek_tokens: kallion eimi o swma proapollumai en armonia eidomai eimi kebhs de egw dokew outos men egw sugxwrew poluxronios ge eimi yuxh swma alla ode adhlos pas mh polus dh swma kai pollakis katatribw o yuxh o teleutaios swma katalimpanw nun autos apollumi kai eimi autos outos qanatos yuxazw oleqros epei swma ge aei apollumi oudeis pauw ara allos h outos eimi eimi simmias te kai kebhs o deomai egw episkopew
english_head: excellent than the body, may perish first, being of the nature of a harmony. And, Cebes, I believe, granted that the soul is more lasting than the body, but said that no one could know that the soul, after wearing out many bodies, did not at last perish itself upon leaving the body; and that this was death—the destruction of the soul, since the body is continually being destroyed. Are those the points, Simmias and Cebes, which we must consider?

greek_head: Συνωμολογείτην δὴ ταῦτ' εἶναι ἄμφω Πότερον οὖν ἔφη πάντας τοὺς ἔμπροσθε λόγους οὐκ
greek_tokens: sunomologew dh outos eimi amfw poteros oun fhmi pas o emprosqen logos ou apodexomai h o men o de ou o men fhmi o de ou tis oun eimi de os peri ekeinos o logos legw en os fhmi o maqhsis anamnhsis eimi kai outos outws exw anagkaios exw alloqi proteros egw eimi o
english_head: They both agreed that these were the points. Now, said he, do you reject all of our previous arguments, or only some of them? Only some of them, they replied. What you think, he asked, about the argument in which we said that learning is recollection and that, since this is so, our soul must necessarily have been somewhere

greek_head: ψυχήν πρὶν ἐν τῷ σώματι ἐνδεθῆναι Ἐγὼ μέν ἔφη ὁ Κέβης καὶ τότε θαυμαστῶς ὡς ἐπείσθην
greek_tokens: yuxh prin en o swma endew egw men fhmi o kebhs kai tote qaumastos ws efezomai upo autos kai nun emmenw ws oudeis logos kai meis fhmi o simmias kai autos outws exw kai panu an qaumazw ei egw peri ge outos allos pote tis dokew kai o swkratew alla anagkh su fhmi eimi cenos qhbaios allos doca ean menw ode o oihsis o armonia men eimi sunqetos pragma yuxh de armonia tis ek o kata o swma enteinw sugkeimai ou gar pou apodecis ge
english_head: before it was imprisoned in the body? I, said Cebes, was wonderfully convinced by it at the time and I still believe it more firmly than any other argument. And I too, said Simmias, feel just as he does, and I should be much surprised if I should ever think differently on this point. And Socrates said: You must, my Theban friend, think differently, if you persist in your opinion that a harmony is a compound and that the soul is a harmony made up of the elements that are strung like harpstrings in the body.

greek_head: σαυτοῦ λέγοντος ὡς πρότερον ἦν ἁρμονία συγκειμένη πρὶν ἐκεῖνα εἶναι ἐξ ὧν ἔδει αὐτὴν συντεθῆναι ἢ ἀποδέξῃ
greek_tokens: sautou legw ws proteros eimi armonia sugkeimai prin ekeinos eimi ek os dew autos suntiqhmi h apodecis oudamos fhmi eimi swkraths aisqanomai oun eimi de os ostis outos su sumbainw legw otan fhmi men eimi o yuxh prin kai eis anqrwpos eidos te kai swma afikneomai eimi de autos sugkeimai ek o oudepw eimi ou gar dh armonia ge su toioutos eimi os apeikazw alla proteros kai o lura kai o xordh kai
english_head: For surely you will not accept your own statement that a composite harmony existed before those things from which it had to be composed, will you? Certainly not, Socrates. Then do you see, said he, that this is just what you say when you assert that the soul exists before it enters into the form and body of a man, and that it is composed of things that do not yet exist? For harmony is not what your comparison assumes it to be. The lyre and the strings and the sounds

greek_head: οἱ φθόγγοι ἔτι ἀνάρμοστοι ὄντες γίγνονται τελευταῖον δὲ πάντων συνίσταται ἡ ἁρμονία καὶ πρῶτον ἀπόλλυται οὗτος
greek_tokens: e fqoggos eti anarmostos eimi gignomai teleutaios de pas sunisthmi o armonia kai proteros apollumi outos oun su o logos ekeinos pws sunadw oudamos fhmi o simmias kai meis eimi de os prepw ge eiper tis allos logos sunwdos eimi kai o peri armonia prepw gar fhmi o simmias outos toinun fhmi sos ou sunwdos alla oraw poteros airew o logos o maqhsis anamnhsis eimi h yuxh armonia polus mallon fhmi ekeinos eimi swkraths ode men gar
english_head: come into being in a tuneless condition, and the harmony is the last of all to be composed and the first to perish. So how can you bring this theory into harmony with the other? I cannot at all, said Simmias. And yet, said Socrates, there ought to be harmony between it and the theory about harmony above all others. Yes, there ought, said Simmias. Well, said he, there is no harmony between the two theories. Now which do you prefer, that knowledge is recollection or that the soul is a harmony? The former, decidedly, Socrates, he replied. For this other came to me without demonstration; it merely seemed probable

greek_head: μοι γέγονεν ἄνευ ἀποδείξεως μετὰ εἰκότος τινὸς καὶ εὐπρεπείας ὅθεν καὶ τοῖς πολλοῖς δοκεῖ ἀνθρώποις ἐγὼ δὲ τοῖς
greek_tokens: egw gignomai aneu apodeicis meta eoika tis kai euprepeia oqen kai o polus dokew anqrwpos egw de o dia o eoika o apodeicis poiew logos sunoida eimi alazwn kai an tis autos mh fulassw eu mala ecapataw kai en gewmetria kai en o allos apas o de peri o anamnhsis kai maqhsis logos dia upoqesis acios apodexomai eromai erw gar pou outws egw eimi o yuxh kai prin eis swma afikneomai wsper autos eimi o ousia exw o epwnumios o o o eimi
english_head: and attractive, which is the reason why many men hold it. I am conscious that those arguments which base their demonstrations on mere probability are deceptive, and if we are not on our guard against them they deceive us greatly, in geometry and in all other things. But the theory of recollection and knowledge has been established by a sound course of argument. For we agreed that our soul before it entered into the body existed just as the very essence which is called the absolute exists.

greek_head: ἐγὼ δὲ ταύτην ὡς ἐμαυτὸν πείθω ἱκανῶς τε καὶ ὀρθῶς ἀποδέδεγμαι ἀνάγκη οὖν μοι ὡς ἔοικε διὰ ταῦτα μήτε ἐμαυτοῦ

english_head: Now I am persuaded that I have accepted this essence on sufficient and right grounds. I cannot therefore accept from myself or anyone else the statement that the soul is a harmony. Here is another way of looking at it, Simmias, said he. Do you think a harmony or any other composite thing can be in any other state

greek_head: τινὶ συνθέσει προσήκειν ἄλλως πως ἔχειν ἢ ὡς ἂν ἐκεῖνα ἔχῃ ἐξ ὧν ἂν συγκέηται

english_head: than that in which the elements are of which it is composed? Certainly not. And it can neither do nor suffer anything other than they do or suffer? He agreed. Then a harmony cannot be expected to lead the elements of which it is composed, but to follow them. He assented. A harmony, then, is quite unable to move or make a sound or do anything else that is opposed to its component parts. Quite unable, said he. Well then, is not every harmony by nature a harmony according as it is harmonized? I do not understand, said Simmias. Would it not, said Socrates, be more completely a harmony

greek_head: εἴπερ ἐνδέχεται τοῦτο γίγνεσθαι μᾶλλόν τε ἂν ἁρμονία εἴη καὶ πλείων εἰ δ' ἧττόν τε καὶ ἐπ' ἔλαττον ἥττων τε καὶ ἐλάττων
greek_tokens: eiper endexomai outos gignomai mallon te an armonia eaw kai plew ei de hsswn te kai epi elasswn hsswn te kai elasswn panu ge eimi oun eimi outos peri yuxh wste kai kata o mikros mallon eteros eteros yuxazw epi pleos kai mallon h epi elasswn kai hsswn autos outos eimi yuxh oude opwsoun fhmi ferw dh fhmi pros zeus legw yuxh o men new te exw kai areth kai eimi agaqos o de anoia te kai moxqhria
english_head: and a greater harmony if it were harmonized more fully and to a greater extent, assuming that to be possible, and less completely a harmony and a lesser harmony if less completely harmonized and to a less extent? Certainly. Is this true of the soul? Is one soul even in the slightest degree more completely and to a greater extent a soul than another, or less completely and to a less extent? Not in the least, said he. Well now, said he, one soul is said to possess sense and virtue and to be good, and another to possess folly and wickedness and to be bad; and is this true?

greek_head: καὶ εἶναι κακή καὶ ταῦτα ἀληθῶς λέγεται Ἀληθῶς μέντοι
greek_tokens: kai eimi kakos kai outos alhqhs legw alhqhs mentoi o oun tiqhmi yuxh armonia eimi tis tis fhmi outos eimi eimi en o yuxh o te areth kai o kakia poteros armonia au tis allos kai anarmostia kai o men armozw o agaqos kai exw en eautou armonia eimi allos armonia o de anarmostos autos te eimi kai ou exw en eautou allos ou exw egw fhmi o simmias eipon dhlos de ostis toioutos atta an legos o ekeinos upotiqhmi
english_head: Yes, it is true. Now what will those who assume that the soul is a harmony say that these things—the virtue and the wickedness—in the soul are? Will they say that this is another kind of harmony and a discord, and that the soul, which is itself a harmony, has within it another harmony and that the other soul is discordant and has no other harmony within it? I cannot tell, replied Simmias, but evidently those who make that assumption would say some thing of that sort. But we agreed, said Socrates,

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ προωμολόγηται ἔφη μηδὲν μᾶλλον μηδ' ἧττον ἑτέραν ἑτέρας ψυχὴν ψυχῆς εἶναι τοῦτο δ' ἔστι τὸ ὁμολόγημα
