english_head: that one soul is no more or less a soul than another; and that is equivalent to an agreement that one is no more and to no greater extent, and no less and to no less extent, a harmony than another, is it not? Certainly. And that which is no more or less a harmony, is no more or less harmonized. Is that so? Yes. But has that which is no more and no less harmonized any greater or any less amount of harmony, or an equal amount? An equal amount. Then a soul, since it is neither more nor less

greek_head: ἄλλης αὐτὸ τοῦτο ψυχή ἐστίν οὐδὲ δὴ μᾶλλον οὐδὲ ἧττον ἥρμοσται
greek_tokens: allos autos outos yuxh eimi oude dh mallon oude hsswn armozw outws outos de ge pasxw oudeis pleos anarmostia oude armonia metexw an ou gar oun outos de au pasxw ara an tis pleos kakia h aretaw metexw eteros eteros eiper o men kakia anarmostia o de areth armonia eaw oudeis pleos
english_head: a soul than another, is neither more nor less harmonized. That is so. And therefore can have no greater amount of discord or of harmony? No. And therefore again one soul can have no greater amount of wickedness or virtue than another, if wickedness is discord and virtue harmony? It cannot. Or rather, to speak exactly, Simmias,

greek_head: Μᾶλλον δέ γέ που ὦ Σιμμία κατὰ τὸν ὀρθὸν λόγον κακίας οὐδεμία ψυχὴ μεθέξει εἴπερ ἁρμονία ἐστίν ἁρμονία
greek_tokens: mallon de ge pou eimi simmias kata o orqos logos kakia oudeis yuxh meqecis eiper armonia eimi armonia gar dhpou pantelhs autos outos eimi armonia anarmostia oupote an metexw ou mentoi oude ge dhpou yuxh eimi pantelhs yuxh kakia pws gar ek ge o proerew ek outos ara o logos egw paomai yuxh pas zwion omoios agaqos eimi eiper omoios yuxh fuw autos outos yuxh eimi egw dokew fhmi eimi swkraths eimi kai kalos dokew eimi de os outws legw kai pasxw
english_head: no soul will have any wickedness at all, if the soul is a harmony; for if a harmony is entirely harmony, it could have no part in discord. Certainly not. Then the soul, being entirely soul, could have no part in wickedness. How could it, if what we have said is right? According to this argument, then, if all souls are by nature equally souls, all souls of all living creatures will be equally good. So it seems, Socrates, said he.

greek_head: ἂν ταῦτα ὁ λόγος εἰ ὀρθὴ ἡ ὑπόθεσις ἦν τὸ ψυχὴν ἁρμονίαν εἶναι
greek_tokens: an outos o logos ei orqos o upoqesis eimi o yuxh armonia eimi oude opwsoun fhmi tis de eimi de os o en anqrwpos pas esqw ostis allos legw arxw h yuxh allos te kai fronimos ou egw poteros sugxwrew o kata o swma paqos h kai enantioomai legos de o toiosde oios kauma eneimi kai diyos epi enantion elkw o mh pinw kai peina nousew epi o mh esqiw kai allos murios pou oraw
english_head: And, said Socrates, do you think that this is true and that our reasoning would have come to this end, if the theory that the soul is a harmony were correct? Not in the least, he replied. Well, said Socrates, of all the parts that make up a man, do you think any is ruler except the soul, especially if it be a wise one? No, I do not. Does it yield to the feelings of the body or oppose them? I mean, when the body is hot and thirsty, does not the soul oppose it and draw it away from drinking, and from eating when it is hungry, and do we not see the soul opposing the body

greek_head: ἐναντιουμένην τὴν ψυχὴν τοῖς κατὰ τὸ σῶμα ἢ οὔ Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: enantioomai o yuxh o kata o swma h ou panu men oun oukoun au omologew en o prosqen mhpote an autos armonia ge eimi enantios aeidw os epiteinw kai xalaw kai yallw kai allos otioun paqos pasxw ekeinos ek os tugxanw eimi alla epomai ekeinos kai oupote an hgemoneuw omologew fhmi pws gar ou tis oun nun ou pas enantion egw fainw ergazomai hgemoneuw te ekeinos pas ek os fhmi tis autos
english_head: in countless other ways? Certainly. Did we not agree in our previous discussion that it could never, if it be a harmony, give forth a sound at variance with the tensions and relaxations and vibrations and other conditions of the elements which compose it, but that it would follow them and never lead them? Yes, he replied, we did, of course. Well then, do we not now find that the soul acts in exactly the opposite way, leading those elements of which it is said to consist and opposing them

greek_head: εἶναι καὶ ἐναντιουμένη ὀλίγου πάντα διὰ παντὸς τοῦ βίου καὶ δεσπόζουσα πάντας τρόπους τὰ μὲν χαλεπώτερον κολάζουσα

english_head: in almost everything through all our life, and tyrannizing over them in every way, sometimes inflicting harsh and painful punishments (those of gymnastics and medicine), and sometimes milder ones, sometimes threatening and sometimes admonishing, in short, speaking to the desires and passions and fears as if it were distinct from them and they from it, as Homer has shown in the Odyssey when he says of Odysseus: He smote his breast, and thus he chid his heart: Endure it, heart, you have born worse than this. Hom. Od 20.17-18

english_head: Do you suppose that, when he wrote those words, he thought of the soul as a harmony which would be led by the conditions of the body, and not rather as something fitted to lead and rule them, and itself a far more divine thing than a harmony? By Zeus, Socrates, the latter, I think. Then, my good friend, it will never do for us to say that the soul is a harmony; for we should, it seems,

english_head: agree neither with Homer, the divine poet, nor with ourselves. That is true, said he. Very well, said Socrates, Harmonia, the Theban goddess, has, it seems, been moderately gracious to us; but how, Cebes, and by what argument can we find grace in the sight of Cadmus? I think, said Cebes, you will find a way. At any rate, you conducted this argument against harmony wonderfully and better than I expected. For when Simmias was telling of his difficulty, I wondered if anyone could make head against

english_head: his argument; so it seemed to me very remarkable that it could not withstand the first attack of your argument. Now I should not be surprised if the argument of Cadmus met with the same fate. My friend, said Socrates, do not be boastful, lest some evil eye put to rout the argument that is to come. That, however, is in the hands of God. Let us, in Homeric fashion, charge the foe and test the worth of what you say. Now the sum total of what you seek is this: You demand a proof that our soul is indestructible

english_head: and immortal, if the philosopher, who is confident in the face of death and who thinks that after death he will fare better in the other world than if he had lived his life differently, is not to find his confidence senseless and foolish. And although we show that the soul is strong and godlike and existed before we men were born as men, all this, you say, may bear witness not to immortality, but only to the fact that the soul lasts a long while, and existed somewhere an immeasurably long time before our birth, and knew and did various things; yet it was none the more immortal for all that,

english_head: but its very entrance into the human body was the beginning of its dissolution, a disease, as it were; and it lives in toil through this life and finally perishes in what we call death. Now it makes no difference, you say, whether a soul enters into a body once or many times, so far as the fear each of us feels is concerned; for anyone, unless he is a fool, must fear, if he does not know and cannot prove that the soul is immortal. That,

english_head: Cebes, is, I think, about what you mean. And I restate it purposely that nothing may escape us and that you may, if you wish, add or take away anything. And Cebes said, I do not at present wish to take anything away or to add anything. You have expressed my meaning. Socrates paused for some time and was absorbed in thought. Then he said: It is no small thing that you seek; for the cause of generation and decay must be completely investigated.

greek_head: ἐγὼ οὖν σοι δίειμι περὶ αὐτῶν ἐὰν βούλῃ τά γε ἐμὰ πάθη ἔπειτα ἄν τί σοι χρήσιμον

english_head: Now I will tell you my own experience in the matter, if you wish; then if anything I say seems to you to be of any use, you can employ it for the solution of your difficulty. Certainly, said Cebes, I wish to hear your experiences. Listen then, and I will tell you. When I was young, Cebes, I was tremendously eager for the kind of wisdom which they call investigation of nature. I thought it was a glorious thing to know the causes of everything, why each thing comes into being and why it perishes and why it exists;

english_head: and I was always unsettling myself with such questions as these: Do heat and cold, by a sort of fermentation, bring about the organization of animals, as some people say? Is it the blood, or air, or fire by which we think? Or is it none of these, and does the brain furnish the sensations of hearing and sight and smell, and do memory and opinion arise from these, and does knowledge come from memory and opinion in a state of rest? And again I tried to find out

english_head: how these things perish, and I investigated the phenomena of heaven and earth until finally I made up my mind that I was by nature totally unfitted for this kind of investigation. And I will give you a sufficient proof of this. I was so completely blinded by these studies that I lost the knowledge that I, and others also, thought I had before; I forgot what I had formerly believed I knew about many things and even about the cause of man’s growth. For I had thought previously that it was plain to everyone that man grows through eating and

And I cannot yet believe that if one is divided, the division causes it to become two; for this is the opposite of

greek_head: γίγνεται ἢ τότε αἰτία τοῦ δύο γίγνεσθαι τότε μὲν γὰρ ὅτι συνήγετο πλησίον ἀλλήλων καὶ προσετίθετο ἕτερον ἑτέρῳ
greek_tokens: gignomai h tote aitios o duo gignomai tote men gar ostis sunagw pimplhmi allhlwn kai prostiqhmi eteros eteros nun de ostis apagw kai xwrizw eteros apo eteros oude ge dia ostis eis gignomai ws efisthmi eti peiqw emautou oude allos oudeis eis logos dia ostis gignomai h apollumi h eimi kata outos o tropos o meqodos alla tis allos tropos autos eikazw furw outos de oudamh prosihmi alla aekwn men pote ek biblion tis ws fhmi anacagorhs
english_head: the cause which produced two in the former case; for then two arose because one was brought near and added to another one, and now because one is removed and separated from other. And I no longer believe that I know by this method even how one is generated or, in a word, how anything is generated or is destroyed or exists, and I no longer admit this method, but have another confused way of my own. Then one day I heard a man reading from a book, as he said, by Anaxagoras,

greek_head: ἀναγιγνώσκοντος καὶ λέγοντος ὡς ἄρα νοῦς ἐστιν ὁ διακοσμῶν τε καὶ πάντων αἴτιος ταύτῃ δὴ τῇ αἰτίᾳ ἥσθην τε
greek_tokens: anagignwskw kai legw ws ara noos eimi o diakosmew te kai pas aitios outos dh o aitios ezomai te kai dokew egw tropos tis eu exw o o new eimi pas aitios kai ageomai ei outos outws exis o ge new kosmew pas kosmew kai ekastos tiqhmi outos oph an beltistos exis ei oun tis boulomai o aitios euriskw peri ekastos oph gignomai h apollumi h eimi outos dew peri autos euriskw oph beltistos autos eimi h eimi h
english_head: that it is the mind that arranges and causes all things. I was pleased with this theory of cause, and it seemed to me to be somehow right that the mind should be the cause of all things, and I thought, If this is so, the mind in arranging things arranges everything and establishes each thing as it is best for it to be. So if anyone wishes to find the cause of the generation or destruction or existence of a particular thing, he must find out what sort of existence, or passive state of any kind, or activity is best for it. And therefore in respect to

greek_head: ἄλλο ὁτιοῦν πάσχειν ἢ ποιεῖν ἐκ δὲ δὴ τοῦ λόγου τούτου οὐδὲν ἄλλο σκοπεῖν προσήκειν ἀνθρώπῳ καὶ περὶ αὐτοῦ ἐκείνου
greek_tokens: allos otioun pasxw h poiew ek de dh o logos outos oudeis allos skopaw proshkw anqrwpos kai peri autos ekeinos kai peri o allos alla h o ariston kai o beltistos anagkaion de eimi o autos outos kai o xeirwn oida o autos gar eimi episthmh peri autos outos dh logizomai asmenos euriskw oiomai didaskalos o aitios peri o eimi kata new emautou o anacagorhs kai egw frazw proteros men poteros o gaia plateia eimi h
english_head: that particular thing, and other things too, a man need examine nothing but what is best and most excellent; for then he will necessarily know also what is inferior, since the science of both is the same. As I considered these things I was delighted to think that I had found in Anaxagoras a teacher of the cause of things quite to my mind, and I thought he would tell me whether the earth is flat or round, and when

greek_head: στρογγύλη ἐπειδὴ δὲ φράσειεν ἐπεκδιηγήσεσθαι τὴν αἰτίαν καὶ τὴν ἀνάγκην λέγοντα τὸ ἄμεινον καὶ ὅτι αὐτὴν ἄμεινον
greek_tokens: stroggulos epei de frazw epekdihgeomai o aitios kai o anagkh legw o ameinwn kai ostis autos ameinwn eimi toioutos eimi kai ei en mesos fhmi eimi autos epekdihgeomai ws ameinwn eimi autos en mesos eimi kai ei egw
english_head: he had told me that, would go on to explain the cause and the necessity of it, and would tell me the nature of the best and why it is best for the earth to be as it is; and if he said the earth was in the center, he would proceed to show that it is best for it to be in the center; and I had made up my mind that

greek_head: ταῦτα ἀποφαίνοι παρεσκευάσμην ὡς οὐκέτι ποθεσόμενος αἰτίας ἄλλο εἶδος καὶ δὴ καὶ περὶ ἡλίου οὕτω παρεσκευάσμην
greek_tokens: outos apofainw paraskeuazw ws ouketi poqew aitios allos eidos kai dh kai peri hlios outws paraskeuazw wsautws punqanomai kai selhnh kai o allos astron taxos te peri pros allhlwn kai tropew kai o allos paqhma ph pote outos ameinwn eimi ekastos kai poiew kai pasxw o pasxw ou gar an pote autos oiomai faskw ge upo neomai autos kosmew allos tis autos aitios epiferw h ostis beltistos autos outws exw
english_head: if he made those things clear to me, I would no longer yearn for any other kind of cause. And I had determined that I would find out in the same way about the sun and the moon and the other stars, their relative speed, their revolutions, and their other changes, and why the active or passive condition of each of them is for the best. For I never imagined that, when he said they were ordered by intelligence, he would introduce any other cause for these things than that it it is best for them to be as they are.

greek_head: ἐστὶν ὥσπερ ἔχει ἑκάστῳ οὖν αὐτῶν ἀποδιδόντα τὴν αἰτίαν καὶ κοινῇ πᾶσι τὸ ἑκάστῳ βέλτιστον ᾤμην καὶ τὸ κοινὸν
greek_tokens: eimi wsper exis ekastos oun autos apodidwmi o aitios kai koinh pas o ekastos beltistos oiomai kai o koinos pas epekdihgeomai agaqos kai ou an apodidwmi polus o elpis alla panu spoudazw lambanw o biblos ws taxistos oios su eimi anagignwskw ina ws taxistos oida o beltistos kai o xeirwn apo dh qaumastos elpis eimi etairos oixomai ferw epei proeimi kai anagignwskw oraw anhr o men egw oudeis xraomai oude tis aitios epaitiaomai eis o
english_head: So I thought when he assigned the cause of each thing and of all things in common he would go on and explain what is best for each and what is good for all in common. I prized my hopes very highly, and I seized the books very eagerly and read them as fast as I could, that I might know as fast as I could about the best and the worst. My glorious hope, my friend, was quickly snatched away from me. As I went on with my reading I saw that the man made no use of intelligence,

greek_head: διακοσμεῖν τὰ πράγματα ἀέρας δὲ καὶ αἰθέρας καὶ ὕδατα αἰτιώμενον καὶ ἄλλα πολλὰ καὶ ἄτοπα καί μοι ἔδοξεν
greek_tokens: diakosmew o pragma ahr de kai aiqhr kai udwr aitiaomai kai allos polus kai atopos kai egw dokew omoios pasxw wsper an ei tis legos ostis swkratew pas osos prassw egw prassw epeita epixeirew legw o aitios ekastos os prassw legos proteros men ostis dia outos nun enqade kaqhmai ostis sugkeimai egw o swma ek osteon kai neuron kai o men osteon eimi stereos kai diafuas exis xwris apo allhlwn o de neuron oios epiteinw
english_head: and did not assign any real causes for the ordering of things, but mentioned as causes air and ether and water and many other absurdities. And it seemed to me it was very much as if one should say that Socrates does with intelligence whatever he does, and then, in trying to give the causes of the particular thing I do, should say first that I am now sitting here because my body is composed of bones and sinews, and the bones are hard and have joints which divide them and the sinews

greek_head: καὶ ἀνίεσθαι περιαμπέχοντα τὰ ὀστᾶ μετὰ τῶν σαρκῶν καὶ δέρματος ὃ συνέχει αὐτά αἰωρουμένων οὖν τῶν
greek_tokens: kai anihmi periampexw o osteon meta o sarc kai derma o sugxew autos aiwrew oun o osteon en o eautou sumbolh xalaw kai sunteinw o neuron kamptw pou poiew oios su eimi egw nun o melh kai dia outos o aitios sugkamptw enqade kaqhmai kai au peri o dialegw su eteros toioutos aitios legos fwnh te kai ahr kai akoh kai allos murios
english_head: can be contracted and relaxed and, with the flesh and the skin which contains them all, are laid about the bones; and so, as the bones are hung loose in their ligaments, the sinews, by relaxing and contracting, make me able to bend my limbs now, and that is the cause of my sitting here with my legs bent. Or as if in the same way he should give voice and air and hearing and countless other things of the sort as causes for our talking with each other,

greek_head: τοιαῦτα αἰτιώμενος ἀμελήσας τὰς ὡς ἀληθῶς αἰτίας λέγειν ὅτι ἐπειδὴ Ἀθηναίοις ἔδοξε βέλτιον εἶναι ἐμοῦ καταψηφίσασθαι
greek_tokens: toioutos aitiaomai amelew o ws alhqhs aitios legw ostis epei aqhnaia dokew beltiwn eimi egw katayhfizomai dia outos dh kai egw beltiwn au dokew enqade kaqhmai kai dikaios paramenw upexw o dikh os an keleuw epei nh o kuwn ws oimh pala an
english_head: and should fail to mention the real causes, which are, that the Athenians decided that it was best to condemn me, and therefore I have decided that it was best for me to sit here and that it is right for me to stay and undergo whatever penalty they order.

greek_head: ταῦτα τὰ νεῦρα καὶ τὰ ὀστᾶ ἢ περὶ Μέγαρα ἢ Βοιωτοὺς ἦν ὑπὸ δόξης φερόμενα τοῦ βελτίστου εἰ μὴ δικαιότερον ᾤμην
greek_tokens: outos o neuron kai o osteon h peri megaron h boiwtos eimi upo doca ferw o beltistos ei mh dikaios oiomai kai kallion eimi pro o feugw te kai apodidraskw upexw o polis dikh ostis an tassw alla aitios men o toioutos kalew lian atopos ei de tis legos ostis aneu o o toioutos exw kai osteon kai neuron kai osos allos exw ou an oios su eimi poiew o dokew egw alhqhs an legos ws mentoi dia outos poiew o poiew kai outos egw prassw alla ou
english_head: For, by Dog, I fancy these bones and sinews of mine would have been in Megara or Boeotia long ago, carried thither by an opinion of what was best, if I did not think it was better and nobler to endure any penalty the city may inflict rather than to escape and run away. But it is most absurd to call things of that sort causes. If anyone were to say that I could not have done what I thought proper if I had not bones and sinews and other things that I have, he would be right. But to say that those things are the cause of my doing what I do,

greek_head: τῇ τοῦ βελτίστου αἱρέσει πολλὴ ἂν καὶ μακρὰ ῥᾳθυμία εἴη τοῦ λόγου τὸ γὰρ μὴ διελέσθαι οἷόν τ' εἶναι ὅτι ἄλλο μέν
greek_tokens: o o beltistos airesis polus an kai makros raqumia eaw o logos o gar mh diairew oios su eimi ostis allos men tis eimi o aitios o eimi allos de ekeinos aneu e o aitios ou an pote eaw aitios o dh egw fainw yhlafaw e polus wsper en skotos allotrios onoma prosxraomai ws aitios autos prosagoreuw dio dh kai o men tis dinh peritiqhmi o gaia upo o ouranos menw dh poiew o gaia o de wsper kardopos plateia baqron o ahr upereidw
english_head: and that I act with intelligence but not from the choice of what is best, would be an extremely careless way of talking. Whoever talks in that way is unable to make a distinction and to see that in reality a cause is one thing, and the thing without which the cause could never be a cause is quite another thing. And so it seems to me that most people, when they give the name of cause to the latter, are groping in the dark, as it were, and are giving it a name that does not belong to it. And so one man makes the earth stay below the heavens by putting a vortex about it, and another regards the earth as a flat trough supported on a foundation of air; but they do not look for

greek_head: τὴν δὲ τοῦ ὡς οἷόν τε βέλτιστα αὐτὰ τεθῆναι δύναμιν οὕτω νῦν κεῖσθαι ταύτην οὔτε ζητοῦσιν οὔτε τινὰ οἴονται δαιμονίαν
greek_tokens: o de o ws oios te beltistos autos tiqhmi dunamis outws nun kew outos oute zhtew oute tis oiomai daimonios isxus exw alla hgeomai outos atlas an pote isxuros kai aqanatos kai mallon apas sunoxwka eceuriskw kai ws alhqhs o agaqos kai deon sundeomai kai sunoxwka oudeis oiomai egw men oun o toioutos aitios oph pote exis maqhths ostis hdus an gignomai epei de outos sterew kai oute autos euriskw oute para allos manqanw oios te gignomai o deuteros
english_head: the power which causes things to be now placed as it is best for them to be placed, nor do they think it has any divine force, but they think they can find a new Atlas more powerful and more immortal and more all-embracing than this, and in truth they give no thought to the good, which must embrace and hold together all things. Now I would gladly be the pupil of anyone who would teach me the nature of such a cause; but since that was denied me and I was not able to discover it myself or to learn of it from anyone else,

greek_head: πλοῦν ἐπὶ τὴν τῆς αἰτίας ζήτησιν ᾗ πεπραγμάτευμαι βούλει σοι ἔφη ἐπίδειξιν ποιήσωμαι ὦ Κέβης
greek_tokens: ploos epi o o aitios zhthsis h pragmateuomai boulomai su fhmi epideicis poiew eimi kebhs uperfuhs men oun fhmi ws boulomai dokew toinun egw eimi de os meta outos epei apoerw o eimi skopaw dew eulabeomai mh pasxw osper e o hlios ekleipw qewrew kai skopaw pasxw diafqeirw gar pou enioi o omma ean mh en udwr h
english_head: do you wish me, Cebes, said he, to give you an account of the way in which I have conducted my second voyage in quest of the cause? I wish it with all my heart, he replied. After this, then, said he, since I had given up investigating realities, I decided that I must be careful not to suffer the misfortune which happens to people who look at the sun and watch it during an eclipse. For some of them ruin their eyes unless they look at its image in water

greek_head: τινι τοιούτῳ σκοπῶνται τὴν εἰκόνα αὐτοῦ τοιοῦτόν τι καὶ ἐγὼ διενοήθην καὶ ἔδεισα μὴ παντάπασι τὴν ψυχὴν τυφλωθείην
greek_tokens: tis toioutos skopaw o eikwn autos toioutos tis kai egw dianoeomai kai deidw mh pantapasi o yuxh tuflow blepw pros o pragma o omma kai ekastos o aisqhsis epixeirew aptw autos dokew dh egw xrh eis o logos katafeugw en ekeinos skopaw o eimi o alhqeia isos men oun os eikazw tropos
english_head: or something of the sort. I thought of that danger, and I was afraid my soul would be blinded if I looked at things with my eyes and tried to grasp them with any of my senses. So I thought I must have recourse to conceptions and examine in them the truth of realities. Now perhaps my metaphor

greek_head: τινὰ οὐκ ἔοικεν οὐ γὰρ πάνυ συγχωρῶ τὸν ἐν τοῖς λόγοις σκοπούμενον τὰ ὄντα ἐν εἰκόσι μᾶλλον σκοπεῖν ἢ τὸν ἐν
greek_tokens: tis ou eoika ou gar panu sugxwrew o en o logos skopaw o eimi en eoika mallon skopaw h o en o ergon alla oun dh outos ge ormaw kai upotiqhmi ekastote logos os an krinon errwmenos eimi o men an egw dokazw outos sumfwnew tiqhmi ws alhqhs eimi kai peri aitios kai peri o allos apas eimi o de an mh ws ou alhqhs boulomai de su safhs eipon o legos oimh gar su nun ou manqanw ou ma o dios fhmi o kebhs ou sfodra
english_head: is not quite accurate; for I do not grant in the least that he who studies realities by means of conceptions is looking at them in images any more than he who studies them in the facts of daily life. However, that is the way I began. I assume in each case some principle which I consider strongest, and whatever seems to me to agree with this, whether relating to cause or to anything else, I regard as true, and whatever disagrees with it, as untrue. But I want to tell you more clearly what I mean; for I think you do not understand now. Not very well, certainly, said Cebes.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἦ δ' ὅς ὧδε λέγω οὐδὲν καινόν ἀλλ' ἅπερ ἀεί τε ἄλλοτε καὶ ἐν τῷ παρεληλυθότι λόγῳ οὐδὲν πέπαυμαι
greek_tokens: alla eimi de os wde legos oudeis kainos alla o aei te allote kai en o parerxomai logos oudeis pauw legos erxomai gar dh epixeirew su epideiknumi o aitios o eidos o pragmateuomai kai eimi palin epi ekeinos o poluqrulhtos kai arxw apo ekeinos upotiqhmi eimi tis kalos autos kaqa eautou kai agaqos kai megas kai allos pas o ei egw didwmi te kai sugxwrew eimi outos elpizw su ek outos o aitios epideiknumi kai aneuriskw ws aqanatos o yuxh
english_head: Well, said Socrates, this is what I mean. It is nothing new, but the same thing I have always been saying, both in our previous conversation and elsewhere. I am going to try to explain to you the nature of that cause which I have been studying, and I will revert to those familiar subjects of ours as my point of departure and assume that there are such things as absolute beauty and good and greatness and the like. If you grant this and agree that these exist, I believe I shall explain cause to you and shall prove that

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μήν ἔφη ὁ Κέβης ὡς διδόντος σοι οὐκ ἂν φθάνοις περαίνων
greek_tokens: alla meis fhmi o kebhs ws didwmi su ou an fqanw perainw skopaw dh fhmi o echs ekeinos ean su sundokew wsper egw fainw gar egw ei tis eimi allos kalos plhn autos o kalos oude dia eis allos kalos eimi h dioti metexw ekeinos o kalew kai pas dh outws legos o toiosde aitios sugxwrew sugxwrew fhmi ou toinun eimi de os eti manqanw oude dunamai o allos aitios o sofos outos gignwskw alla ean tis egw legos
english_head: the soul is immortal. You may assume, said Cebes, that I grant it, and go on. Then, said he, see if you agree with me in the next step. I think that if anything is beautiful besides absolute beauty it is beautiful for no other reason than because it partakes of absolute beauty; and this applies to everything. Do you assent to this view of cause? I do, said he. Now I do not yet, understand, he went on, nor can I perceive those other ingenious causes. If anyone tells me that what makes a thing beautiful is its lovely color,

greek_head: δι' ὅτι καλόν ἐστιν ὁτιοῦν ἢ χρῶμα εὐανθὲς ἔχον ἢ σχῆμα ἢ ἄλλο ὁτιοῦν τῶν τοιούτων τὰ μὲν ἄλλα χαίρειν ἐῶ
greek_tokens: dia ostis kalos eimi otioun h xrwma euanqhs exw h sxhma h allos otioun o toioutos o men allos xairw eaw tarassw gar en o allos pas outos de aplos kai atexnos kai isos euhqhs exw para emautou ostis ou allos tis poiew autos kalos h o ekeinos o kalew eite parousia eite koinwnia eite oph dh kai opws prosgignomai ou gar eti outos diisxurizomai alla ostis o kalos pas o kalos gignomai kalos outos gar egw dokew asfalhs eimi kai emautou apokrinw kai allos kai outos exw
english_head: or its shape or anything else of the sort, I let all that go, for all those things confuse me, and I hold simply and plainly and perhaps foolishly to this, that nothing else makes it beautiful but the presence or communion (call it which you please) of absolute beauty, however it may have been gained; about the way in which it happens, I make no positive statement as yet, but I do insist that beautiful things are made beautiful by beauty. For I think this is the safest answer I can give to myself or to others, and if I cleave fast to this,

greek_head: ἡγοῦμαι οὐκ ἄν ποτε πεσεῖν ἀλλ' ἀσφαλὲς εἶναι καὶ ἐμοὶ καὶ ὁτῳοῦν ἄλλῳ ἀποκρίνασθαι ὅτι τῷ καλῷ τὰ καλὰ
greek_tokens: hgeomai ou an pote piptw alla asfalhs eimi kai egw kai ostis allos apokrinw ostis o kalos o kalos gignomai kalos h ou kai sos dokew dokew kai megeqos ara o megas megas kai o megas megas kai mikroths o elasswn elasswn nai oude su ara an apodexomai ei tis tis fhmi eteros eteros o kefalh megas eimi kai o elasswn o autos outos
english_head: I think I shall never be overthrown, and I believe it is safe for me or anyone else to give this answer, that beautiful things are beautiful through beauty. Do you agree? I do. And great things are great and greater things greater by greatness, and smaller things smaller by smallness? Yes. And you would not accept the statement, if you were told that one man was greater or smaller than another by a head,

greek_head: ἐλάττω ἀλλὰ διαμαρτύροιο ἂν ὅτι σὺ μὲν οὐδὲν ἄλλο λέγεις ἢ ὅτι τὸ μεῖζον πᾶν ἕτερον ἑτέρου οὐδενὶ ἄλλῳ μεῖζόν ἐστιν
greek_tokens: elasswn alla diamarturomai an ostis su men oudeis allos legw h ostis o megas pas eteros eteros oudeis allos megas eimi h megeqos kai dia outos megas dia o megeqos o de elasswn oudeis allos elasswn h mikroths kai dia outos elasswn dia o mikroths fobew oimh mh tis su enantios logos apanthsis ean o kefalh megas tis fhmi eimi kai elasswn proteros men o autos o megas megas eimi kai o elasswn elasswn epeita o kefalh mikros eimi o
english_head: but you would insist that you say only that every greater thing is greater than another by nothing else than greatness, and that it is greater by reason of greatness, and that which is smaller is smaller by nothing else than smallness and is smaller by reason of smallness. For you would, I think, be afraid of meeting with the retort, if you said that a man was greater or smaller than another by a head, first that the greater is greater and the smaller is smaller by the same thing, and secondly, that

greek_head: μείζω μείζω εἶναι καὶ τοῦτο δὴ τέρας εἶναι τὸ σμικρῷ τινι μέγαν τινὰ εἶναι ἢ οὐκ ἂν φοβοῖο ταῦτα
greek_tokens: megas megas eimi kai outos dh teras eimi o mikros tis megas tis eimi h ou an fobew outos kai o kebhs gelaw egw fhmi oukoun eimi de os o deka o oktw duo plew eimi kai dia outos o aitios uperballw fobew an legw alla mh plhqw kai dia o plhqos kai o diphxus o phxuaios hmisus megas eimi alla ou megeqos o autos gar pou fobos panu ge fhmi tis de eis eis prostiqhmi o prosqesis aitios eimi
english_head: the greater man is greater by a head, which is small, and that it is a monstrous thing that one is great by something that is small. Would you not be afraid of this? And Cebes laughed and said, Yes, I should. Then, he continued, you would be afraid to say that ten is more than eight by two and that this is the reason it is more. You would say it is more by number and by reason of number; and a two cubit measure is greater than a one-cubit measure not by half but by magnitude, would you not? For you would have the same fear. Certainly, said he. Well, then, if one is added to one

greek_head: τοῦ δύο γενέσθαι ἢ διασχισθέντος τὴν σχίσιν οὐκ εὐλαβοῖο ἂν λέγειν καὶ μέγα ἂν βοῴης ὅτι οὐκ οἶσθα ἄλλως πως
greek_tokens: o duo gignomai h diasxizw o sxisis ou eulabeomai an legw kai megas an bazw ostis ou oida allos pws ekastos gignomai h metexw o idios ousia ekastos e an metexw kai en outos ou exis allos tis aitios o duo gignomai alla h o o duas metasxesis kai dew outos metexw o mellw duo eimi kai monas o an mellw eis eimi o de sxisis outos kai prosqesis kai o allos o toioutos komyeia eaw an xairw parihmi apokrinw o sautou sofos su de deidw an o
english_head: or if one is divided, you would avoid saying that the addition or the division is the cause of two? You would exclaim loudly that you know no other way by which any thing can come into existence than by participating in the proper essence of each thing in which it participates, and therefore you accept no other cause of the existence of two than participation in duality, and things which are to be two must participate in duality, and whatever is to be one must participate in unity, and you would pay no attention to the divisions and additions and other such subtleties, leaving those for wiser men to explain. You would distrust

greek_head: λεγόμενον τὴν σαυτοῦ σκιὰν καὶ τὴν ἀπειρίαν ἐχόμενος ἐκείνου τοῦ ἀσφαλοῦς τῆς ὑποθέσεως οὕτως ἀποκρίναιο ἄν
greek_tokens: legw o sautou skia kai o apeiria exw ekeinos o asfalhs o upoqesis outws apokrinw an ei de tis autos o upoqesis exw xairw eaw an kai ou apokrinw ews an o apo ekeinos ormaw skeptomai ei su allhlwn sumfwnew h diafwnew epei de ekeinos autos dew su didwmi logos wsautws an didwmi allos au upoqesis upotiqhmi ostis o anwqen beltistos fainw
english_head: your inexperience and would be afraid, as the saying goes, of your own shadow; so you would cling to that safe principle of ours and would reply as I have said. And if anyone attacked the principle, you would pay him no attention and you would not reply to him until you had examined the consequences to see whether they agreed with one another or not; and when you had to give an explanation of the principle, you would give it in the same way by assuming some other principle which seemed to you the best of the higher ones, and so on until

greek_head: ἕως ἐπί τι ἱκανὸν ἔλθοις ἅμα δὲ οὐκ ἂν φύροιο ὥσπερ οἱ ἀντιλογικοὶ περί τε τῆς ἀρχῆς διαλεγόμενος καὶ τῶν ἐξ
greek_tokens: ews epi tis ikanos erxomai ama de ou an furw wsper e antilogikos peri te o arxh dialegw kai o ek ekeinos ormaw eiper boulomai tis o eimi euriskw ekeinos men gar isos oude eis peri outos logos oude frontis ikanos gar upo sofia omos pas kukaw omws dunamai autos eautou areskw su de eiper ei o filosofos
english_head: you reached one which was adequate. You would not mix things up, as disputants do, in talking about the beginning and its consequences, if you wished to discover any of the realities; for perhaps not one of them thinks or cares in the least about these things. They are so clever that they succeed in being well pleased with themselves even when they mix everything up;

greek_head: οἶμαι ἂν ὡς ἐγὼ λέγω ποιοῖς Ἀληθέστατα ἔφη λέγεις ὅ τε Σιμμίας ἅμα καὶ ὁ Κέβης
greek_tokens: oimh an ws egw legos poiew alhqhs fhmi legw o te simmias ama kai o kebhs nh dios eimi faidwn eoikotws ge qaumastos gar egw dokew ws enarghs o kai mikros new exw eipon ekeinos outos panu men oun eimi exekraths kai pas o pareimi dokew kai gar egw o apeimi nun de akouw alla tis dh eimi o meta outos legw ws men egw oimh epei autos outos sugxwrew
english_head: but if you are a philosopher, I think you will do as I have said. That is true, said Simmias and Cebes together. By Zeus, Phaedo, they were right. It seems to me that he made those matters astonishingly clear, to anyone with even a little sense. Certainly, Echecrates, and all who were there thought so, too. And so do we who were not there, and are hearing about it now. But what was said after that? As I remember it, after all this had been admitted, and they had agreed that

greek_head: καὶ ὡμολογεῖτο εἶναί τι ἕκαστον τῶν εἰδῶν καὶ τούτων τἆλλα μεταλαμβάνοντα αὐτῶν τούτων τὴν ἐπωνυμίαν ἴσχειν
greek_tokens: kai omologew eimi tis ekastos o eidos kai outos allos metalambanw autos outos o epwnumios isxw o dh meta outos erwtaw ei dh eimi de os outos outws legw ara ou otan simmias swkraths fhmi megas eimi faidwn de elasswn legw tote eimi en o simmias amfoteros kai megeqos kai mikroths egw alla gar eimi de os omologew o o simmias uperexw swkraths ou ws o rhma legw outws kai o alhqhs
english_head: each of the abstract qualities exists and that other things which participate in these get their names from them, then Socrates asked: Now if you assent to this, do you not, when you say that Simmias is greater than Socrates and smaller than Phaedo, say that there is in Simmias greatness and smallness? Yes. But, said Socrates, you agree that the statement that Simmias is greater than Socrates is not true as stated in those words. For Simmias is not greater than Socrates

greek_head: ἔχειν οὐ γάρ που πεφυκέναι Σιμμίαν ὑπερέχειν τούτῳ τῷ Σιμμίαν εἶναι ἀλλὰ τῷ μεγέθει ὃ τυγχάνει ἔχων οὐδ' αὖ
greek_tokens: exw ou gar pou fuw simmias uperexw outos o simmias eimi alla o megeqos o tugxanw exw oude au swkraths uperexw ostis swkratew o swkratew eimi alla ostis mikroths exis o swkratew pros o ekeinos megeqos alhqhs oude ge au upo faidwn uperexw o ostis faidwn o faidwn eimi alla ostis megeqos exis o faidwn pros o simmias mikroths eimi outos outws ara o simmias epwnumios exis mikros te kai megas eimi en mesos eimi amfoteros o men o megeqos
english_head: by reason of being Simmias, but by reason of the greatness he happens to have; nor is he greater than Socrates because Socrates is Socrates, but because Socrates has smallness relatively to his greatness. True. And again, he is not smaller than Phaedo because Phaedo is Phaedo, but because Phaedo has greatness relatively to Simmias’s smallness. That is true. Then Simmias is called small and great, when he is between the two,

greek_head: ὑπερέχειν τὴν σμικρότητα ὑπέχων τῷ δὲ τὸ μέγεθος τῆς σμικρότητος παρέχων ὑπερέχον Καὶ ἅμα μειδιάσας Ἔοικα
greek_tokens: uperexw o mikroths upexw o de o megeqos o mikroths paraxwnnumi uperexw kai ama meidiaw eoika fhmi kai suggrafikos erew alla oun exis ge pou ws legos sumfhmi legos dh ode eneka boulomai doca sos osper egw egw gar fainw ou monos autos o megeqos oudepote eqelw ama megas kai mikros eimi alla kai o en egw megeqos oudepote prosdexomai o mikros oude eqelw uperexw alla duo o eteros h feugw kai upekxwrew otan autos
english_head: surpassing the smallness of the one by exceeding him in height, and granting to the other the greatness that exceeds his own smallness. And he laughed and said, I seem to he speaking like a legal document, but it really is very much as I say. Simmias agreed. I am speaking so because I want you to agree with me. I think it is evident not only that greatness itself will never be great and also small, but that the greatness in us will never admit the small or allow itself to be exceeded. One of two things must take place: either it flees or withdraws when

greek_head: προσίῃ τὸ ἐναντίον τὸ σμικρόν ἢ προσελθόντος ἐκείνου ἀπολωλέναι ὑπομένον δὲ καὶ δεξάμενον τὴν σμικρότητα
greek_tokens: proseimi o enantion o mikros h proserxomai ekeinos apollumi upomenw de kai dexomai o mikroths ou eqelw eimi eteros h osper eimi wsper egw dexomai kai upomenw o mikroths kai eti eimi os eimi outos o autos mikros eimi ekeinos de ou tolmaw megas eimi mikros eimi ws de autws kai o mikros o en egw ou eqelw pote megas gignomai oude eimi oude allos oudeis o enantios eti eimi osper eimi ama enantion gignomai te
english_head: its opposite, smallness, advances toward it, or it has already ceased to exist by the time smallness comes near it. But it will not receive and admit smallness, thereby becoming other than it was. So I have received and admitted smallness and am still the same small person I was; but the greatness in me, being great, has not suffered itself to become small. In the same way the smallness in us will never become or be great, nor will any other opposite which is still what it was, ever become or be also its own opposite. It either goes away or loses its existence in the change.

greek_head: καὶ εἶναι ἀλλ' ἤτοι ἀπέρχεται ἢ ἀπόλλυται ἐν τούτῳ τῷ παθήματι
greek_tokens: kai eimi alla htoi aperxomai h apollumi en outos o paqhma pantapasi fhmi o kebhs outws fainw egw kai tis eipon o pareimi aekwn ostis de eimi ou safhs mimnhskw pros qea ou en o prosqen egw logos autos o enantion o nuni legw omologew ek o elasswn o megas gignomai kai ek o megas o elasswn kai atexnos outos eimi o genesis o enantios ek o enantios nun de egw dokew legw ostis outos ou an pote gignomai kai o swkratew paraballw o kefalh kai aekwn
english_head: That, said Cebes, seems to me quite evident. Then one of those present—I don’t just remember who it was—said: In Heaven’s name, is not this present doctrine the exact opposite of what was fitted in our earlier discussion, that the greater is generated from the less and the less from the greater and that opposites are always generated from their opposites? But now it seems to me we are saying, this can never happen. Socrates cocked his head on one side and listened.

greek_head: Ἀνδρικῶς ἔφη ἀπεμνημόνευκας οὐ μέντοι ἐννοεῖς τὸ διαφέρον τοῦ τε νῦν λεγομένου καὶ τοῦ τότε τότε μὲν
greek_tokens: andrikos fhmi apomnhmoneuw ou mentoi ennoew o diaferw o te nun legw kai o tote tote men gar legw ek o enantios pragma o enantion pragma gignomai nun de ostis autos o enantion eautou enantion ou an pote gignomai oute o en egw oute o en o fusis tote men gar eimi filos peri o exw o enantios legw eponomazw autos o ekeinos epwnumios nun de peri ekeinos autos os eneimi exis o epwnumios o onomazw
english_head: You have spoken up like a man, he said, but you do not observe the difference between the present doctrine and what we said before. We said before that in the case of concrete things opposites are generated from opposites; whereas now we say that the abstract concept of an opposite can never become its own opposite, either in us or in the world about us. Then we were talking about things which possess opposite qualities and are called after them, but now about those very opposites the immanence of which gives the things their names. We say that these latter

greek_head: αὐτὰ δ' ἐκεῖνα οὐκ ἄν ποτέ φαμεν ἐθελῆσαι γένεσιν ἀλλήλων δέξασθαι Καὶ ἅμα βλέψας πρὸς τὸν Κέβητα
greek_tokens: autos de ekeinos ou an pote fhmi eqelw genesis allhlwn dexomai kai ama blepw pros o kebhta eipon ara mh pou eimi kebhs fhmi kai sos tis outos tarassw os ode eipon oude au fhmi o kebhs outws exw kaitoi outi legos ws ou polus egw tarassw sunomologew ara eimi de os aplos outos mhdepote enantion eautou o enantion eimi pantapasi fhmi eti dh egw kai ode skeptomai fhmi ei ara sunomologew qermos tis kalew kai yuxros egw ara osper xiwn kai pur
english_head: can never be generated from each other. At the same time he looked at Cebes and said: And you—are you troubled by any of our friends’ objections? No, said Cebes, not this time; though I confess that objections often do trouble me. Well, we are quite agreed, said Socrates, upon this, that an opposite can never be its own opposite. Entirely agreed, said Cebes. Now, said he, see if you agree with me in what follows: Is there something that you call heat and something you call cold? Yes. Are they the same as snow and fire?

greek_head: Μὰ Δί' οὐκ ἔγωγε Ἀλλ' ἕτερόν τι πυρὸς τὸ θερμὸν καὶ ἕτερόν τι χιόνος τὸ
greek_tokens: ma diw ou egw alla eteros tis pur o qermos kai eteros tis xiwn o yuxros nai alla ode ge oimh dokew su oudepote xiwn ge eimi dexomai o qermos wsper en o prosqen legw eti eimi osper eimi xiwn kai qermos alla proseimi o qermos h upekxwrew autos h apollumi panu ge kai o pur ge au proseimi o yuxroomai autos h upeceimi h apollumi ou mentoi pote tolmaw dexomai o yuxroths eti eimi osper eimi pur kai yuxros
english_head: No, not at all. But heat is a different thing from fire and cold differs from snow? Yes. Yet I fancy you believe that snow, if (to employ the form of phrase we used before) it admits heat, will no longer be what it was, namely snow, and also warm, but will either withdraw when heat approaches it or will cease to exist. Certainly. And similarly fire, when cold approaches it, will either withdraw or perish. It will never succeed in admitting cold and being still fire,

greek_head: Ἀληθῆ ἔφη λέγεις Ἔστιν ἄρα ἦ δ' ὅς περὶ ἔνια τῶν τοιούτων ὥστε μὴ
greek_tokens: alhqhs fhmi legw eimi ara eimi de os peri enioi o toioutos wste mh monos autos o eidos aciow o eautou onoma eis o aei xronos alla kai allos tis o eimi men ou ekeinos exis de o ekeinos morfh aei otan eimi eti de en ode isos eimi safhs o legos o gar perissos aei pou deomai outos o onoma tugxanw osper nun legw h ou panu ge ara monos o eimi outos gar erwtaw h kai allos
english_head: as it was before, and also cold. That is true, said he. The fact is, said he, in some such cases, that not only the abstract idea itself has a right to the same name through all time, but also something else, which is not the idea, but which always, whenever it exists, has the form of the idea. But perhaps I can make my meaning clearer by some examples. In numbers, the odd must always have the name of odd, must it not? Certainly. But is this the only thing so called (for this is what I mean to ask), or is there something else, which is not

greek_head: τι ὃ ἔστι μὲν οὐχ ὅπερ τὸ περιττόν ὅμως δὲ δεῖ αὐτὸ μετὰ τοῦ ἑαυτοῦ ὀνόματος καὶ τοῦτο καλεῖν ἀεὶ διὰ τὸ οὕτω
greek_tokens: tis o eimi men ou osper o perissos omws de deomai autos meta o eautou onoma kai outos kalew aei dia o outws fuw wste o perissos mhdepote apoleipw legos de autos eimi oios kai o trias pasxw kai allos polus skopaw de peri o trias ara ou dokew su o te eautou onoma aei prosagoreuteos eimi kai o o perissos eimi ou osper o trias alla omws outws fuw kai o trias kai o pemptos kai o hmisus o ariqmew apas wste
english_head: identical with the odd but nevertheless has a right to the name of odd in addition to its own name, because it is of such a nature that it is never separated from the odd? I mean, for instance, the number three, and there are many other examples. Take the case of three; do you not think it may always be called by its own name and also be called odd, which is not the same as three? Yet the number three and the number five and half of numbers in general are so constituted, that each of them is odd

greek_head: οὐκ ὢν ὅπερ τὸ περιττὸν ἀεὶ ἕκαστος αὐτῶν ἐστι περιττός καὶ αὖ τὰ δύο καὶ τὰ τέτταρα καὶ ἅπας ὁ ἕτερος αὖ στίχος
greek_tokens: ou eimi osper o perissos aei ekastos autos eimi perissos kai au o duo kai o tessares kai apas o eteros au stixos o ariqmew ou eimi osper o artios omws ekastos autos artios eimi aei sugxwrew h ou pws gar ou fhmi o toinun fhmi boulomai dhlow aqrew eimi de ode ostis fainw ou monos ekeinos o enantios allhlwn ou dexomai alla kai osos ou eimi allhlwn enantios exis aei enantios oude outos eoika dexomai ekeinos o idea o an o en autos eimi enantios eimi alla epeimi autos htoi
english_head: though not identified with the idea of odd. And in the same way two and four and all the other series of numbers are even, each of them, though not identical with evenness. Do you agree, or not? Of course, he replied. Now see what I want to make plain. This is my point, that not only abstract opposites exclude each other, but all things which, although not opposites one to another, always contain opposites; these also, we find, exclude the idea which is opposed to the idea contained in them,

greek_head: ἀπολλύμενα ἢ ὑπεκχωροῦντα ἢ οὐ φήσομεν τὰ τρία καὶ ἀπολεῖσθαι πρότερον καὶ ἄλλο ὁτιοῦν πείσεσθαι πρὶν ὑπομεῖναι
greek_tokens: apollumi h upekxwrew h ou fhmi o treis kai apollumi proteros kai allos otioun pasxw prin upomenw eti treis eimi artios gignomai panu men oun fhmi o kebhs oude meis eimi de os enantion ge eimi duas trias ou gar oun ou ara monos o eidos o enantios ou upomenw epeimi allhlwn alla kai allos atta o enantios ou upomenw epeimi alhqhs fhmi legw boulomai oun eimi de os ean oios su eimi orizw opoios outos eimi panu ge
english_head: and when it approaches they either perish or withdraw. We must certainly agree that the number three will endure destruction or anything else rather than submit to becoming even, while still remaining three, must we not? Certainly, said Cebes. But the number two is not the opposite of the number three. No. Then not only opposite ideas refuse to admit each other when they come near, but certain other things refuse to admit the approach of opposites. Very true, he said. Shall we then, said Socrates, determine if we can, what these are? Certainly.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ἔφη ὦ Κέβης τάδε εἴη ἄν ἃ ὅτι ἂν κατάσχῃ μὴ μόνον ἀναγκάζει τὴν αὑτοῦ ἰδέαν αὐτὸ ἴσχειν ἀλλὰ καὶ
greek_tokens: ara oun fhmi eimi kebhs ode eaw an o ostis an katexw mh monos anagkazw o eautou idea autos isxw alla kai enantios autos aei tis pws legw wsper arti legw oida gar dhpou ostis o an o o treis idea katexw anagkh autos ou monos treis eimi alla kai perissos panu ge epi o toioutos dh fhmi o enantios idea ekeinos o morfaw o an outos apergazomai oudepote an erxomai ou gar ergazomai de ge o perissos nai enantios de outos o o artios nai
english_head: Then, Cebes, will they be those which always compel anything of which they take possession not only to take their form but also that of some opposite? What do you mean? Such things as we were speaking of just now. You know of course that those things in which the number three is an essential element must be not only three but also odd. Certainly. Now such a thing can never admit the idea which is the opposite of the concept which produces this result. No, it cannot. But the result was produced by the concept of the odd? Yes. And the opposite of this is the idea

greek_head: Ἐπὶ τὰ τρία ἄρα ἡ τοῦ ἀρτίου ἰδέα οὐδέποτε ἥξει Οὐ δῆτα
greek_tokens: epi o treis ara o o artios idea oudepote hkw ou dhta amoiros dh o artios o treis amoiros anartios ara o trias nai o toinun elegos orizw poios ou enantios tis eimi omws ou dexomai autos o enantion oios nun o trias o artios ou eimi enantios oudeis tis mallon autos dexomai o gar enantion aei autos epiferw kai o duas o perissos kai
english_head: of the even? Yes. Then the idea of the even will never be admitted by the number three. No. Then three has no part in the even. No, it has none. Then the number three is uneven. Yes. Now I propose to determine what things, without being the opposites of something, nevertheless refuse to admit it, as the number three, though it is not the opposite of the idea of even, nevertheless refuses to admit it, but always brings forward its opposite against it, and

greek_head: τὸ πῦρ τῷ ψυχρῷ καὶ ἄλλα πάμπολλα ἀλλ' ὅρα δὴ εἰ οὕτως ὁρίζῃ μὴ μόνον τὸ ἐναντίον τὸ ἐναντίον μὴ δέχεσθαι
greek_tokens: o pur o yuxros kai allos pampolus alla oraw dh ei outws orizw mh monos o enantion o enantion mh dexomai alla kai ekeinos o an epiferw tis enantion ekeinos epi ostis an autos eimi autos o epiferw o o epiferw enantioths mhdepote dexomai palin de anamimnhskw ou gar xeirwn pollakis akouw o pente o o artios ou dexomai oude o deka o o perissos o diplasios outos men oun kai autos allos enantion omws de o
english_head: as the number two brings forward the opposite of the odd and fire that of cold, and so forth, for there are plenty of examples. Now see if you accept this statement: not only will opposites not admit their opposites, but nothing which brings an opposite to that which it approaches will ever admit in itself the oppositeness of that which is brought. Now let me refresh your memory; for there is no harm in repetition. The number five will not admit the idea of the even, nor will ten, the double of five, admit the idea of the odd. Now ten is not itself an opposite, and yet it will not admit the idea of the odd;

greek_head: τοῦ περιττοῦ οὐ δέξεται οὐδὲ δὴ τὸ ἡμιόλιον οὐδὲ τἆλλα τὰ τοιαῦτα τὸ ἥμισυ τὴν τοῦ ὅλου καὶ τριτημόριον αὖ
greek_tokens: o perissos ou dexomai oude dh o hmiolios oude allos o toioutos o hmisus o o oloc kai trithmorion au kai pas o toioutos eiper epomai te kai sundokew su outws panu sfodra kai sundokew fhmi kai epomai palin dh egw fhmi ek arxh legos kai mh egw o an erwtaw apokrinw alla mimeomai egw legos dh para os o proteros elegos apokrisls o asfalhs ekeinos ek o nun legw allos oraw asfaleia ei gar eromai egw os an tis en o swma eggignomai qermos eimi ou o
english_head: and so one-and-a-half and other mixed fractions and one-third and other simple fractions reject the idea of the whole. Do you go with me and agree to this? Yes, I agree entirely, he said, and am with you. Then, said Socrates, please begin again at the beginning. And do not answer my questions in their own words, but do as I do. I give an answer beyond that safe answer which I spoke of at first, now that I see another safe reply deduced from what has just been said. If you ask me what causes anything in which it is to be hot, I will not give

greek_head: ἀσφαλῆ σοι ἐρῶ ἀπόκρισιν ἐκείνην τὴν ἀμαθῆ ὅτι ᾧ ἂν θερμότης ἀλλὰ κομψοτέραν ἐκ τῶν νῦν ὅτι ᾧ ἂν πῦρ οὐδὲ
greek_tokens: asfalhs su eraomai apokrisls ekeinos o amaqhs ostis os an qermoths alla komyos ek o nun ostis os an pur oude an era os an swma tis eggignomai nosew ou eraomai ostis os an nosos alla os an puretos oude os an ariqmos tis eggignomai perissos eimi ou eraomai os an perissoths alla os an monas kai allos outws alla oraw ei hdh ikanos oistos ostis boulomai alla panu ikanos fhmi apokrinw dh eimi de os os an tis eggignomai swma zaw eimi os an yuxh fhmi
english_head: you that safe but stupid answer and say that it is heat, but I can now give a more refined answer, that it is fire; and if you ask, what causes the body in which it is to be ill, I shall not say illness, but fever; and if you ask what causes a number in which it is to be odd, I shall not say oddness, but the number one, and so forth. Do you understand sufficiently what I mean? Quite sufficiently, he replied. Now answer, said he. What causes the body in which it is to be alive? The soul, he replied.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἀεὶ τοῦτο οὕτως ἔχει Πῶς γὰρ οὐχί ἦ δ' ὅς
greek_tokens: oukoun aei outos outws exis pws gar ou eimi de os yuxh ara ostis an autos katexw aei hkw epi ekeinos ferw zwh hkw mentoi fhmi poteros de eimi tis zwh enantion h oudeis eimi fhmi tis qanatos oukoun yuxh o enantion os autos epiferw aei ou mh pote dexomai ws ek o prosqen omologew kai mala sfodra fhmi o kebhs tis oun o mh dexomai o o artios idea tis nundi onomazw anartios fhmi o de dikaios mh dexomai kai o an mousikos mh dexomai
english_head: Is this always the case? Yes, said he, of course. Then if the soul takes possession of anything it always brings life to it? Certainly, he said. Is there anything that is the opposite of life? Yes, said he. What? Death. Now the soul, as we have agreed before, will never admit the opposite of that which it brings with it. Decidedly not, said Cebes. Then what do we now call that which does not admit the idea of the even? Uneven, said he. And those which do not admit justice and music?

greek_head: Ἄμουσον ἔφη τὸ δὲ ἄδικον Εἶεν ὃ δ' ἂν θάνατον μὴ δέχηται τί καλοῦμεν
greek_tokens: amousos fhmi o de adikos eien o de an qanatos mh dexomai tis kalew aqanatos fhmi oukoun yuxh ou dexomai qanatos ou aqanatos ara yuxh aqanatos eien fhmi outos men dh apodeiknumi fhmi h pws dokew kai mala ge ikanos eimi swkraths tis oun eimi de os eimi kebhs ei o anartios anagkaion eimi
english_head: Unjust, he replied, and unmusical. Well then what do we call that which does not admit death? Deathless or immortal, he said. And the soul does not admit death? No. Then the soul is immortal. Yes. Very well, said he. Shall we say then that this is proved? Yes, and very satisfactorily, Socrates. Well then, Cebes, said he, if the odd were necessarily imperishable,

greek_head: ἀνωλέθρῳ εἶναι ἄλλο τι τὰ τρία ἢ ἀνώλεθρα ἂν ἦν Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: anwleqros eimi allos tis o treis h anwleqros an eimi pws gar ou oukoun ei kai o aqermos anagkaion eimi anwleqros eimi opote tis epi xiwn qermos epagw upeceimi an o xiwn eimi sos kai athktos ou gar an apollumi ge oude au upomenw dexomai an o qermoths alhqhs fhmi legw os de autws oimh kan ei o ayuktos anwleqros eimi opote epi o pur yuxros tis epeimi oupote an aposbennumi oude apollumi alla sew an aperxomai oixomai anagkh fhmi
english_head: would not the number three be imperishable? Of course. And if that which is without heat were imperishable, would not snow go away whole and unmelted whenever heat was brought in conflict with snow? For it could not have been destroyed, nor could it have remained and admitted the heat. That is very true, he replied. In the same way, I think, if that which is without cold were imperishable, whenever anything cold approached fire, it would never perish or be quenched, but would go away unharmed. Necessarily, he said.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ ὧδε ἔφη ἀνάγκη περὶ τοῦ ἀθανάτου εἰπεῖν εἰ μὲν τὸ ἀθάνατον καὶ ἀνώλεθρόν ἐστιν ἀδύνατον ψυχῇ
greek_tokens: oukoun kai wde fhmi anagkh peri o aqanatos eipon ei men o aqanatos kai anwleqros eimi adunatos yuxw otan qanatos epi autos eimi apollumi qanatos men gar dh ek o proerew ou dexomai oude eimi qnhskw wsper o treis ou eimi fhmi artios oude ge au o perissos oude dh pur yuxros oude ge o en o pur qermoths alla tis kwluw fhmi an tis artios men o perissos mh gignomai epeimi o artios wsper omologew
english_head: And must not the same be said of that which is immortal? If the immortal is also imperishable, it is impossible for the soul to perish when death comes against it. For, as our argument has shown, it will not admit death and will not be dead, just as the number three, we said, will not be even, and the odd will not be even, and as fire, and the heat in the fire, will not be cold. But, one might say, why is it not possible that the odd does not become even when the even comes against it (we agreed to that), but perishes,

greek_head: ἀπολομένου δὲ αὐτοῦ ἀντ' ἐκείνου ἄρτιον γεγονέναι τῷ ταῦτα λέγοντι οὐκ ἂν ἔχοιμεν διαμαχέσασθαι ὅτι οὐκ
greek_tokens: apollumi de autos anti ekeinos artios gignomai o outos legw ou an exw diamaxomai ostis ou apollumi o gar anartios ou anwleqros eimi epei ei outos omologew egw radios an diamaxomai ostis eperxomai o artios o perissos kai o treis oixomai apeimi kai peri pur kai qermos kai o allos outws an diamaxomai h ou panu men oun oukoun kai nun peri o aqanatos ei men egw omologew kai anwleqros eimi yuxh an eaw pros o aqanatos eimi
english_head: and the even takes its place? Now we cannot silence him who raises this question by saying that it does not perish, for the odd is not imperishable. If that were conceded to us, we could easily silence him by saying that when the even approaches, the odd and the number three go away; and we could make the corresponding reply about fire and heat and the rest, could we not? Certainly. And so, too, in the case of the immortal; if it is conceded that the immortal is imperishable, the soul would be imperishable as well as immortal,

greek_head: καὶ ἀνώλεθρος εἰ δὲ μή ἄλλου ἂν δέοι λόγου Ἀλλ' οὐδὲν δεῖ ἔφη τούτου γε ἕνεκα σχολῇ γὰρ ἄν
greek_tokens: kai anwleqros ei de mh allos an dew logos alla oudeis deomai fhmi outos ge eneka sxolazw gar an tis allos fqora mh dexomai ei o ge aqanatos aidios eimi fqora dexomai o de ge qeos oimh fhmi o swkratew kai autos o o zwh eidos kai ei tis allos aqanatos eimi para pas an omologew mhdepote apollumi para pas mentoi nh diw fhmi anqrwpos su ge kai eti mallon ws oimh para qea
english_head: but if not, further argument is needed. But, he said, it is not needed, so far as that is concerned; for surely nothing would escape destruction, if the immortal, which is everlasting, is perishable. All, I think, said Socrates, would agree that God and the Principle of life, and anything else that is immortal, can never perish. All men would, certainly, said he, and still more, I fancy, the Gods. Since, then, the immortal

greek_head: Ὁπότε δὴ τὸ ἀθάνατον καὶ ἀδιάφθορόν ἐστιν ἄλλο τι ψυχὴ ἤ εἰ ἀθάνατος τυγχάνει οὖσα καὶ ἀνώλεθρος
greek_tokens: opote dh o aqanatos kai adiafqoros eimi allos tis yuxh h ei aqanatos tugxanw eimi kai anwleqros an eaw polus anagkh epeimi ara qanatos epi o anqrwpos o men qnhtos ws eoika autos apoqnhskw o de aqanatos sew kai adiafqoros oixomai apeimi upekxwrew o qanatos fainw pas mallon ara fhmi eimi kebhs yuxh aqanatos kai
english_head: is also indestructible, would not the soul, if it is immortal, be also imperishable? Necessarily. Then when death comes to a man, his mortal part, it seems, dies, but the immortal part goes away unharmed and undestroyed, withdrawing from death. So it seems. Then, Cebes, said he, it is perfectly certain

greek_head: ἀνώλεθρον καὶ τῷ ὄντι ἔσονται ἡμῶν αἱ ψυχαὶ ἐν Ἅιδου Οὔκουν ἔγωγε ὦ Σώκρατες ἔφη ἔχω παρὰ ταῦτα ἄλλο
greek_tokens: anwleqros kai o eimi eimi egw o yuxh en adhs oukoun egw eimi swkraths fhmi exw para outos allos tis legw oude ph apistew o logos alla ei dh tis simmias ode h tis allos exis legw eu exis mh katasigaw ws ou oida eis ostis tis allos kairos anaballw h o nun pareimi peri o toioutos boulomai h tis eipon h akew alla meis eimi de os o simmias oude autos exw eti oph afisthmi ek ge o legw upo mentoi o megeqos peri
english_head: that the soul is immortal and imperishable, and our souls will exist somewhere in another world. I, said Cebes, have nothing more to say against that, and I cannot doubt your conclusions. But if Simmias, or anyone else, has anything to say, he would do well to speak, for I do not know to what other time than the present he could defer speaking, if he wishes to say or hear anything about those matters. But, said Simmias, I don’t see how I can doubt, either, as to the result of the discussion; but the subject is so great,

greek_head: ὧν οἱ λόγοι εἰσίν καὶ τὴν ἀνθρωπίνην ἀσθένειαν ἀτιμάζων ἀναγκάζομαι ἀπιστίαν ἔτι ἔχειν παρ' ἐμαυτῷ περὶ τῶν
greek_tokens: os e logos eimi kai o anqrwpinos asqeneia atimazw anagkazw apistia eti exw para emautou peri o erw ou monos ge fhmi eimi simmias o swkratew alla outos te eu legw kai o ge upoqesis o proteros kai ei pistos su eimi omws episkepteos safhs kai ean autos ikanos diairew ws oimh akolouqew o logos kaqa osos dunatos malista anqrwpos epakolouqew kan outos autos safhs gignomai oudeis zhtew peraiteros alhqhs fhmi legw
english_head: and I have such a poor opinion of human weakness, that I cannot help having some doubt in my own mind about what has been said. Not only that, Simmias, said Socrates, but our first assumptions ought to be more carefully examined, even though they seem to you to be certain. And if you analyze them completely, you will, I think, follow and agree with the argument, so far as it is possible for man to do so. And if this is made clear, you will seek no farther. That is true, he said. But my friends, he said, we ought to bear in mind,

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ τόδε γ' ἔφη ὦ ἄνδρες δίκαιον διανοηθῆναι ὅτι εἴπερ ἡ ψυχὴ ἀθάνατος ἐπιμελείας δὴ δεῖται οὐχ ὑπὲρ τοῦ
greek_tokens: alla ode ge fhmi eimi anhr dikaios dianoeomai ostis eiper o yuxh aqanatos epimeleia dh deomai ou uper o xronos outos monos en os kalew o zaw alla uper o pas kai o kindunos nun dh kai dokew an deinos eimi ei tis autos amelew ei men gar eimi o qanatos o pas apallagh ermaion an eimi o kakos apoqnhskw o te swma ama apallassw kai o eautou kakia meta o yuxazw nun de epei aqanatos fainw eimi oudeis an
english_head: that, if the soul is immortal, we must care for it, not only in respect to this time, which we call life, but in respect to all time, and if we neglect it, the danger now appears to be terrible. For if death were an escape from everything, it would be a boon to the wicked, for when they die they would be freed from the body and from their wickedness together with their souls. But now, since the soul is seen to be immortal, it cannot escape

greek_head: εἴη αὐτῇ ἄλλη ἀποφυγὴ κακῶν οὐδὲ σωτηρία πλὴν τοῦ ὡς βελτίστην τε καὶ φρονιμωτάτην γενέσθαι οὐδὲν γὰρ ἄλλο
greek_tokens: eaw autos allos apofugh kakh oude swthria plhn o ws beltistos te kai fronimos gignomai oudeis gar allos exw eis adhs o yuxh erxomai plhn o paideia te kai trefw o dh kai megas legw wfelew h blaptw o teleutaw euqus en arxh o ekeise poreia legw de outws ws ara teleutaw ekastos o ekastos daimwn os zaw lagxanw outos agw epixeirew eis dh tis topos e deomai o sunlegw diadikazw eis adhs
english_head: from evil or be saved in any other way than by becoming as good and wise as possible. For the soul takes with it to the other world nothing but its education and nurture, and these are said to benefit or injure the departed greatly from the very beginning of his journey thither. And so it is said that after death, the tutelary genius of each person, to whom he had been allotted in life, leads him to a place where the dead are gathered together; then they are judged and depart to the other world

greek_head: πορεύεσθαι μετὰ ἡγεμόνος ἐκείνου ᾧ δὴ προστέτακται τοὺς ἐνθένδε ἐκεῖσε πορεῦσαι τυχόντας δὲ ἐκεῖ ὧν δὴ τυχεῖν
greek_tokens: poreuw meta hgemwn ekeinos os dh prostassw o enqende ekeise poreuw tugxanw de ekei os dh tugxanw kai menw os xrh xronos allos deuro palin hgemwn komizw en polus xronos kai makros periodos eimi de ara o poreia ou ws o aisxulos thlefos legw ekeinos
english_head: with the guide whose task it is to conduct thither those who come from this world; and when they have there received their due and remained through the time appointed, another guide brings them back after many long periods of time. And the journey is not as Telephus says in the play of Aeschylus;

greek_head: μὲν γὰρ ἁπλῆν οἶμόν φησιν εἰς Ἅιδου φέρειν ἡ δ' οὔτε ἁπλῆ οὔτε μία φαίνεταί μοι εἶναι οὐδὲ γὰρ ἂν ἡγεμόνων
greek_tokens: men gar aploos oimos fhmi eis adhs ferw o de oute aploos oute eis fainw egw eimi oude gar an hgemwn dew ou gar pou tis an diamartanw oudamose eis odaw eimi nun de eoika sxisis te kai triodos polus exw apo o qusia te kai nomimos o enqade tekmairomai legos o men oun kosmios te kai fronimos yuxh epomai te kai ou agnoew o pareimi o de epiqumhtikos o swma exw osper en o emprosqen eipon peri ekeinos polus
english_head: for he says a simple path leads to the lower world, but I think the path is neither simple nor single, for if it were, there would be no need of guides, since no one could miss the way to any place if there were only one road. But really there seem to be many forks of the road and many windings; this I infer from the rites and ceremonies practiced here on earth. Now the orderly and wise soul follows its guide and understands its circumstances; but the soul that is desirous of the body, as I said before, flits about it, and in the visible world for a long time,

greek_head: χρόνον ἐπτοημένη καὶ περὶ τὸν ὁρατὸν τόπον πολλὰ ἀντιτείνασα καὶ πολλὰ παθοῦσα βίᾳ καὶ μόγις ὑπὸ τοῦ
greek_tokens: xronos ptoew kai peri o oratos topos polus antiteinw kai polus pasxw bia kai mogis upo o prostassw daimwn oixomai agw afikneomai de oqi o allos o men akaqartos kai tis poiew toioutos h fonos adikos aptw h allos atta toioutos ergazomai o outos adelfh te kai adelfh yuxos ergon tugxanw eimi outos men apas feugw te kai upektrepw kai oute sunemporos oute hgemwn eqelw gignomai autos
english_head: and after much resistance and many sufferings is led away with violence and with difficulty by its appointed genius. And when it arrives at the place where the other souls are, the soul which is impure and has done wrong, by committing wicked murders or other deeds akin to those and the works of kindred souls, is avoided and shunned by all, and no one is willing to be its companion or its guide,

greek_head: δὲ πλανᾶται ἐν πάσῃ ἐχομένη ἀπορίᾳ ἕως ἂν δή τινες χρόνοι γένωνται ὧν ἐλθόντων ὑπ' ἀνάγκης φέρεται εἰς τὴν
greek_tokens: de planaw en paomai exw aporia ews an dh tis xronos gignomai os erxomai upo anagkh ferw eis o autos prepw eoika o de kaqaros te kai metrios o bios diecerxomai kai sunemporos kai hgemwn qea tugxanw oikew o autos ekastos topos proshkw eimi de polus kai qaumastos o gaia topos kai autos oute oios oute osos docazw upo o peri gaia eqw legw ws egw upo tis peiqw
english_head: but it wanders about alone in utter bewilderment, during certain fixed times, after which it is carried by necessity to its fitting habitation. But the soul that has passed through life in purity and righteousness, finds gods for companions and guides, and goes to dwell in its proper dwelling. Now there are many wonderful regions of the earth, and the earth itself is neither in size nor in other respects such as it is supposed to be by those who habitually discourse about it, as I believe on someone’s authority.

greek_head: Καὶ ὁ Σιμμίας Πῶς ταῦτα ἔφη λέγεις ὦ Σώκρατες περὶ γάρ τοι γῆς καὶ αὐτὸς πολλὰ δὴ ἀκήκοα οὐ μέντοι
greek_tokens: kai o simmias pws outos fhmi legw eimi swkraths peri gar su gaia kai autos polus dh akouw ou mentoi outos o sos peiqw hdews oun an akouw alla mentoi eimi simmias ou o glaukos texnh ge egw dokew eimi dihgeomai o ge eimi ws mentoi alhqhs xalepos egw fainw h kata o glaukos texnh kai ama men egw isos oude an oios te eimi ama de ei kai epistamai o bios egw dokew o emos eimi simmias o mhkos o logos ou ecarkew o mentoi idea o gaia oios
english_head: And Simmias said, What do you mean, Socrates? I have heard a good deal about the earth myself, but not what you believe; so I should like to hear it. Well Simmias, I do not think I need the art of Glaucus to tell what it is. But to prove that it is true would, I think, be too hard for the art of Glaucus, and perhaps I should not be able to do it; besides, even if I had the skill, I think my life, Simmias, will end before the discussion could be finished. However, there is nothing to prevent my telling

greek_head: πέπεισμαι εἶναι καὶ τοὺς τόπους αὐτῆς οὐδέν με κωλύει λέγειν
greek_tokens: peiqw eimi kai o topos autos oudeis egw kwluw legw alla fhmi o simmias kai outos arkew peiqw toinun eimi de os egw ws proteros men ei eimi en mesos o ouranos periferhs eimi mhdeis autos dew mhte
english_head: what I believe the form of the earth to be, and the regions in it. Well, said Simmias, that will be enough. I am convinced, then, said he, that in the first place, if the earth is round and in the middle of the heavens, it needs neither the air

greek_head: ἀέρος πρὸς τὸ μὴ πεσεῖν μήτε ἄλλης ἀνάγκης μηδεμιᾶς τοιαύτης ἀλλὰ ἱκανὴν εἶναι αὐτὴν ἴσχειν τὴν ὁμοιότητα
greek_tokens: ahr pros o mh piptw mhte allos anagkh mhdeis toioutos alla ikanos eimi autos isxw o omoioths o ouranos autos eautou panth kai o gaia autos o isorropia isorropos gar pragma omoios tis en mesos tiqhmi ou ecis mallon oude hsswn oudamose klinw omoios de exw aklinhs menw proteros men toinun eimi de os outos peiqw kai orqos ge fhmi o simmias eti toinun fhmi pammegas tis eimi autos kai egw oikew
english_head: nor any other similar force to keep it from falling, but its own equipoise and the homogeneous nature of the heavens on all sides suffice to hold it in place; for a body which is in equipoise and is placed in the center of something which is homogeneous cannot change its inclination in any direction, but will remain always in the same position. This, then, is the first thing of which I am convinced. And rightly, said Simmias. Secondly, said he, I believe that the earth is very large and that we who dwell between the pillars of Hercules

greek_head: τοὺς μέχρι Ἡρακλείων στηλῶν ἀπὸ Φάσιδος ἐν σμικρῷ τινι μορίῳ ὥσπερ περὶ τέλμα μύρμηκας ἢ βατράχους περὶ
greek_tokens: o mexri hrakleios sthlh apo fasis en mikros tis morion wsper peri telma murmhc h batraxos peri o qalassa oikew kai allos alloqi polus en polus toioutos topos oikew eimi gar pantaxh peri o gaia polus koilos kai pantodapos kai o idea kai o megeqos eis o surrew o te udwr kai o omixlh kai o ahr autos de o gaia kaqaros en kaqaros kew o ouranos en os eimi o astron os dh aiqhr onomazw
english_head: and the river Phasis live in a small part of it about the sea, like ants or frogs about a pond, and that many other people live in many other such regions. For I believe there are in all directions on the earth many hollows of very various forms and sizes, into which the water and mist and air have run together; but the earth itself is pure and is situated in the pure heaven in which the stars are, the heaven which

greek_head: τοὺς πολλοὺς τῶν περὶ τὰ τοιαῦτα εἰωθότων λέγειν οὗ δὴ ὑποστάθμην ταῦτα εἶναι καὶ συρρεῖν ἀεὶ εἰς τὰ κοῖλα τῆς
greek_tokens: o polus o peri o toioutos eqw legw e dh upostaqmh outos eimi kai surrew aei eis o koilos o gaia egw oun oikew en o koilos autos lanqanw kai oiomai anoos epi o gaia oikew wsper an ei tis en mesos o puqmhn o pelagos oikew oiomai te epi o qalassa oikew kai dia o udwr oraw o hlios kai o allos astron o qalassa hgeomai ouranos eimi dia de
english_head: those who discourse about such matters call the ether; the water, mist and air are the sediment of this and flow together into the hollows of the earth. Now we do not perceive that we live in the hollows, but think we live on the upper surface of the earth, just as if someone who lives in the depth of the ocean should think he lived on the surface of the sea, and, seeing the sun and the stars through the water, should think the sea was the sky, and should, by reason of sluggishness or

greek_head: βραδυτῆτά τε καὶ ἀσθένειαν μηδεπώποτε ἐπὶ τὰ ἄκρα τῆς θαλάττης ἀφιγμένος μηδὲ ἑωρακὼς εἴη ἐκδὺς καὶ ἀνακύψας
greek_tokens: braduths te kai asqeneia mhdepwpote epi o akra o qalassa afizw mhde oraw eaw ekduw kai anakuptw ek o qalassa eis o enqade topos osos kaqaros kai kallos tugxanw eimi o para sfeis mhde allos akouw eaw o oraw tautos dh outos kai egw pasxw oikew gar en tis koilos o gaia oiomai epanw autos oikew kai o ahr ouranos kalew ws dia outos ouranos eimi o astron xwrew o de eimi tautos
english_head: feebleness, never have reached the surface of the sea, and should never have seen, by rising and lifting his head out of the sea into our upper world, and should never have heard from anyone who had seen, how much purer and fairer it is than the world he lived in. I believe this is just the case with us; for we dwell in a hollow of the earth and think we dwell on its upper surface; and the air we call the heaven, and think that is the heaven in which the stars move. But the fact is the same,

greek_head: ὑπ' ἀσθενείας καὶ βραδυτῆτος οὐχ οἵους τε εἶναι ἡμᾶς διεξελθεῖν ἐπ' ἔσχατον τὸν ἀέρα ἐπεί εἴ τις αὐτοῦ ἐπ' ἄκρα
greek_tokens: upo asqeneia kai braduths ou oios te eimi egw diecerxomai epi esxatos o ahr epei ei tis autos epi akra erxomai h pthnos gignomai anaptw kateidon an anakuptw wsper enqade e ek o qalassa ixqus anakuptw oraw o enqade outws an tis kai o ekei kateidon kai ei o fusis ikanos eaw anexw qewrew gignwskw an ostis ekeinos eimi o alhqhs ouranos kai o alhqinos faos
english_head: that by reason of feebleness and sluggishness, we are unable to attain to the upper surface of the air; for if anyone should come to the top of the air or should get wings and fly up, he could lift his head above it and see, as fishes lift their heads out of the water and see the things in our world, so he would see things in that upper world; and, if his nature were strong enough to bear the sight, he would recognize that that is the real heaven

greek_head: καὶ ἡ ὡς ἀληθῶς γῆ ἥδε μὲν γὰρ ἡ γῆ καὶ οἱ λίθοι καὶ ἅπας ὁ τόπος ὁ ἐνθάδε διεφθαρμένα ἐστὶν καὶ καταβεβρωμένα
greek_tokens: kai o ws alhqhs gaia ode men gar o gaia kai e liqos kai apas o topos o enqade diafqeirw eimi kai katabibrwskw wsper o en o qalassa upo o almh kai oute fuw acios logos oudeis en o qalassa oute teleios ws epos eipon oudeis eimi shragc de kai amos kai phlos amhxanos kai borboros eimi opou an kai o gaia eimi kai pros o para egw kallos krinw oude opwsoun acios ekeinos de au o para egw polus an eti pleos fainw diaferw
english_head: and the real light and the real earth. For this earth of ours, and the stones and the whole region where we live, are injured and corroded, as in the sea things are injured by the brine, and nothing of any account grows in the sea, and there is, one might say, nothing perfect there, but caverns and sand and endless mud and mire, where there is earth also, and there is nothing at all worthy to be compared with the beautiful things of our world. But the things in that world above would be seen to be even more superior to those in this world of ours.

greek_head: εἰ γὰρ δὴ καὶ μῦθον λέγειν καλόν ἄξιον ἀκοῦσαι ὦ Σιμμία οἷα τυγχάνει τὰ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ὑπὸ τῷ οὐρανῷ ὄντα
greek_tokens: ei gar dh kai muqos legw kalos acios akew eimi simmias oios tugxanw o epi o gaia upo o ouranos eimi alla meis fhmi o simmias eimi swkraths egw ge outos o muqos hdews an akouw legw toinun fhmi eimi etairos proteros men eimi toioutos o gaia autos eidon ei tis anwqen qeaomai wsper o dwdekaskutos sfaira poikilos xrwma dialambanw os kai o enqade eimi xrwma wsper deigma os dh e grafeus
english_head: If I may tell a story, Simmias, about the things on the earth that is below the heaven, and what they are like, it is well worth hearing. By all means, Socrates, said Simmias; we should be glad to hear this story. Well then, my friend, said he, to begin with, the earth when seen from above is said to look like those balls that are covered with twelve pieces of leather; it is divided into patches of various colors, of which the colors which we see here may be regarded as samples, such as painters use.

greek_head: καταχρῶνται ἐκεῖ δὲ πᾶσαν τὴν γῆν ἐκ τοιούτων εἶναι καὶ πολὺ ἔτι ἐκ λαμπροτέρων καὶ καθαρωτέρων ἢ τούτων τὴν
greek_tokens: kataxraomai ekei de pas o gaia ek toioutos eimi kai polus eti ek lampros kai kaqaros h outos o men gar alourghs eimi kai qaumasths o kallos o de xrusoeidhs o de osos leukos guyos h xiwn leukos kai ek o allos xrwma sugkeimai wsautws kai eti pleiwn kai kalos h osos egw oraw kai gar autos outos o koilos autos udwr te kai ahr ekpleos
english_head: But there the whole earth is of such colors, and they are much brighter and purer than ours; for one part is purple of wonderful beauty, and one is golden, and one is white, whiter than chalk or snow, and the earth is made up of the other colors likewise, and they are more in number and more beautiful than those which we see here. For those very hollows of the earth which are full of water and air, present an appearance

greek_head: ὄντα χρώματός τι εἶδος παρέχεσθαι στίλβοντα ἐν τῇ τῶν ἄλλων χρωμάτων ποικιλίᾳ ὥστε ἕν τι αὐτῆς εἶδος συνεχὲς
greek_tokens: eimi xrwma tis eidos parexw stilbw en o o allos xrwma poikilia wste eis tis autos eidos sunexhs poikilos fantazomai en de outos eimi toioutos ana logos o fuw fuw dendron te kai anqh kai o karpos kai au o oros wsautws kai o liqos exw ana o autos logos o te leioths kai o diafaneia kai o xrwma kallias os kai o enqade liqidion eimi outos o agapaw morion sardion te kai iaspis kai smaragdos
english_head: of color as they glisten amid the variety of the other colors, so that the whole produces one continuous effect of variety. And in this fair earth the things that grow, the trees, and flowers and fruits, are correspondingly beautiful; and so too the mountains and the stones are smoother, and more transparent and more lovely in color than ours. In fact, our highly prized stones, sards and

greek_head: καὶ πάντα τὰ τοιαῦτα ἐκεῖ δὲ οὐδὲν ὅτι οὐ τοιοῦτον εἶναι καὶ ἔτι τούτων καλλίω τὸ δ' αἴτιον τούτου εἶναι ὅτι ἐκεῖνοι οἱ
greek_tokens: kai pas o toioutos ekei de oudeis ostis ou toioutos eimi kai eti outos kallias o de aitios outos eimi ostis ekeinos e liqos eimi kaqaros kai ou katesqiw oude diafqeirw wsper e enqade upo shpedwn kai almh upo o deuro surrew o kai liqos kai gaia kai o allos zwion te kai futon aisxos te kai nosos paraxew o de gaia autos kosmew outos te apas kai eti xruseos te kai arguros kai
english_head: jaspers, and emeralds, and other gems, are fragments of those there, but there everything is like these or still more beautiful. And the reason of this is that there the stones are pure, and not corroded or defiled, as ours are, with filth and brine by the vapors and liquids which flow together here and which cause ugliness and disease in earth and stones and animals and plants. And the earth there is adorned with all the jewels and also with gold and
