greek_tokens: o allos au o toioutos ekfanhs gar autos fuw eimi polus plhqw kai megas kai pantaxou o gaia wste autos eidon eimi qeama eudaimwn qeaths zwion de epi autos eimi allos te polus kai anqrwpos o men en mesogaia oikew o de peri o ahr wsper egw peri o qalassa o de en new os perirrew o ahr pros o hpeiros eimi kai eis logos osper egw o udwr te kai o qalassa eimi pros o hmeteros xreia outos ekei
english_head: silver and everything of the sort. For there they are in plain sight, abundant and large and in many places, so that the earth is a sight to make those blessed who look upon it. And there are many animals upon it, and men also, some dwelling inland, others on the coasts of the air, as we dwell about the sea, and others on islands, which the air flows around, near the mainland; and in short, what water and the sea are

greek_tokens: o ahr o de egw ahr ekeinos o aiqhr o de oraw autos krasis exw toioutos wste ekeinos anosos eimi kai xronos te zaw polus plew o enqade kai oraw kai akoh kai fronhsis kai pas o toioutos egw afisthmi o autos afisthmi hper ahr te udwr afisthmi kai aiqhr ahr pros kaqaroths kai dh kai qea alsis te kai iera autos eimi en os o eimi oikhths qeos eimi kai fhmh te kai manteia kai aisqhsis o qea kai toioutos sunousia
english_head: in our lives, air is in theirs, and what the air is to us, ether is to them. And the seasons are so tempered that people there have no diseases and live much longer than we, and in sight and hearing and wisdom and all such things are as much superior to us as air is purer than water or the ether than air. And they have sacred groves and temples of the gods, in which the gods really dwell, and they have intercourse with the gods by speech and prophecies and visions,

greek_head: γίγνεσθαι αὐτοῖς πρὸς αὐτούς καὶ τόν γε ἥλιον καὶ σελήνην καὶ ἄστρα ὁρᾶσθαι ὑπ' αὐτῶν οἷα τυγχάνει ὄντα καὶ τὴν

english_head: and they see the sun and moon and stars as they really are, and in all other ways their blessedness is in accord with this. Such then is the nature of the earth as a whole, and of the things around it. But round about the whole earth, in the hollows of it, are many regions, some deeper and wider than that in which we live,

greek_head: τόπου ἔστι δ' οὓς καὶ βραχυτέρους τῷ βάθει τοῦ ἐνθάδε εἶναι καὶ πλατυτέρους τούτους δὲ πάντας ὑπὸ γῆν εἰς
greek_tokens: topos eimi de os kai braxus o baqos o enqade eimi kai platus outos de pas upo gaia eis allhlwn suntetrainw te pollaxh kai kata stenos kai eurus kai diecodos exw h polus men udwr rew ek allhlwn eis allhlwn wsper eis krathr kai aenaos potamos amhxanos megeqos upo o gaia kai qerma udwr kai yuxra polus de pur kai pur megas potamos polus de ugros phloomai kai kaqaros kai borborwdhs
english_head: some deeper but with a narrower opening than ours, and some also less in depth and wider. Now all these are connected with one another by many subterranean channels, some larger and some smaller, which are bored in all of them, and there are passages through which much water flows from one to another as into mixing bowls; and there are everlasting rivers of huge size under the earth, flowing with hot and cold water; and there is much fire, and great rivers of fire, and many streams of mud, some thinner

greek_head: ὥσπερ ἐν Σικελίᾳ οἱ πρὸ τοῦ ῥύακος πηλοῦ ῥέοντες ποταμοὶ καὶ αὐτὸς ὁ ῥύαξ ὧν δὴ καὶ ἑκάστους τοὺς τόπους

english_head: and some thicker, like the rivers of mud that flow before the lava in Sicily, and the lava itself. These fill the various regions as they happen to flow to one or another at any time. Now a kind of oscillation within the earth moves all these up and down. And the nature of the oscillation is as follows: One of the chasms of the earth is greater than the rest,

greek_head: μέγιστον τυγχάνει ὂν καὶ διαμπερὲς τετρημένον δι' ὅλης τῆς γῆς τοῦτο ὅπερ Ὅμηρος εἶπε λέγων αὐτό

english_head: and is bored right through the whole earth; this is the one which Homer means when he says: Far off, the lowest abyss beneath the earth; and which elsewhere he and many other poets have called Tartarus. For all the rivers flow together into this chasm and flow out of it again, and they have each the nature of the earth through which they flow. And the reason why all the streams flow in and out here

greek_head: αἰτία ἐστὶν τοῦ ἐκρεῖν τε ἐντεῦθεν καὶ εἰσρεῖν πάντα τὰ ῥεύματα ὅτι πυθμένα οὐκ ἔχει οὐδὲ βάσιν τὸ ὑγρὸν τοῦτο
greek_tokens: aitios eimi o ekrew te enteuqen kai eisrew pas o reuma ostis puqmhn ou exis oude basis o ugros outos aiwrew dh kai kumainw anoos kai katos kai o ahr kai o pneuma o peri autos tautos poiew sunepomai gar autos kai otan eis o epi ekeinos o gaia ormhsis kai otan eis o epi ode kai wsper o anapnew aei ekpnew te kai anapnew reon o pneuma outws kai ekei sunaiwreomai o ugrazw o pneuma deinos tis anemos kai amhxanos parexw kai
english_head: is that this liquid matter has no bottom or foundation. So it oscillates and waves up and down, and the air and wind about it do the same; for they follow the liquid both when it moves toward the other side of the earth and when it moves toward this side, and just as the breath of those who breathe blows in and out, so the wind there oscillates with the liquid and causes terrible and irresistible blasts as it rushes in and out.

greek_head: εἰσιὸν καὶ ἐξιόν ὅταν τε οὖν ὑποχωρήσῃ τὸ ὕδωρ εἰς τὸν τόπον τὸν δὴ κάτω καλούμενον τοῖς κατ' ἐκεῖνα τὰ ῥεύματα
greek_tokens: eiseimi kai eceimi otan te oun upoxwrhsis o udwr eis o topos o dh katos kalew o kata ekeinos o reuma dia o gaia eisrew te kai plhrow autos wsper e epantlew otan te au ekeiqen men apoleipw deuro de ormhsis o enqade plhrow auqis o de plhrow reomai dia o oxetos kai dia o gaia kai eis o topos ekastos afikneomai eis os ekastos odopoiew qalassa te kai limnh kai potamos kai krainw poiew enteuqen de palin duw
english_head: And when the water retires to the region which we call the lower, it flows into the rivers there and fills them up, as if it were pumped into them; and when it leaves that region and comes back to this side, it fills the rivers here; and when the streams are filled they flow through the passages and through the earth and come to the various places to which their different paths lead, where they make seas and marshes, and rivers and springs. Thence they go down again under the earth,

greek_head: κατὰ τῆς γῆς τὰ μὲν μακροτέρους τόπους περιελθόντα καὶ πλείους τὰ δὲ ἐλάττους καὶ βραχυτέρους πάλιν εἰς τὸν
greek_tokens: kata o gaia o men makros topos perierxomai kai plews o de elasswn kai braxus palin eis o tartaros emballw o men polus katos h h epantlew o de oligos pas de upokatw eisrew o ekroh kai enioi men katantikru h h eisrew ekpitnw enioi de kata o autos meros eimi de o pantapasi kuklos perierxomai h apac h kai pleonakis perielissw peri o gaia wsper e ofis eis o dunatos katos kaqihmi palin emballw
english_head: some passing around many great regions and others around fewer and smaller places, and flow again into Tartarus, some much below the point where they were sucked out, and some only a little; but all flow in below their exit. Some flow in on the side from which they flowed out, others on the opposite side; and some pass completely around in a circle, coiling about the earth once or several times, like serpents, then descend to the lowest possible depth and fall again into the chasm.

greek_head: δυνατὸν δέ ἐστιν ἑκατέρωσε μέχρι τοῦ μέσου καθιέναι πέρα δ' οὔ ἄναντες γὰρ ἀμφοτέροις τοῖς ῥεύμασι τὸ ἑκατέρωθεν
greek_tokens: dunatos de eimi ekaterwse mexri o meshs kaqihmi pera de ou anaantomai gar amfoteros o reuma o ekaterwqen gignomai meros o men oun dh allos polus te kai megas kai pantodapos reuma eimi tugxanw de ara eimi en outos o polus tessares atta reuma os o men megas kai ecwtatw reon peri kuklos o kalew wkeanos eimi outos de katantikru kai enantios reon axerwn os dia erhmos te topos
english_head: Now it is possible to go down from each side to the center, but not beyond, for there the slope rises forward in front of the streams from either side of the earth. Now these streams are many and great and of all sorts, but among the many are four streams, the greatest and outermost of which is that called Oceanus, which flows round in a circle, and opposite this, flowing in the opposite direction, is Acheron, which flows through

greek_head: ῥεῖ ἄλλων καὶ δὴ καὶ ὑπὸ γῆν ῥέων εἰς τὴν λίμνην ἀφικνεῖται τὴν Ἀχερουσιάδα οὗ αἱ τῶν τετελευτηκότων ψυχαὶ τῶν
greek_tokens: reomai allos kai dh kai upo gaia reon eis o limnh afikneomai o axerousias e o o teleutaw yuxh o polus afikneomai kai tis meiromai xronos menw o men makros o de braxus palin ekpempw eis o o zwion genesis tritos de potamos outos kata mesos ekballw kai eggus o ekboleus ekpitnw eis topos megas pur polus kaiw kai limnh poiew megas o para egw qalassa zew udwr kai phloomai enteuqen de
english_head: various desert places and, passing under the earth, comes to the Acherusian lake. To this lake the souls of most of the dead go and, after remaining there the appointed time, which is for some longer and for others shorter, are sent back to be born again into living beings. The third river flows out between these two, and near the place whence it issues it falls into a vast region burning with a great fire and makes a lake larger than our Mediterranean sea, boiling with water and mud.

greek_head: χωρεῖ κύκλῳ θολερὸς καὶ πηλώδης περιελιττόμενος δὲ τῇ γῇ ἄλλοσέ τε ἀφικνεῖται καὶ παρ' ἔσχατα τῆς Ἀχερουσιάδος
greek_tokens: xwrew kuklos qoleros kai phlwdhs perielissw de o gaia allote te afikneomai kai para esxatos o axerousias limnh ou summignumi o udwr perielissw de pollakis upo gaia emballw katos o tartarow outos de eimi os eponomazw puriflegeqwn e kai e ruac apospasma anafusaw oph an tugxanw o gaia outos de au katantikru o tetartos ekpitnw eis topos proteros deinos te kai agrios ws legw xrwma de exw oloc oios o
english_head: Thence it flows in a circle, turbid and muddy, and comes in its winding course, among other places, to the edge of the Acherusian lake, but does not mingle with its water. Then, after winding about many times underground, it flows into Tartarus at a lower level. This is the river which is called Pyriphlegethon, and the streams of lava which spout up at various places on earth are offshoots from it. Opposite this the fourth river issues, it is said, first into a wild and awful place, which is all of a dark blue color, like lapis lazuli.

greek_head: κυανός ὃν δὴ ἐπονομάζουσι Στύγιον καὶ τὴν λίμνην ἣν ποιεῖ ὁ ποταμὸς ἐμβάλλων Στύγα ὁ δ' ἐμπεσὼν ἐνταῦθα
greek_tokens: kuanos os dh eponomazw stugew kai o limnh os poiew o potamos emballw stugos o de empitnw entauqa kai deinos dunamis lambanw en o udwr duw kata o gaia perielissw xwrew enantios o puriflegeqwn kai apantaw en o axerousias limnh ek enantios kai oude o outos udwr oudeis mignumi alla kai outos kuklos perierxomai emballw eis o tartaros enantios o puriflegeqwn onoma de outos eimi ws e poihths legw kwkutos
english_head: This is called the Stygian river, and the lake which it forms by flowing in is the Styx. And when the river has flowed in here and has received fearful powers into its waters, it passes under the earth and, circling round in the direction opposed to that of Pyriphlegethon, it meets it coming from the other way in the Acherusian lake. And the water of this river also mingles with no other water, but this also passes round in a circle and falls into Tartarus opposite Pyriphlegethon. And the name of this river, as the Poets say, is Cocytus.

greek_head: Τούτων δὲ οὕτως πεφυκότων ἐπειδὰν ἀφίκωνται οἱ τετελευτηκότες εἰς τὸν τόπον οἷ ὁ δαίμων ἕκαστον κομίζει
greek_tokens: outos de outws fuw epeidan afikneomai e teleutaw eis o topos e o daimwn ekastos komizw proteros men diadikazw e te kalos kai osios biow kai e mh kai e men an dokew mesos biow poreuw epi o axerwn anabainw o dh autos oxhma eimi epi outos afikneomai eis o limnh kai ekei oikew te kai kaqairw o te adikhma didwmi dikh apoluw ei tis tis adikew o te euergesia
english_head: Such is the nature of these things. Now when the dead have come to the place where each is led by his genius, first they are judged and sentenced, as they have lived well and piously, or not. And those who are found to have lived neither well nor ill, go to the Acheron and, embarking upon vessels provided for them, arrive in them at the lake; there they dwell and are purified, and if they have done any wrong they are absolved by paying the penalty for their wrong doings,

greek_head: τιμὰς φέρονται κατὰ τὴν ἀξίαν ἕκαστος οἳ δ' ἂν δόξωσιν ἀνιάτως ἔχειν διὰ τὰ μεγέθη τῶν ἁμαρτημάτων ἢ ἱεροσυλίας
greek_tokens: timh ferw kata o acios ekastos e de an dokew aniatos exw dia o megeqos o amarthma h ierosulia polus kai megas h fonos adikos kai paranomos polus ecergazomai h allos osos toioutos tugxanw eimi outos de o prosakouw moira riptw eis o tartaros oqen oupote ekbainw e de an iasimos men megas de dokew amartanw amarthma oios pros pathr h mhthr
english_head: and for their good deeds they receive rewards, each according to his merits. But those who appear to be incurable, on account of the greatness of their wrongdoings, because they have committed many great deeds of sacrilege, or wicked and abominable murders, or any other such crimes, are cast by their fitting destiny into Tartarus, whence they never emerge. Those, however, who are curable, but are found to have committed great sins—who have, for example, in a moment of passion done some act of violence against father or mother and

greek_tokens: upo orgaw biaios tis prassw kai metamelomai autos o allos bios biaw h androfonos toioutos tis allos tropos gignomai outos de empitnw men eis o tartaros anagkh empitnw de autos kai eniautos ekei gignomai ekballw o kuma o men androfonos kata o kwkutos o de patraloias kai mhtraloias kata o puriflegeqwn epeidan de ferw gignomai kata o limnh o axerousias entauqa boaw te kai kalew e men os apokteinw e de os ubrizw kalew de iketeuw
english_head: have lived in repentance the rest of their lives, or who have slain some other person under similar conditions—these must needs be thrown into Tartarus, and when they have been there a year the wave casts them out, the homicides by way of Cocytus, those who have outraged their parents by way of Pyriphlegethon. And when they have been brought by the current to the Acherusian lake, they shout and cry out, calling to those whom they have slain or outraged, begging and beseeching them

greek_head: καὶ δέονται ἐᾶσαι σφᾶς ἐκβῆναι εἰς τὴν λίμνην καὶ δέξασθαι καὶ ἐὰν μὲν πείσωσιν ἐκβαίνουσί τε καὶ λήγουσι τῶν
greek_tokens: kai deomai eaw sfazw ekbainw eis o limnh kai dexomai kai ean men peiqw ekbainw te kai lhgw o kakh ei de mh ferw auqis eis o tartaros kai ekeiqen palin eis o potamos kai outos pasxw ou proteros pauw prin an peiqw os adikew outos gar o dikh upo o dikasths autos etazw e de dh an dokew diaferontws pros o osios biow outos eimi e ode men o topos o en o gaia eleuqerow te kai apallassw
english_head: to be gracious and to let them come out into the lake; and if they prevail they come out and cease from their ills, but if not, they are borne away again to Tartarus and thence back into the rivers, and this goes on until they prevail upon those whom they have wronged; for this is the penalty imposed upon them by the judges. But those who are found to have excelled in holy living are freed from these regions within the earth and are released as from prisons;

greek_head: ὥσπερ δεσμωτηρίων ἄνω δὲ εἰς τὴν καθαρὰν οἴκησιν ἀφικνούμενοι καὶ ἐπὶ γῆς οἰκιζόμενοι τούτων δὲ αὐτῶν οἱ
greek_tokens: wsper desmwthrion anoos de eis o kaqaros eoika afikneomai kai epi gaia oikizw outos de autos e filosofia ikanos kaqairw aneu te swma zaw o parapan eis o epeita xronos kai eis oikhsis eti outos kalliwn afikneomai os oute radios dhlow oute o xronos ikanos en o pareimi alla outos dh eneka xrh os dierxomai eimi simmias pas poiew wste aretaw kai fronhsis en o bios metexw kalos gar o aqlon kai o elpis megas
english_head: they mount upward into their pure abode and dwell upon the earth. And of these, all who have duly purified themselves by philosophy live henceforth altogether without bodies, and pass to still more beautiful abodes which it is not easy to describe, nor have we now time enough. But, Simmias, because of all these things which we have recounted we ought to do our best to acquire virtue and wisdom in life. For the prize is fair and the hope great.

greek_head: Τὸ μὲν οὖν ταῦτα διισχυρίσασθαι οὕτως ἔχειν ὡς ἐγὼ διελήλυθα οὐ πρέπει νοῦν ἔχοντι ἀνδρί ὅτι μέντοι ἢ ταῦτ'
greek_tokens: o men oun outos diisxurizomai outws exw ws egw dierxomai ou prepw new exw andris ostis mentoi h outos eimi h toioutos atta peri o yuxh egw kai o oikhsis epei aqanatos ge o yuxh fainw eimi outos kai prepw egw dokew kai acios kinduneuw oiomai outws exw kalos gar o kindunos kai xrh o toioutos wsper epaeidw eautou dio dh egw kai pala mhkunw o muqos alla outos dh eneka qarsew xrh peri o eautou yuxw
english_head: Now it would not be fitting for a man of sense to maintain that all this is just as I have described it, but that this or something like it is true concerning our souls and their abodes, since the soul is shown to be immortal, I think he may properly and worthily venture to believe; for the venture is well worth while; and he ought to repeat such things to himself as if they were magic charms, which is the reason why I have been lengthening out the story so long. This then is why a man should be of good cheer about his soul, who in his life

greek_head: ἄνδρα ὅστις ἐν τῷ βίῳ τὰς μὲν ἄλλας ἡδονὰς τὰς περὶ τὸ σῶμα καὶ τοὺς κόσμους εἴασε χαίρειν ὡς ἀλλοτρίους τε
greek_tokens: anhr ostis en o bios o men allos hdona o peri o swma kai o kosmos eaw xairw ws allotrios te eimi kai pleos eteros hgeomai apergazomai o de peri o manqanw spoudazw te kai kosmew o yuxh ou allotrios alla o autos kosmos swfrosunos te kai
english_head: has rejected the pleasures and ornaments of the body, thinking they are alien to him and more likely to do him harm than good, and has sought eagerly for those of learning, and after adorning his soul with no alien ornaments, but with its own proper adornment of self-restraint and justice and

greek_head: δικαιοσύνῃ καὶ ἀνδρείᾳ καὶ ἐλευθερίᾳ καὶ ἀληθείᾳ οὕτω περιμένει τὴν εἰς Ἅιδου πορείαν ὡς πορευσόμενος ὅταν ἡ
greek_tokens: dikaiosunh kai andreia kai eleuqeria kai alhqeia outws perimenw o eis adhs poreia ws poreuw otan o meiromai kalew su men oun fhmi eimi simmias te kai kebhs kai e allos eis auqis en tis xronos ekastos poreuw egw de nun hdh kalew fhmi an anhr tragikos o meiromai kai sxedon tis egw oraw trepw pros o loutron dokew gar dh beltiwn eimi louw pinw o farmakon kai mh pragma o gunh parexw nekros louw
english_head: courage and freedom and truth, awaits his departure to the other world, ready to go when fate calls him. You, Simmias and Cebes and the rest, he said, will go hereafter, each in his own time; but I am now already, as a tragedian would say, called by fate, and it is about time for me to go to the bath; for I think it is better to bathe before drinking the poison, that the women may not have the trouble of bathing the corpse. When he had finished speaking, Crito said:

greek_head: Ταῦτα δὴ εἰπόντος αὐτοῦ ὁ Κρίτων Εἶεν ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες τί δὲ τούτοις ἢ ἐμοὶ ἐπιστέλλεις ἢ περὶ τῶν
greek_tokens: outos dh eipon autos o kritwn eien fhmi eimi swkraths tis de outos h egw epistellw h peri o pais h peri allos tis ostis an su poiew egw en xaris malista poiew o aei legos fhmi eimi kritwn oudeis kainos ostis su autos epimeleomai su kai egw kai o emew kai su autos en xaris poiew ostis an poiew kan mh nun omologew ean de su men autos amelew kai mh aqelhtos wsper kata ixnos kata o nun te erw kai o en o emprosqen xronos zaw oude ean polus omologew
english_head: Well, Socrates, do you wish to leave any directions with us about your children or anything else—anything we can do to serve you? What I always say, Crito, he replied, nothing new. If you take care of yourselves you will serve me and mine and yourselves, whatever you do, even if you make no promises now; but if you neglect yourselves and are not willing to live following step by step, as it were, in the path marked out by our present and past discussions, you will accomplish nothing,

greek_head: ἐν τῷ παρόντι καὶ σφόδρα οὐδὲν πλέον ποιήσετε Ταῦτα μὲν τοίνυν προθυμησόμεθα ἔφη οὕτω ποιεῖν
greek_tokens: en o pareimi kai sfodra oudeis pleos poiew outos men toinun proqumeomai fhmi outws poiew qaptw de su tis tropos opws an fhmi boulomai ean ge laptw egw kai mh ekfeugw su gelaw de ama hsuxaw kai pros egw apoblepw eipon ou peiqw eimi anhr kritwna ws egw eimi outos swkratew o nuni dialegw kai diatassw ekastos o legw alla oiomai egw ekeinos eimi
english_head: no matter how much or how eagerly you promise at present. We will certainly try hard to do as you say, he replied. But how shall we bury you? However you please, he replied, if you can catch me and I do not get away from you. And he laughed gently, and looking towards us, said: I cannot persuade Crito, my friends, that the Socrates who is now conversing and arranging the details of his argument is really I; he thinks I am the one whom he will presently see as a corpse,

greek_head: ὃν ὄψεται ὀλίγον ὕστερον νεκρόν καὶ ἐρωτᾷ δὴ πῶς με θάπτῃ ὅτι δὲ ἐγὼ πάλαι πολὺν λόγον πεποίημαι ὡς
greek_tokens: os oraw oligos usteron nekros kai erwtaw dh pws egw qaptw ostis de egw pala polus logos poiew ws epeidan pinw o farmakon ouketi su paramenw alla oixomai apeimi eis makar dh tis eudaimonia outos egw dokazw autos allos legw paramuqeomai ama men su ama de emautou egguaw oun egw pros kritwna fhmi o enantios eggua h os outos pros o dikasths egguaw outos men gar eimi meis paramenw su de eimi meis mh paramenw egguaw epeidan apoqnhskw alla oixomai
english_head: and he asks how to bury me. And though I have been saying at great length that after I drink the poison I shall no longer be with you, but shall go away to the joys of the blessed you know of, he seems to think that was idle talk uttered to encourage you and myself. So, he said, give security for me to Crito, the opposite of that which he gave the judges at my trial; for he gave security that I would remain, but you must give security that I shall not remain when I die,

greek_head: ἀπιόντα ἵνα Κρίτων ῥᾷον φέρῃ καὶ μὴ ὁρῶν μου τὸ σῶμα ἢ καόμενον ἢ κατορυττόμενον ἀγανακτῇ ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ
greek_tokens: apeimi ina kritwn raios ferw kai mh oraw egw o swma h kaiw h katorussw aganaktew uper egw ws deinos pasxw mhde legos en o qaptw ws h prostiqhmi swkratew h ekferw h katorussw eu gar eimi eimi de os eimi aristos kritwn o mh kalos legw ou monos eis autos outos plhmmelhs alla kai kakos tis empoiew o yuxh alla qarsew te xrh kai fainw emos swma qaptw kai qaptw
english_head: but shall go away, so that Crito may bear it more easily, and may not be troubled when he sees my body being burnt or buried, or think I am undergoing terrible treatment, and may not say at the funeral that he is laying out Socrates, or following him to the grave, or burying him. For, dear Crito, you may be sure that such wrong words are not only undesirable in themselves, but they infect the soul with evil. No, you must be of good courage, and say that you bury my body,—and bury it

greek_head: οὕτως ὅπως ἄν σοι φίλον ᾖ καὶ μάλιστα ἡγῇ νόμιμον εἶναι Ταῦτ' εἰπὼν ἐκεῖνος μὲν ἀνίστατο εἰς οἴκημά τι ὡς λουσόμενος
greek_tokens: outws opws an su filos eimi kai malista hgeomai nomimos eimi outos eipon ekeinos men anisthmi eis oikhma tis ws louw kai o kritwn epomai autos egw de keleuw perimenw perimenw oun pros egw autos dialegw peri o erw kai anaskopew tote de au peri o sumfora dieceimi osos egw gignomai eaw atexnos hgeomai wsper pathr sterew diagw orfanoomai o epeita bios epei
english_head: as you think best and as seems to you most fitting. When he had said this, he got up and went into another room to bathe; Crito followed him, but he told us to wait. So we waited, talking over with each other and discussing the discourse we had heard, and then speaking of the great misfortune that had befallen us, for we felt that he was like a father to us and that when bereft of him we should pass the rest of our lives as orphans. And when he had bathed

greek_head: δὲ ἐλούσατο καὶ ἠνέχθη παρ' αὐτὸν τὰ παιδία δύο γὰρ αὐτῷ ὑεῖς σμικροὶ ἦσαν εἷς δὲ μέγας καὶ αἱ οἰκεῖαι γυναῖκες
greek_tokens: de louw kai ferw para autos o paidia duo gar autos uios mikros eimi eis de megas kai o oikew gunh afikneomai ekeinos enantion o kritwnos dialegw te kai epistellw ostis boulomai o men gunh kai o paidia apeimi keleuw autos de hkw para egw kai eimi hdh eggus hlios dusmh xronos gar polus diatribw endon erxomai de katakaqezomai louw kai ou polus atta meta outos dialegw kai hkw o o endeka uphreths kai isthmi
english_head: and his children had been brought to him—for he had two little sons and one big one—and the women of the family had come, he talked with them in Crito’s presence and gave them such directions as he wished; then he told the women to go away, and he came to us. And it was now nearly sunset; for he had spent a long time within. And he came and sat down fresh from the bath. After that not much was said, and the servant

greek_head: παρ' αὐτόν Ὦ Σώκρατες ἔφη οὐ καταγνώσομαί γε σοῦ ὅπερ ἄλλων καταγιγνώσκω ὅτι μοι χαλεπαίνουσι καὶ καταρῶνται
greek_tokens: para autos eimi swkraths fhmi ou katagignwskw ge sew osper allos katagignwskw ostis egw xalepainw kai kataraomai epeidan autos paraggellw pinw o farmakon anagkazw o arxw sos de egw kai allos gignwskw en outos o xronos gennaios kai praos kai ariston anhr eimi o pwpote deuro afikneomai kai dh kai nun eu oida ostis ou egw xalepainw gignwskw gar o aitios alla ekeinos nun oun oida gar o erxomai
english_head: of the eleven came and stood beside him and said: Socrates, I shall not find fault with you, as I do with others, for being angry and cursing me, when at the behest of the authorities, I tell them to drink the poison. No, I have found you in all this time in every way the noblest and gentlest and best man who has ever come here, and now I know your anger is directed against others, not against me, for you know who are blame. Now, for you know the message I came to bring you, farewell and try to bear what you must

greek_head: ἀγγέλλων χαῖρέ τε καὶ πειρῶ ὡς ῥᾷστα φέρειν τὰ ἀναγκαῖα Καὶ ἅμα δακρύσας μεταστρεφόμενος ἀπῄει
greek_tokens: aggellw xairw te kai peiraw ws radios ferw o anagkaion kai ama dakruw metastrefw apeimi kai o swkratew anablepw pros autos kai su fhmi xairw kai egw outos poiew kai ama pros egw ws asteios fhmi o anqrwpos kai para pas egw o xronos proseimi kai dialegw eniote kai eimi androw lwstos kai nun ws gennaios egw apodakruw alla age dh eimi kritwn peiqw autos kai ferw tis o farmakon ei tribw ei de mh tribw o anqrwpos
english_head: as easily as you can. And he burst into tears and turned and went away. And Socrates looked up at him and said: Fare you well, too; I will do as you say. And then he said to us: How charming the man is! Ever since I have been here he has been coming to see me and talking with me from time to time, and has been the best of men, and now how nobly he weeps for me! But come, Crito, let us obey him, and let someone bring the poison, if it is ready; and if not, let the man prepare it. And Crito said:

greek_head: Καὶ ὁ Κρίτων Ἀλλ' οἶμαι ἔφη ἔγωγε ὦ Σώκρατες ἔτι ἥλιον εἶναι ἐπὶ τοῖς ὄρεσιν καὶ οὔπω δεδυκέναι καὶ ἅμα
greek_tokens: kai o kritwn alla oimh fhmi egw eimi swkraths eti hlios eimi epi o oros kai oupw duw kai ama egw oida kai allos panu oye pinw epeidan paraggellw autos deipnew te kai pinw eu mala kai suggignomai ge enioi os an tugxanw epiqumew alla mhdeis epeigw eti gar egxwrew kai o swkratew eoikotws ge fhmi eimi kritwn ekeinos te outos poiew os su legw oiomai gar kerdainw outos poiew kai egw outos eoikotws ou poiew oudeis gar
english_head: But I think, Socrates, the sun is still upon the mountains and has not yet set; and I know that others have taken the poison very late, after the order has come to them, and in the meantime have eaten and drunk and some of them enjoyed the society of those whom they loved. Do not hurry; for there is still time. And Socrates said: Crito, those whom you mention are right in doing as they do, for they think they gain by it; and I shall be right in not doing as they do;

greek_head: οἶμαι κερδανεῖν ὀλίγον ὕστερον πιὼν ἄλλο γε ἢ γέλωτα ὀφλήσειν παρ' ἐμαυτῷ γλιχόμενος τοῦ ζῆν καὶ φειδόμενος οὐδενὸς
greek_tokens: oimh kerdainw oligos usteron pinw allos ge h gelws oflew para emautou glixomai o zaw kai feidomai oudeis eti eneimi alla eimi fhmi peiqw kai mh allos poiew kai o kritwn aekwn new o pais pimplhmi isthmi kai o pas ecerxomai kai suxnos xronos diatribw hkw agw o mellw didwmi o farmakon en kulic ferw tribw eidon de o swkratew o anqrwpos eien fhmi eimi beltistos su gar outos episthmos tis xrh poiew oudeis allos fhmi h pinw perieimi ews an su baros
english_head: for I think I should gain nothing by taking the poison a little later. I should only make myself ridiculous in my own eyes if I clung to life and spared it, when there is no more profit in it. Come, he said, do as I ask and do not refuse. Thereupon Crito nodded to the boy who was standing near. The boy went out and stayed a long time, then came back with the man who was to administer the poison, which he brought with him in a cup ready for use. And when Socrates saw him, he said: Well, my good man, you know about these things; what must I do? Nothing, he replied, except drink the poison and walk about

greek_head: ἐν τοῖς σκέλεσι γένηται ἔπειτα κατακεῖσθαι καὶ οὕτως αὐτὸ ποιήσει Καὶ ἅμα ὤρεξε τὴν κύλικα τῷ Σωκράτει
greek_tokens: en o skelos gignomai epeita katakeimai kai outws autos poihsis kai ama oregw o kulic o swkratew kai os lambanw kai mala ilaos eimi exekraths oudeis trew oude diafqeirw oute o xrwma oute o proswpon alla wsper eqw taurhdon upoblepw pros o anqrwpos tis legw fhmi peri ode o pwma pros o apospendw tis ecesti h ou tosoutos fhmi eimi swkraths tribw osos oiomai metrios eimi pinw
english_head: till your legs feel heavy; then lie down, and the poison will take effect of itself. At the same time he held out the cup to Socrates. He took it, and very gently, Echecrates, without trembling or changing color or expression, but looking up at the man with wide open eyes, as was his custom, said: What do you say about pouring a libation to some deity from this cup? May I, or not? Socrates, said he, we prepare only as much as we think is enough. I understand, said Socrates;

greek_head: Μανθάνω ἦ δ' ὅς ἀλλ' εὔχεσθαί γέ που τοῖς θεοῖς ἔξεστί τε καὶ χρή τὴν μετοίκησιν τὴν ἐνθένδε ἐκεῖσε εὐτυχῆ γενέσθαι
greek_tokens: manqanw eimi de os alla euxomai ge pou o qeaw ecesti te kai xrh o metoikhsis o enqende ekeise eutuxhs gignomai o dh kai egw euxomai te kai gignomai outos kai ama eipon outos episxw kai mala euxerhs kai eukolos ecefihmi kai egw e polus teos men epieikhs oios te eimi katexw o mh dakruw ws de eidon pinw te kai pinw ouketi alla egw ge bia kai autos astakti xwrew o dakruon wste egkaluptw apoklaw emautou ou gar dh ekeinos ge alla o emautou tuxh oios anhr
english_head: but I may and must pray to the gods that my departure hence be a fortunate one; so I offer this prayer, and may it be granted. With these words he raised the cup to his lips and very cheerfully and quietly drained it. Up to that time most of us had been able to restrain our tears fairly well, but when we watched him drinking and saw that he had drunk the poison, we could do so no longer, but in spite of myself my tears rolled down in floods, so that I wrapped my face in my cloak and wept for myself; for it was not for him that I wept,

greek_head: ἑταίρου ἐστερημένος εἴην ὁ δὲ Κρίτων ἔτι πρότερος ἐμοῦ ἐπειδὴ οὐχ οἷός τ' ἦν κατέχειν τὰ δάκρυα ἐξανέστη
greek_tokens: etairos sterew eimi o de kritwn eti proteros egw epei ou oios su eimi katexw o dakruon ecanisthmi apollodwros de kai en o emprosqen xronos oudeis epauw dakruon kai dh kai tote anabruxaomai klaiw kai aganaktew oudeis ostis ou kataklaw o pareimi plhn ge autos swkraths ekeinos de oios fhmi poiew eimi qaumasios egw mentoi ou hkistos outos eneka o gunh apopempw ina mh
english_head: but for my own misfortune in being deprived of such a friend. Crito had got up and gone away even before I did, because he could not restrain his tears. But Apollodorus, who had been weeping all the time before, then wailed aloud in his grief and made us all break down, except Socrates himself. But he said, What conduct is this, you strange men! I sent the women away chiefly for this very reason, that they might not behave in this absurd way; for I have heard that

greek_head: τοιαῦτα πλημμελοῖεν καὶ γὰρ ἀκήκοα ὅτι ἐν εὐφημίᾳ χρὴ τελευτᾶν ἀλλ' ἡσυχίαν τε ἄγετε καὶ καρτερεῖτε
greek_tokens: toioutos plhmmelew kai gar akouw ostis en eufhmia xrh teleutaw alla hsuxia te age kai karterew kai egw akouw aisxunw te kai epwxato o dakruw o de perierxomai epei e barunw fhmi o skelos kataklinw uptios outws gar keleuw o anqrwpos kai ama efaptw autos outos o didwmi o farmakon dialeipw xronos episkopew o pous kai o skelos epeita sfodra piezw autos o pous airw ei aisqanomai
english_head: it is best to die in silence. Keep quiet and be brave. Then we were ashamed and controlled our tears. He walked about and, when he said his legs were heavy, lay down on his back, for such was the advice of the attendant. The man who had administered the poison laid his hands on him and after a while examined his feet and legs, then pinched his foot hard and asked if he felt it. He said No; then after that,

greek_head: ὁ δ' οὐκ ἔφη καὶ μετὰ τοῦτο αὖθις τὰς κνήμας καὶ ἐπανιὼν οὕτως ἡμῖν ἐπεδείκνυτο ὅτι ψύχοιτό τε καὶ πήγνυτο καὶ
greek_tokens: o de ou fhmi kai meta outos auqis o knhmh kai epaneimi outws egw epideiknumi ostis yuxw te kai phgnumi kai autos aptw kai eipon ostis epeidan pros o kardia gignomai autos tote oixomai hdh oun sxedon tis autos eimi o peri o htron yuxw kai ekkaluptw egkaluptw gar eipon o dh teleutaios fqeggomai eimi kritwn fhmi o asklhpios ofellw alektruwn alla apodotos kai mh amelew alla outos fhmi eimi o kritwn alla oraw ei tis allos legw outos eromai autos oudeis eti apokrinw alla oligos xronos dialeipw kinew te kai o anqrwpos ekkaluptw autos kai os o omma isthmi eidon de o kritwn sullambanw o stoma kai o ofqalmos ode o teleuth eimi exekraths o etairos egw gignomai anhr ws egw fhmi an o tote os peiraw ariston kai allos fronimos kai dikaios
english_head: his thighs; and passing upwards in this way he showed us that he was growing cold and rigid. And again he touched him and said that when it reached his heart, he would be gone. The chill had now reached the region about the groin, and uncovering his face, which had been covered, he said—and these were his last words—Crito, we owe a cock to Aesculapius. Pay it and do not neglect it. That, said Crito, shall be done; but see if you have anything else to say. To this question he made no reply, but after a little while he moved; the attendant uncovered him; his eyes were fixed. And Crito when he saw it, closed his mouth and eyes. Such was the end, Echecrates, of our friend, who was, as we may say, of all those of his time whom we have known, the best and wisest and most righteous man.
